1.1: 1 Chronicles begins with nine chapters of genealogies (chs. 1-9) — from Adam to the post-exilic community. The genealogical prologue serves a theological purpose: to demonstrate the continuity of YHWH's covenant people through the Babylonian exile. The lists trace: Adam to Israel (ch. 1), the twelve tribes (chs. 2-9), with special focus on the line of David (ch. 3, which includes the post-exilic Davidic line down to the 6th generation after Zerubbabel — into the 5th century BCE). Matthew's genealogy (Matt 1:1-17) is a direct descendant of the Chronicler's method: beginning with Abraham, structured in three sets of fourteen, it traces the covenant line to the Messiah through the same Davidic focus that Chronicles establishes.
1.2: The three names — Kenan, Mahalalel, Jared (qênān mah˘l˘lʾêl yereḏ) — continue the asyndetic list: names only, no ‘begat’ verb. In Gen 5:9-20 each patriarch has a full entry with lifespan and the wayyôled formula. The Chronicler strips all that away — maximum genealogical compression. What Genesis takes three verses per name, Chronicles covers in one verse. The purpose is lineage, not biography: the claim that this thread of human beings leads unbroken to Israel and David.
1.3: Enoch, Methuselah, Lamech (ĥnôḵ mêtûšelaḥ lāmeḵ) — the three pre-Flood names before Noah. Enoch is theologically significant in Gen 5:24 (‘God took him’) and Heb 11:5 (translated by faith without seeing death), but the Chronicler omits this. The genealogy is purely structural: names as markers of the chain toward David, not occasions for biographical comment.
1.4: nôaḥ stands alone; the sentence then shifts: ‘The sons of Noah: Shem, Ham, and Japheth’ (bênê-nôaḥ šêm ĥām wāyepet). The syntactic break from name-only list to the explicit sons-of formula marks the major division point. At Noah the genealogy branches; what follows in vv5-23 is the Table of Nations (Gen 10 compressed). The break also signals theological weight: the flood divides primordial history from the nations, and the Shem line will carry the covenant forward.
1.5: The seven sons of Japheth (bênê yepet): Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, Tiras. Identical to Gen 10:2. The Japheth branch is listed first (eldest son), though the covenant passes through Shem. Ezek 38–39 uses Gog, Magog, Tubal, and Meshech as the great northern adversary — the Chronicler’s naming them grounds them within YHWH’s created order, not outside it.
1.6: Sons of Gomer (bênê gômer): Ashkenaz, Riphath, Togarmah. In Jer 51:27 Ashkenaz is summoned to fight against Babylon. The names of Gen 10 are not merely ancient ethnic labels but geo-political realities in the Chronicler’s world — he writes knowing that these table-of-nations names map onto known peoples and territories.
1.7: Sons of Javan (bênê yāwān): Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, Dodanim. Javan = Ionia (Greece/Aegean). Kittim = Cyprus or the western Mediterranean more broadly; in Dan 11:30 ‘ships of Kittim’ refers to Rome. Tarshish is the far western sea-route (Jonah 1:3; Ps 48:7). The Japheth branch charts the northern and western peoples.
1.8: Sons of Ham (bênê ĥām): Cush, Egypt (miṣrayim), Put, Canaan. miṣrayim is the standard Hebrew dual-form name for Egypt, referring to Upper and Lower Egypt. The Ham branch is theologically loaded for Israel: Canaan is the land Israel occupied; Egypt is the land of slavery; Cush is the region to Egypt’s south. These names frame the nations Israel will encounter as part of the single human family descended from Noah.
1.9: Sons of Cush (bênê kûš): Seba, Havilah, Sabta, Raamah, Sabteca; sons of Raamah: Sheba and Dedan. The Cush branch extends into southern Arabia and East Africa. Sheba appears in 2 Chr 9 (the queen of Sheba visits Solomon) — one of the distant nations that comes to honor the Davidic king. The genealogical root of Sheba in Cush’s line traces her people back to Noah.
1.10: wayyôleḏ kûš ʾet-nimrôḏ hûʾ hêḥêl lihyôt gibbôr bāʾāreṣ — ‘Cush fathered Nimrod; he was the first to be a mighty man on earth.’ The verbal form hêḥêl (began, Hiphil of ĥālal) + infinitive construct lihyôt signals inception: Nimrod was the initiator of a type. gibbôr (mighty one, champion) is the same term used for the primordial Nephilim (gibbôrîm) in Gen 6:4 — figures of ambiguous human greatness before the flood. Chronicles drops Gen 10:9’s qualifier ‘mighty hunter before YHWH’ and the proverb ‘like Nimrod, a mighty hunter before YHWH’ — focusing purely on his status as the first gibbôr. The city-building tradition (Gen 10:10-12, founding Babylon and Nineveh) is also omitted; the Chronicler streamlines to lineage.
1.11: miṣrayim fathered the Ludites, Anamites, Lehabites, Naphtuhites. These are gentilics — collective nouns for peoples derived from eponymous ancestors. The Hebrew form ending in -îm (plural) is the marker: lûḏîm, ʿănāmîm, etc. The ancestors are individuals; the descendants are peoples. The Hebrew reader understands that these names refer to nations, not persons.
1.12: Pathrusites (patrûsîm, from Pathros = Upper Egypt), Casluhites (kaslûḥîm) — ‘from whom the Philistines came’ (ʾăšer yāṣĕʾû miššāmm pêeštîm), and Caphtorites. The parenthetical identification of the Philistines’ origin inserts a politically charged note: the Philistines, Israel’s persistent enemy in the Davidic narrative (Chronicles focuses on David’s Philistine victories), are placed within the same genealogical structure as Israel. Amos 9:7: ‘Did I not bring the Philistines from Caphtor?’ — all ultimately under YHWH’s governance.
1.13: Canaan fathered Sidon (ṣîḏôn bêḵôrô, ‘his firstborn’) and Heth. bêḵôr (firstborn) is the only birth-order designation in the Ham genealogy — Sidon’s primacy in the Canaanite branch is noted. Heth is the ancestor of the Hittites; Abraham buys the cave of Machpelah from Ephron the Hittite (Gen 23), grounding the patriarchal land-purchase within this genealogical lineage.
1.14: The Jebusites (yêḇûsî), Amorites (ʾĕmôrî), Girgashites (girgāšî) — three of the seven Canaanite nations of Deut 7:1. The Jebusites are specifically the inhabitants of Jerusalem before David conquered it (2 Sam 5:6-9; 1 Chr 11:4-8) — the Chronicler who narrates David’s capture of Jerusalem places the Jebusite population within Noah’s grandson Canaan’s genealogy.
1.15: Hivites (ĥiwwî), Arkites (ʿarqî), Sinites (sînî). The Hivites are the people of Gibeon who deceived Joshua into a covenant (Josh 9); the covenant Joshua was tricked into became the template for Israelite treatment of Canaanites within the land. The genealogy places even Israel’s treaty-partner peoples within the Noahic framework.
1.16: Arvadites (ʾarwāḏî), Zemarites (ṣĕmārî), Hamathites (ĥămātî). Hamath is the northernmost city of the Canaanite region — ‘the entrance of Hamath’ is the standard northern boundary marker for the ideal extent of Israel’s territory (Num 34:8; 1 Kgs 8:65). The Chronicler closes the Canaan list at Hamath, bracketing the Canaanite genealogy within the promised land’s geographical limits.
1.17: Sons of Shem (bênê šêm): Elam, Asshur, Arphaxad, Lud, Aram — then Uz, Hul, Gether, and Meshech. The list in Gen 10:22 gives only five sons of Shem; the additional names (Uz, Hul, Gether, Meshech) appear in Gen 10:23 as sons of Aram, not Shem. Chronicles conflates them, placing Aram’s sons at the same level as Shem’s sons — possibly a textual transmission error or deliberate compression. The LXX of Chronicles reads similarly. This is a significant textual variant from the Genesis Vorlage: the Chronicler’s Shem-list is not a simple copy of Gen 10.
1.18: Arphaxad fathered Shelah, and Shelah fathered Eber (wayyôleḏ ʾarpaḵšaḏ ʾet-šelaḥ wĕšelaḥ yālaḏ ʾet-ʿēḇer). The introduction of the wayyôleḏ verb (narrative past: ‘and he fathered’) after the name-only lists of vv5-17 signals that the Arphaxad-Shelah-Eber line is the one that matters. The genealogy narrows structurally as it approaches the covenant ancestor: verbal narration replaces paratactic names.
1.19: Eber had two sons: the first was named Peleg (peleḡ), ‘because in his time the earth was divided’ (kî bêyāmāyw niplêḡāh hāʾāreṣ). peleḡ derives from the root pālaḡ (to divide, split). The paronomasia — naming a child after the event of his era — is standard Hebrew etymological convention. The phrase niplêḡāh hāʾāreṣ (the earth was divided, Niphal perfect) is usually interpreted as the dispersal of nations after Babel (Gen 11:1-9). The Chronicler preserves this note verbatim from Gen 10:25 without elaboration.
1.20: Joktan (yoqṭān) fathered Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth, Jerah — beginning the South Arabian branch. Several names correspond to known Arabian toponyms: Hazarmaveth = Hadramaut in Yemen. The Joktan branch (vv20-23) is a closed catalogue; the genealogy does not extend it beyond one generation. The covenant path through Peleg is where the narrative interest lies.
1.21: Hadoram, Uzal, Diklah — three more Joktan sons. Uzal is usually identified with Sana’a in Yemen (cf. Ezek 27:19). The place-names embedded in the genealogy provide the Chronicler’s audience with a mental map of the known world from the vantage of the ancient Near East.
1.22: Ebal, Abimael, Sheba — more Joktan sons. Sheba appears here as a Joktan descendant (South Arabian) and also in v9 as a Cushite (through Raamah). The double appearance of Sheba in both the Ham and Shem branches reflects either competing traditions the genealogy preserves without harmonizing, or the awareness that the ‘Sheba’ toponym covered multiple geographic/ethnic entities.
1.23: Ophir, Havilah, Jobab — final Joktan sons. The closing formula kol-ʾēlleh bĕnê yoqṭān (all these were the sons of Joktan) marks the end of a genealogical sub-unit. Ophir is the gold-source to which Solomon sent trading expeditions (1 Kgs 9:28; 1 Chr 29:4 ‘gold of Ophir’). Its placement in the Table of Nations grounds the Solomonic trade network in the created human geography — the distant lands that supply the temple treasures are part of the same Noahic family.
1.24: Shem, Arphaxad, Shelah (šêm ʾarpaḵšaḏ šālaḥ) — the asyndetic list resumes after the narrative notes of vv18-23, now tracing the line directly from Shem to Abraham. The use of the same paratactic style as vv1-3 signals a vertical genealogy (linear descent) rather than a branching one (horizontal). The Shem-to-Abraham spine is the structural backbone of all OT covenantal genealogy.
1.25: Eber, Peleg, Reu (ʿēḇer peleḡ rĕʿû) — continuing the direct descent line. Eber is the ancestor of the ʿiḇrîm (Hebrews); the connection between Eber and ‘Hebrew’ was recognized in ancient tradition (Gen 10:21 ‘Shem, the father of all the children of Eber’).
1.26: Serug, Nahor, Terah (śĕrûḡ nāĥôr teraḥ) — three generations before Abraham. Terah led the family from Ur of the Chaldeans toward Canaan but stopped at Haran (Gen 11:31). The brevity of the list (one name per verse) accelerates the reader toward the climax: Abraham.
1.27: ʾaḇrām hûʾ ʾaḇrāhām — ‘Abram, that is, Abraham.’ The identifying particle hûʾ functions as an equative marker: this person Abram is the one who became Abraham (Gen 17:5, where YHWH changes the name because ‘I have made you the father of a multitude of nations’ — ʾaḇ h˘môn gôyîm, a paronomasia on ʾaḇrāhām). The Chronicler signals the name-change without retelling the covenant of circumcision (Gen 17). The hûʾ identification also appears in v34 for Israel/Jacob — the Chronicler’s shorthand for covenant identity transition.
1.28: Sons of Abraham: Isaac and Ishmael (bênê ʾaḇrāhām yiṣḥāq wĕyišmāʿêl). The order (Isaac first, Ishmael second) reverses birth order — Ishmael is the elder (Gen 16) — to signal covenantal priority. In Hebrew genealogy, first position in a list connotes primary standing. Paul makes this argument explicitly in Gal 4:22-31 (the two sons of Abraham representing the two covenants).
1.29: Ishmael’s sons ‘in birth order’ (bêḵûrôtām): the firstborn Nebaioth, then Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam. bêḵûrôt is an abstract noun from bêḵôr (firstborn) meaning ‘sequence of birth.’ The specification that Ishmael’s sons are listed in birth order emphasizes that the list is complete and properly ordered. Kedar appears in prophetic texts (Isa 21:16-17; 42:11; 60:7) as a significant North Arabian tribal group.
1.30: Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadad, Tema — five more sons of Ishmael. Massa and Tema appear in Prov 30:1 and 31:1 as place-names associated with wisdom literature. The genealogy grounds the non-Israelite wisdom tradition (Proverbs draws on an international wisdom heritage) within the Abrahamic family.
1.31: Jetur, Naphish, Kedemah — final sons of Ishmael. ‘These were the sons of Ishmael’ (ʾēlleh hêm bênê yišmāʿêl) closes the Ishmael catalogue. Jetur and Naphish appear in 1 Chr 5:19-22 as peoples against whom the Transjordanian tribes of Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh fought successfully — the genealogical names in ch. 1 remain historically active in the Chronicler’s own narrative.
1.32: Sons of Keturah, Abraham’s concubine (pîlegeš): Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, Shuah. pîlegeš (concubine) is the precise term used in Gen 25:6 for Keturah’s status — secondary wife, not the primary covenant wife Sarah. Midian is the most significant: the Midianites are Israel’s enemy (Num 25; Judg 6-8), yet Moses’s father-in-law Jethro is a Midianite priest (Exod 2:16-18) — Abraham’s descendant and Israel’s adversary simultaneously.
1.33: Sons of Midian: Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, Eldaah. ‘All these were the descendants of Keturah’ (kol-ʾēlleh bênê qĕṭûrāh). The closing formula ends the three-branch Abraham genealogy (Ishmael / Keturah / Isaac). Ephah of Midian appears in Isa 60:6 among the nations that will bring gold and frankincense to Jerusalem — the genealogical root of Ephah in Abraham positions this future ingathering of nations as a homecoming of distant family.
1.34: wayyôleḏ ʾaḇrāhām ʾet-yiṣḥāq — ‘Abraham fathered Isaac.’ After the extensive Ishmael and Keturah branches, the covenantal line snaps back to a single statement. ‘Sons of Isaac: Esau and Israel’ (ʿêšāw wĕyiśrāʾêl) — the Chronicler uses the covenant name ‘Israel’ rather than ‘Jacob’ throughout, consistent with his practice of preferring the name given in Gen 32:28 / Gen 35:10. Esau is listed first by birth order; Israel second by covenantal priority. The same inversion as Isaac before Ishmael (v28): covenantal priority supersedes birth order throughout this genealogy.
1.35: Sons of Esau (bênê ʿêšāw): Eliphaz, Reuel, Jeush, Jalam, Korah. Corresponding exactly to Gen 36:4-5. Eliphaz and Reuel are sons of Esau’s Canaanite and Ishmaelite wives respectively. The Esau branch receives more attention (vv35-42) than the Keturah branch, because Edom (Esau’s nation) is the Chronicler’s politically relevant neighbor in the post-exilic period.
1.36: Sons of Eliphaz (bênê ʾĕlîpaz): Teman, Omar, Zephi, Gatam, Kenaz, Timna, and Amalek. The list differs from Gen 36:12, which lists Timna as a concubine of Eliphaz who bore Amalek — making Timna a mother rather than a sibling. Chronicles places both as Eliphaz’s progeny at the same level. Amalek is the significant name: the Amalekites are Israel’s primordial enemy (Exod 17:8-16; Deut 25:17-19; 1 Sam 15), and their eponymous ancestor is here placed as Eliphaz’s son — Abraham’s great-great-grandson.
1.37: Sons of Reuel (bênê rĕʿûʾêl): Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, Mizzah — identical to Gen 36:13 (with the same names). Reuel is also the name of Moses’s Midianite father-in-law in Exod 2:18; the shared name is a coincidence of common ancient Near Eastern naming practice, not a genealogical connection.
1.38: Sons of Seir (bênê śêʿîr): Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, Dishan. These are not Esau’s sons but the original Horite inhabitants of the Seir region whom Esau displaced and intermarried with (Gen 36:20-21; Deut 2:12 ‘the Horites formerly lived in Seir’). The Chronicler includes them because the nation Israel knows as Edom is an ethnic blend of Esau’s sons and the indigenous Horite population.
1.39: Sons of Lotan (bênê lôṭān): Hori and Homam. Lotan’s sister was Timna (wĕʾăĥôt lôṭān timnāʿ). This Timna is the same woman who becomes Eliphaz’s concubine and the mother of Amalek (Gen 36:22). The Chronicler notes the relationship without comment: the intermarriage of Esau’s line with the Seirite Horites produces the Edomite nation.
1.40: Sons of Shobal (bênê šôḇāl): Alian, Manahath, Ebal, Shephi, Onam. Sons of Zibeon: Aiah and Anah. The Horite clans continue. Manahath appears also in 1 Chr 8:6 as a place-name in Benjamin — the genealogy’s personal names and later territorial names overlap in ways that trace Israel’s own land-distribution back to pre-Israelite inhabitants.
1.41: Anah’s son: Dishon (bênê ʿānāh dîšôn). Sons of Dishon: Hamran, Eshban, Ithran, Cheran. The list corresponds to Gen 36:26 with minor spelling differences (Hemdan/Hamran). The depth of this Horite genealogy — four generations from Seir — reflects that the Chronicler is tracking the Edomite social structure with some precision.
1.42: Sons of Ezer (bênê ʾêṣer): Bilhan, Zaavan, Jaakan. Sons of Dishan (bênê dîšān): Uz and Aran. The Ezer/Dishan branch closes the Horite catalogue. Jaakan appears in Num 33:31-32 as a wilderness camping station (Bene-jaakan) — connecting the genealogical eponyms to the physical geography of Israel’s wilderness journey.
1.43: wĕʾêlleh hammêlāḵîm ʾăšer māleḵû bêʾereṣ ʾeḏôm lipnê meloḵ-melek lêḇenê yiśrāʾêl — ‘These are the kings who reigned in Edom before any king reigned over Israel.’ The temporal clause lipnê meloḵ-melek (before a king reigned) uses the infinitive construct of mālaḵ (to reign as king) twice — the idiom marks the Edomite list as pre-Davidic. For the Chronicler, whose entire narrative centers on the Davidic monarchy, this clause is prospective: it marks the Edomite king-list as temporally prior and implicitly asks “when will Israel have its own king?” The list of Edom’s kings without a permanent dynasty sets up the contrast with the Davidic covenant’s promise of an eternal dynasty.
1.44: When Bela died, Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah reigned (wayyimloḵ taḥtāyw, ‘reigned in his place’). The succession formula taḥtāyw (lit. ‘under/in his place’) is the standard idiom for replacement-succession. What is absent is any father-son relation: Jobab is ‘son of Zerah,’ not ‘son of Bela.’ The Edomite kingship is elective or achieved, not hereditary — a stark contrast with the Davidic promise in 2 Sam 7:12 (‘I will raise up your offspring after you’) and 1 Chr 17:11-14.
1.45: When Jobab died, Husham of the land of the Temanites reigned (wayyimloḵ taḥtāyw ĥûšām mêʾereṣ hatêmānî). Geographic origins replace patronymics throughout this list: kings are identified by where they come from (Bozrah, Temanite land, Moabite territory, Masrekah, Rehoboth-by-the-River), not whose sons they are. Power rotates among regional power bases — the non-dynastic nature of Edomite kingship is built into the formula.
1.46: Hadad son of Bedad (h˘ḏaḏ ben-bêḏaḏ) reigned after Husham — ‘he had defeated Midian on the territory of Moab’ (hammaккeh ʾet-miḏyān biśĕḏêh môʾāḇ). This is the only king in the list identified by a military achievement. The mention of a Midian-defeat connects the Edomite king-list to the broader geopolitical struggles of the region — the same Midianites who descended from Keturah (v33) and who are Israel’s adversary in the wilderness period.
1.47: Samlah of Masrekah (śamllāh mimmāśrêqāh) reigned after Hadad. Masrekah is unidentified but the name suggests a wadi-region (the root śrq relates to vines or a winding course). The place-names in this list are drawn from an ancient source that the Chronicler and Gen 36 preserve verbatim; they provide a window into the political geography of Transjordan before the Israelite settlement.
1.48: Shaul of Rehoboth-by-the-River (šāʾûl mêreĥôḇôt hannāhār) reigned after Samlah. hannāhār (the River) in OT geographical language refers to the Euphrates (cf. Gen 31:21; Exod 23:31). ‘Rehoboth-by-the-Euphrates’ places this king’s origin in northern Syria — evidence that the Edomite royal selection drew from a wide geographic pool, perhaps through trade networks.
1.49: Baal-hanan son of Achbor (baʿal-ḥānān ben-ʿaḵbôr) reigned after Shaul. The theophoric name Baal-hanan (‘the Lord [Baal] is gracious’) is the only Baal-compound name in the Edomite king-list. In 1 Chr 27:28, a Baal-hanan is David’s overseer of the olive and sycamore groves in the Shephelah — possibly an Edomite or Canaanite official appointed by David, with the name surviving in administrative use.
1.50: When Baal-hanan died, Hadad reigned (wayyimloḵ taḥtāyw h˘ḏaḏ). His city was Pai (pāʿî; written Pau in Gen 36:39 — a minor spelling variant). His wife was Mehetabel daughter of Matred, daughter of Me-zahab (mêhêṭaḇʾêl bat-maṭrêḏ bat mê-zāhāḇ). The wife’s name and two-generation maternal genealogy is unusual for this list; it is the most detailed entry. mê-zāhāḇ literally means ‘waters of gold’ — possibly a gold-producing region.
1.51: Hadad also died (wayyāmāt h˘ḏaḏ) — the abrupt death notice without naming a successor signals the end of the king-list and the transition to the chief-list. The chiefs of Edom (ʾallûpê ʾeḏôm): Timna, Aliah, Jetheth. ʾallûp (chief, lit. ‘one of a thousand’) differs from melek (king) — the chiefs are clan-leaders rather than monarchs. The shift from king-list to chief-list may reflect different periods of Edomite social organization.
1.52: Chief Oholibamah (ʾallûp ʾohôlîḇāmāh), Elah, Pinon. Oholibamah is a female name (the same as Esau’s wife in Gen 36:2) used here as a chief’s name — either the same person or a place-name that became a clan designation. The use of female-associated names for clan chiefs reflects that tribal genealogical identity was often traced through prominent women.
1.53: Chief Kenaz (ʾallûp qênaz), Teman, Mibzar. Kenaz is also the name of Esau’s grandson (v36: Eliphaz’s son) and the ancestor of Othniel the Kenizzite (Josh 15:17; Judg 3:9 ‘Othniel the son of Kenaz, Caleb’s younger brother’). The Kenizzites are among the pre-Israelite peoples whose land is promised to Abraham’s descendants (Gen 15:19). The overlap between the Edomite chief Kenaz and Israelite judge Othniel’s lineage reflects the complex ethnic interweaving of Israelite and Edomite ancestry in the Negev region.
1.54: Chief Magdiel (ʾallûp magdîʾêl) and chief Iram. ‘These were the chiefs of Edom’ (ʾêlleh ʾallûpê ʾeḏôm) — the closing formula ends the Edomite section and closes chapter 1 entirely. The Chronicler has moved from Adam (v1) to the post-Abrahamic nations, demonstrating that Israel and Judah stand within, not apart from, the full human genealogical matrix. The chapter is simultaneously universal (beginning at Adam) and particular (closing on Edom, Israel’s near-neighbor and adversary) before the Chronicler turns in chapter 2 to Israel’s own tribes.

2.7: The Chronicler names Achan as ʿāḵār — not the form used in Joshua (where the name is ʿāḵān, Josh 7:1-26) — and immediately glosses it: ʿōḵēr yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer māʿal maʿal bᵉḥērem, ‘the troubler of Israel who broke faith in the matter of the devoted thing.’ The name ʿāḵār is a deliberate morphological pun on the verb ʿāḵar (to trouble, disturb, bring calamity) — the same root that appears in Josh 7:25, where Joshua says meh ʿăḵartānû YHWH yaʿkorᵉḵā hayyôm hazzeh, ‘Why did you bring trouble on us? YHWH brings trouble on you today.’ The valley of execution is subsequently named ʿēmeq ʿāḵôr, ‘Valley of Achor/Trouble’ (Josh 7:26; Hos 2:15; Isa 65:10). The Chronicler’s renaming of Achan as Achar encodes the interpretation into the name itself: the man whose act brought ʿāḵar on Israel is remembered by the name of his deed. This is the genealogy’s only explicit negative notation in the Judahite line — a single name that carries the full weight of the ḥērem-violation and its consequences. The principle that one person’s covenant-breach affects the entire community (Josh 7:1: ‘the people of Israel broke faith’) is what the Achar/ʿāḵar wordplay encodes.
2.1: The twelve sons of Israel (bᵉnê yiśrāʾēl) are listed: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun (v1), Dan, Joseph, Benjamin, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher (v2). The order differs from Gen 35:23-26 (which groups by mother: Leah’s six, Rachel’s two, Bilhah’s two, Zilpah’s two) and from Num 1 (which follows census and military order). Chronicles places Reuben first as legal firstborn, then proceeds in birth order for Leah’s sons. The variation across lists reflects different functions: this list heads the genealogical program without the narrative context of the patriarchal narratives.
2.2: The second verse completes the twelve: Dan, Joseph (whose sons Ephraim and Manasseh function as tribes), Benjamin, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher. The inclusion of Joseph rather than Ephraim and Manasseh at this stage reflects the patriarchal level of the genealogy; the tribal subdivisions into Ephraim and Manasseh appear later in the tribal registers. The Chronicler’s decision to present the full Israel → Judah → David → Solomon genealogical chain before other tribal registers signals the theological priority of the Davidic covenant as the organizing principle of the entire work.
2.3: Judah’s sons are listed first (ahead of Reuben, who was the legal firstborn — the reason given in 5:1-2). The three sons by bat šûaʿ haKᵉnaʿănît (Bath-shua the Canaanite) — Er, Onan, and Shelah — correspond to Gen 38:2-5. The brief note wayyihî ʿēr bᵉḵôr yᵉhûḏāh rāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH wayyᵉmîtēhû — Er, Judah’s firstborn, was evil in YHWH’s sight and YHWH put him to death — is a condensed allusion to the Genesis narrative without retelling it. The Chronicler assumes the reader knows the story and moves rapidly to the productive genealogical lines.
2.4: Tamar, Judah’s daughter-in-law, bore him Perez and Zerah — a total of five sons for Judah. The notice is genealogically important because Perez is the ancestor of David (v5, 9-15); Zerah is the ancestor of the wise men of Solomon’s court (v6; cf. 1 Kgs 4:31). The Tamar incident (Gen 38) involved levirate obligation — Tamar disguised herself and conceived from Judah when Shelah was withheld from her. The Chronicler includes Tamar’s name (rare in genealogies) because the two lines from Judah through her are crucial for the genealogical program.
2.5: The sons of Perez: ḥeṣrôn wᵉḥāmûl — Hezron and Hamul (cf. Num 26:21). Hezron is the genealogical pivot connecting Judah to David — the bulk of ch2 is devoted to the Hezronite clan’s subdivisions (Jerahmeel, Ram, and Caleb/Chelubai). Hamul’s line is not developed here, reflecting that the Chronicler’s interest follows the genealogical branches that lead to significant historical figures rather than providing exhaustive coverage of all lines.
2.6: The sons of Zerah: Zimri, Ethan, Heman, Calcol, and Dara — five in total. This list overlaps with 1 Kgs 4:31 where Ethan the Ezrahite, and Heman, Calcol, and Darda are listed among those Solomon exceeded in wisdom — called there “sons of Mahol.” The difference between “sons of Zerah” (Chronicles) and “sons of Mahol” (Kings) may indicate that māḥôl is a professional title (guild of musicians/dancers) rather than a personal name, or that there are two distinct traditions. Ethan and Heman appear as Psalm titles (Pss 88-89), linking these genealogical figures to the Psalter tradition.
2.8: The son of Ethan: Azariah. A single-verse line that preserves continuity for Ethan’s branch without developing it. The terse notation reflects the Chronicler’s selective presentation: some lines are traced extensively (Hezron’s three sons take up most of ch2); others receive single-verse preservation. Azariah is a common Levitical name (meaning “YHWH has helped”), raising the possibility of confusion with priestly lines, but this Azariah belongs to the Zerahite/Judahite genealogy.
2.9: The sons of Hezron: Jerahmeel, Ram, and Chelubai (kᵉlûḇāy). Chelubai is the same person as Caleb, a variant spelling (kālēḇ / kᵉlûḇāy) — possibly representing two dialectal forms of the same name. The three branches of Hezron organize the following genealogical material: Ram’s line leads directly to David (v10-17); Jerahmeel’s line occupies v25-41; Caleb’s line (v18-24, 42-55) is interwoven throughout as the most expansive subdivision. The prioritization of Ram over Jerahmeel (though Jerahmeel is listed first, suggesting firstborn status) follows the principle that the genealogically significant line — David’s — is developed first.
2.10: Ram fathered Amminadab (ʿammînāḏāḇ — “my kinsman is noble/generous”), and Amminadab fathered Nahshon. Nahshon is given the honorific nᵉśîʾ bᵉnê yᵉhûḏāh — prince/chief of the sons of Judah — the same title he bears at the Sinai census (Num 1:7; 2:3) and at the tabernacle dedication (Num 7:12, 17). He is the most prominent named ancestor between the patriarchal period and the David/Jesse generation. Ruth 4:18-22 parallels this line almost exactly (Perez → Hezron → Ram → Amminadab → Nahshon → Salmon → Boaz → Obed → Jesse → David), confirming it as a received genealogical tradition.
2.11: Nahshon fathered Salma (śalmāʾ, also spelled Salmon in Ruth 4:21 as śalmôn), and Salma fathered Boaz. The spelling variation Salma/Salmon reflects transliteration variants; both names derive from the root šālam (peace/completeness). Boaz’s position here — three generations below Nahshon — is chronologically compressed relative to the four-century span from Sinai to Judges; the genealogy condenses multiple actual generations into named ancestors. Salma appears again as a place/clan name in v51, 54 (Bethlehem and surrounding areas), suggesting a double identity as personal name and clan eponym.
2.12: Boaz fathered Obed (ʿōḇēḏ — “worshipper/servant”), and Obed fathered Jesse (yišay). Obed is the son of Boaz and Ruth the Moabitess (Ruth 4:13-17), the pivot between the Ruth narrative and David’s origins. His name “worshipper” is given by the women of Bethlehem (Ruth 4:17) — a covenantal name marking him as one who will serve. Jesse (yišay) is of uncertain etymology; possible connections to ʾîš (man) + “YHWH” or to yēš (there is) have been proposed without consensus. He appears in 1 Sam 16 as the father to whom Samuel goes for the anointing of Israel’s next king.
2.13: Jesse fathered seven sons listed here: Eliab the firstborn, Abinadab the second, Shimma (šimmāʾ, also Shammah in 1 Sam 16:9) the third. The harmonization with 1 Sam 16’s list of Jesse’s sons that Samuel reviewed is intentional; Chronicles adapts the Samuel account. Eliab (“my God is father”) is the eldest whose imposing appearance impressed Samuel but whom YHWH rejected (1 Sam 16:6-7). The listing of seven older brothers before David emphasizes David’s position as the unexpected, seemingly unlikely choice of YHWH.
2.14: Nethanel (nᵉtanʾēl — “God has given”) the fourth, Raddai (raddāy, etymology uncertain — possibly “YHWH rules” or a diminutive) the fifth. These two brothers are mentioned only in this genealogical list; they play no role in the Samuel narratives. Their inclusion reflects the Chronicler’s commitment to completeness within the Davidic family, even of figures who left no narrative trace. The names with theophoric elements (Nethanel) and others of uncertain meaning (Raddai) represent the normal range of Israelite personal-name formation in this period.
2.15: Ozem (ʾōṣem, possibly “strong/vigorous”) the sixth, and David the seventh. The discrepancy with 1 Sam 16 is well-known: Samuel’s narrative presents seven sons who pass before Samuel plus David as the eighth (1 Sam 16:10-11). The usual resolution is that one of Jesse’s eight sons died without progeny before the Chronicler’s time and was therefore dropped from the genealogical record. Only sons who continued a line would be retained in living genealogical tradition. The placement of David as seventh (rather than eighth) reflects genealogical rather than narrative interest — the Chronicler preserves only the productive line.
2.16: The sisters of Jesse’s sons: Zeruiah and Abigail. Zeruiah is not David’s sister in the sense of full sibling — 2 Sam 17:25 identifies Zeruiah’s mother as Nahash (not Jesse), suggesting half-sistership through the same mother. Her three sons — ʾăḇîšay wᵉyôʾāḇ wᵉʿăśāhēʾl (Abishai, Joab, and Asahel) — are David’s nephews and constitute the three most prominent military figures of his reign. Asahel was killed by Abner (2 Sam 2:23); Joab killed both Abner and Absalom; Abishai served faithfully throughout. The Chronicler’s inclusion of the sisters and their sons here rather than later in the narrative reflects the genealogical completeness principle.
2.17: Abigail (a different Abigail from Nabal’s wife of 1 Sam 25 — both names mean “father’s joy”) bore Amasa (ʿămāśāʾ), whose father was Jether the Ishmaelite (yeter haYYišmᵉʿēlî). The parallel in 2 Sam 17:25 reads “Ithra the Israelite” (yitrāʾ haYYiśrᵉʾēlî), creating a text-critical variant. The Ishmaelite reading may be original (an Ishmaelite connection being more surprising and therefore more likely to be changed to Israelite); alternatively the Chronicles variant may have harmonized from genealogical necessity. Amasa became commander of Absalom’s army in the rebellion and was later murdered by Joab (2 Sam 20:8-10).
2.18: Caleb son of Hezron had children by his wife Azubah and by Jerioth (yᵉrîʿôt). The relationship between Azubah and Jerioth is unclear — the Hebrew is difficult and may indicate that Jerioth was a daughter or concubine rather than a second wife. Her sons are listed: Jesher, Shobab, and Ardon. These are place names as much as personal names — the Chronicler’s genealogies frequently blur person/place identity, reflecting clan eponyms that encode geographic settlement. Caleb here is the Hezronite Caleb (grandson of Judah), not the famous Caleb of Numbers 13 (son of Jephunneh), though the names’ overlap created confusion in later tradition.
2.19: After Azubah died, Caleb married Ephrath (ʾeprāṯāh), who bore him Hur (ḥûr). Ephrath gives her name to Ephrathah, a designation for the Bethlehem area (Gen 35:19; Ruth 4:11; Mic 5:2). The connection — Caleb → Ephrath(ah) → the Bethlehem district — encodes geographic-genealogical information: the Calebite clan’s settlement in the Bethlehem/Hebron area of Judah. This also establishes Hur’s Judahite credentials; Hur appears in Exodus as Moses’ supporter who held up his hands at Rephidim (Exod 17:10-12), though the identification of the two Hurs is not certain.
2.20: Hur fathered Uri (ʾûrî — “my flame/light”), and Uri fathered Bezalel (bᵉṣalʾēl — “in the shadow of God”). Bezalel is the master craftsman whom YHWH appointed to construct the tabernacle and its furnishings (Exod 31:2; 35:30-35; 36:1-2). His description in Exodus — filled with the Spirit of God (rûaḥ ʾelōhîm) in wisdom, understanding, knowledge, and craftsmanship — makes him unique in the Pentateuch as a named recipient of the Spirit for artistic-technical work. The Chronicler’s placement of this genealogical note here connects the tabernacle tradition to the Judahite-Calebite-Ephrathite genealogical tree, establishing the craftsman’s tribal identity within Judah’s family.
2.21: Hezron married the daughter of Machir (māḵîr), the father of Gilead, when he was sixty years old. Machir son of Manasseh is the major figure who controlled Gilead in the conquest tradition (Num 27:1; 32:39-40; Josh 17:1). The marriage of Hezron (a Judahite clan leader) to a Machirite daughter reflects the historical reality of inter-tribal connections and the absorption of Transjordanian territory into the genealogical orbit of major Judahite families. The age notation — sixty years old — is unique in the genealogical lists and may indicate this was a late second marriage or an alliance marriage.
2.22: She bore him Segub, and Segub fathered Jair, who had twenty-three cities (ʿārîm) in the land of Gilead. Jair is a famous figure in the Judges tradition: a minor judge who had thirty sons riding thirty donkeys and possessed thirty cities in Gilead (Judg 10:3-5 — “Havvoth-jair” = “the tent-villages of Jair”). The different numbers (23 vs. 30) may reflect different historical moments or different counting criteria. The genealogical tradition here grounds Jair’s Gileadite land-holding in a Judahite ancestor through Machir — the Chronicler is explaining how a Judahite (Hezron) came to have Transjordanian territorial holdings through inter-tribal marriage.
2.23: Geshur and Aram took Havvoth-jair (ḥawwōt yāʾîr) and Kenath (qᵉnāt) and its settlements — sixty cities — from them. The names Geshur and Aram reflect Aramean groups in the northeastern Transjordan; the loss of the sixty Gileadite cities to Aram marks a territorial contraction in Israel’s history (cf. 2 Kgs 10:32-33 for Hazael’s seizures). The note closes with “all these were sons of Machir the father of Gilead” — a summary that emphasizes the Machirite-Gileadite identity of these territories and their genealogical ownership despite the military loss.
2.24: After Hezron died in Caleb-ephrathah, Hezron’s wife Abijah bore him Ashhur (ʾaššûr), the father of Tekoa. The posthumous birth notation — Abijah bears a son after Hezron’s death — is unusual and may reflect a levirate arrangement or simply a child conceived before Hezron’s death but born after. Ashhur as “father of Tekoa” is a place eponym: Tekoa (southeast of Bethlehem) was Amos’s hometown and the city from which Joab fetched the wise woman (2 Sam 14:2). The genealogical note grounds the Tekoite clan in the Hezronite-Calebite family tree of Judah.
2.25: The sons of Jerahmeel (Hezron’s firstborn): Ram the firstborn (rāʾm habbᵉḵôr — distinguished from Ram son of Hezron, v9), then Bunah, Oren, Ozem, and Ahijah. The Jerahmeelite clan (yᵉraḥmᵉʾēlîm) appears in 1 Samuel as a people group in the Negev of Judah whom David raided (1 Sam 27:10) and with whom he cultivated relations (1 Sam 30:29). The genealogical listing of Jerahmeel’s extensive family (v25-41) provides the genealogical background for this historically attested Negev clan absorbed into the Judahite tribal structure.
2.26: Jerahmeel also had another wife named Atarah (ʿăṭārāh — “crown”); she was the mother of Onam (ʿônām). The notation of a second wife introduces a branch of the Jerahmeelite genealogy. Multiple wives in the genealogies typically mark clan-level divisions — each wife’s line represents a distinct clan subsection. Onam’s name (possibly “vigorous/wealthy”) appears in v28 as the father of two sons whose lines are developed.
2.27: The sons of Ram, the firstborn of Jerahmeel: Maaz, Jamin, and Eker. Three names with brief notation — no narrative expansion. Maaz (maʿaṣ) possibly means “wrath” or “strength”; Jamin (yāmîn, “right hand” or “south”) is the same name as Simeon’s son (Gen 46:10); Eker (ʿeqer, “stock/root”) appears only here. The Jerahmeelite Ram line receives less development than the Hezronite Ram line (v9-17), reflecting the Chronicler’s interest in the David-ancestral branch.
2.28: The sons of Onam: Shammai and Jada. The sons of Shammai: Nadab and Abishur. The genealogy descends through Onam’s son Shammai. Shammai (šammay) is a name shared with several other biblical figures; Nadab and Abishur are common Israelite names combining divine and nobility vocabulary. The genealogy at this level (four generations from Jerahmeel) represents clans that were historically identifiable to the Chronicler’s audience but whose specific narratives did not survive.
2.29: The wife of Abishur was named Abihail (ʾăḇîḥayil — “my father is might/army”); she bore him Ahban and Molid. Women’s names are rare in these genealogical lists; when they appear, it typically signals either that the woman was notable in clan tradition or that the line’s continuity depended on her. Ahban (ʾaḥbān, “brother of understanding/intelligence”) and Molid (mwlyd, “one who gives birth/begets”) are otherwise unattested names.
2.30: The sons of Nadab: Seled and Appaim. Seled died without sons (wayyāmot seleḏ bᵉlî bānîm). The notation of dying without sons preserves a genealogical dead end — important because it explains why Seled’s line does not continue. Genealogical records in the ancient Near East tracked inheritance and land tenure; lines that died out were documented precisely because their property reverted to other family members. Appaim’s son Ishi continues the line (v31).
2.31: The son of Appaim: Ishi. The son of Ishi: Sheshan. The son of Sheshan: Ahlai. Ahlai is referred to as Sheshan’s child, but v34 notes Sheshan had no sons, only daughters — making Ahlai a daughter’s name. This is one of the few places in the genealogy where a female child holds the genealogical position, reflecting the exceptional circumstance when there are no male heirs (cf. the daughters of Zelophehad, Num 27).
2.32: The sons of Jada (Shammai’s brother): Jether and Jonathan. Jether died without sons (wayyāmot yeter bᵉlî bānîm). The repetition of the “died without sons” notation (cf. v30 for Seled) shows the Chronicler preserving even non-productive genealogical lines — the record is complete rather than selective for survivorship. Jonathan’s son Peleth and his son Zaza continue this branch.
2.33: The sons of Jonathan: Peleth and Zaza. These were the sons of Jerahmeel. The summary formula ʾēlleh hāyû bᵉnê yᵉraḥmᵉʾēl closes the Jerahmeelite section. The genealogy has traced six generations from Jerahmeel with multiple branches, demonstrating the clan’s historical depth and the Chronicler’s access to detailed clan records for this Negev group.
2.34: Sheshan had no sons, only daughters (ûlᵉšēšān ʾên bānîm kî ʾim bānôt). He had an Egyptian servant (ʿeḇed) named Jarha. The absence of sons in Sheshan’s line creates the occasion for the remarkable arrangement that follows (v35): Sheshan gave his daughter to Jarha in marriage to preserve the family line. The designation “Egyptian servant” (ʾiš Miṣrî ʿeḇed) reflects the social reality of permanent household servants in the ancient Near East, some of whom attained significant social standing; compare the Egyptian Hagar’s prominent role in the patriarchal narratives.
2.35: Sheshan gave his daughter in marriage to Jarha his servant, and she bore him Attai (ʿattay). The social arrangement — master’s daughter marrying the household slave — preserved the lineage through the maternal line when the male line failed. Attai’s name (possibly “timely/appropriate”) begins a genealogical chain traced for six more generations (v36-41). The Chronicler’s preservation of this line through an Egyptian servant reflects the historically complex social composition of Judah’s clans.
2.36: Attai fathered Nathan (nātān — “he gave”), and Nathan fathered Zabad. The name Nathan here is a common Israelite name (borne by the prophet Nathan, among others) and unrelated to the prophet. The genealogy continues the Sheshan → Jarha → Attai line for several more generations, tracing a clan that descended from the Egyptian servant’s marriage into the Jerahmeelite family. Zabad appears as a name elsewhere (2 Chr 24:26) in a murder narrative.
2.37: Zabad fathered Ephlal, and Ephlal fathered Obed. Two additional generations in the extended Sheshan line. Ephlal (ʾeplāl, possibly “YHWH has judged”) is unique to this passage; Obed reappears as a name (cf. v12 in the Jesse line, and Ruth 4:17). The repetition of common names in different genealogical lines is expected — Israelite name-stock was limited and names were recycled across families and generations.
2.38: Obed fathered Jehu (yēhûʾ — “YHWH is he”), and Jehu fathered Azariah. The name Jehu here should not be confused with the famous military commander and king of Israel (2 Kgs 9-10); theophoric names were widely shared. Azariah (“YHWH has helped”) is one of the most common Hebrew names, borne by multiple kings and priests throughout the monarchic period.
2.39: Azariah fathered Helez (ḥeleṣ — possibly “loins/strength”), and Helez fathered Eleasah (ʾelʿāśāh — “God has made/done”). Helez the Pelonite appears in David’s list of mighty men (1 Chr 11:27; 2 Sam 23:26), potentially the same person as in this genealogy — which would date the Sheshan line’s sixth generation to approximately David’s time, consistent with the chronological framework.
2.40: Eleasah fathered Sismai, and Sismai fathered Shallum (šallûm — “recompense/restored”). The name Shallum is common in Chronicles and Ezra-Nehemiah for post-exilic figures; the same name in this pre-Davidic genealogy reflects the name’s broad usage across centuries. The Sheshan genealogy at this point (eight generations from Attai) reaches approximately the monarchic period.
2.41: Shallum fathered Jekamiah (yᵉqamyāh — “YHWH has established”), and Jekamiah fathered Elishama (ʾelîšāmāʿ — “my God has heard”). The extended Sheshan-Jarha line ends here after twelve named generations from Attai. The line’s conclusion without further development suggests either that this is the genealogy’s endpoint in the Chronicler’s sources or that the line continued without significant historical figures to anchor further entries. Elishama appears as a name in the Davidic genealogy (3:6, 8), creating potential confusion with different individuals bearing the same name.
2.42: The sons of Caleb brother of Jerahmeel: Mesha his firstborn, the father of Ziph, and the sons of Mareshah, the father of Hebron. This section (v42-55) traces Caleb’s line through his concubines and secondary wives, with a strong focus on eponyms of Judahite towns. Mesha (father of Ziph) connects to Ziph in southern Judah (where David hid from Saul, 1 Sam 23:14-15); Mareshah was a Judahite city in the Shephelah (Josh 15:44; Mic 1:15). The genealogy is simultaneously a record of clan ancestry and a map of territorial settlement in Judah.
2.43: The sons of Hebron: Korah, Tappuah, Rekem, and Shema. Hebron (ḥeḇrôn) here is both the city name and the name of Caleb’s descendant/eponym; the Calebite clan’s association with the Hebron area (which Caleb received as his inheritance in Josh 14:13-14) is encoded in the genealogy. Korah, Tappuah, Rekem, and Shema are otherwise obscure names; Korah shares a name with the Levite rebel (Num 16) but belongs to a different family here.
2.44: Shema fathered Raham, the father of Jorkeam; and Rekem fathered Shammai. Place-eponym genealogy continues: Jorkeam is unknown from other sources but presumably represents a settlement in Judah. The Chronicler’s sources for this extensive Calebite genealogy appear to be local clan records from the Hebron/southern Judah area — records that were not incorporated into the Samuel-Kings narrative tradition but preserved in Judean administrative archives.
2.45: The son of Shammai: Maon; and Maon was the father of Beth-zur (bêt ṣûr). Both Maon and Beth-zur are identifiable Judean locations: Maon is the town associated with Nabal (1 Sam 25:2) in the same territory; Beth-zur is a fortified city in the hill country of Judah (Josh 15:58; Neh 3:16), archaeologically identified and later important in the Maccabean period. The genealogy thus charts the Calebite clan’s territorial history through eponyms of historically attested settlements.
2.46: Ephah, Caleb’s concubine (pîlegeš), bore Haran, Moza, and Gazez. Haran fathered Gazez. The concubine notation marks a secondary genealogical line. The repetition of Gazez as both Ephah’s son and Haran’s son may reflect a textual error (dittography), a practice of naming a grandson after an uncle, or two distinct individuals. Ephah as a woman’s name connects to the place-name Ephah (an Ishmaelite/Midianite group of Isa 60:6), possibly reflecting a marriage alliance at the geographic edges of Calebite territory.
2.47: The sons of Jahdai: Regem, Jotham, Geshan, Pelet, Ephah, and Shaaph. Jahdai’s relationship to Caleb is unclear — the text omits the connecting formula, creating a syntactic gap. He may be another concubine’s son or a secondary figure whose relationship to Caleb has been lost in transmission. The six sons are otherwise unknown; the list preserves the names without narrative context, suggesting the Chronicler drew from genealogical records that had no associated narrative tradition.
2.48: Maacah, another of Caleb’s concubines, bore Sheber and Tirhanah. The second named concubine introduces another branch. Maacah (māʿăḵāh) is also a place name in the northern Transjordan and a personal name borne by several women in the royal genealogy (David’s wife, Absalom’s mother, and others). The Chronicler’s genealogical record preserves a complex picture of Caleb’s household with multiple secondary unions, each producing distinct clan branches.
2.49: She also bore Shaaph (šāʿap̄) the father of Madmannah (maḏmannāh), and Sheva (šᵉwāʾ) the father of Machbenah (maḵbēnāh) and Gibea (giḇᵉʿāh); and Caleb’s daughter was Achsah (ʿaḵsāh). Madmannah and Machbenah are Judean towns of uncertain identification. The mention of Achsah by name is notable: she is the only named daughter in the Calebite genealogy, and her name connects her to the famous Achsah of Josh 15:16-19 and Judg 1:12-15 — the daughter of Caleb the Jephunnehite who requested the upper and lower springs as her inheritance. Whether this is the same Achsah (the Chronicler conflating the two Calebs) or a different person bearing the same name within the clan is debated.
2.50: These were the sons of Caleb. The sons of Hur, the firstborn of Ephrathah: Shobal the father of Kiriath-jearim (qiryat yᵉʿārîm), Salma the father of Bethlehem (bêt leḥem), and Hareph the father of Beth-gader (bêt gāḏer). The summary formula closes Caleb’s section and introduces a new subsection through Hur (Caleb’s son via Ephrath, v19-20). The three sons of Hur are eponyms of three significant Judahite locations: Kiriath-jearim (where the ark rested before Jerusalem, 1 Sam 7:1-2), Bethlehem (David’s city), and Beth-gader (unidentified).
2.51: Salma was the father of Bethlehem (bêt leḥem), Hareph the father of Beth-gader. This verse continues v50’s list. The repetition of Salma as Bethlehem’s eponym confirms the Calebite-Ephrathite clan’s connection to the Bethlehem area; the same clan traditions explain why the MKT consistently calls this area “Ephrathah” (Ruth 4:11; Mic 5:2). The Chronicler’s genealogy traces David’s Bethlehem roots not just through Jesse’s family line (v10-15) but through the geographic-eponymic traditions of the Calebite territory.
2.52: Shobal the father of Kiriath-jearim had the following sons: Haroeh, half of the Manahathites (ḥăṣî hamMᵉnûḥôt). The unusual phrase “half of the Manahathites” appears twice (v52, 54) and refers to two clan groups sharing the settlement at Manahath — a division of one clan between two genealogical lines. Manahath is a place in Judah (cf. 2 Sam 14:23, 32 where Absalom went to Geshur, though that is a different place). The genealogical terminology encodes territorial realities about how clans divided and shared settlements.
2.53: The clans of Kiriath-jearim: the Ithrites, Puthites, Shumathites, and Mishraites; from these came the Zorathites and Eshtaolites. The listing of clan groups associated with Kiriath-jearim reflects the city’s composite character as a settlement housing multiple clans. Zorah and Eshtaol are towns in the Shephelah associated with Samson (Judg 13:2, 25; 16:31) and with the Danite migration (Judg 18:2, 8, 11) — a geographic cluster in the western Judah/Dan border area. The Chronicler links these Shephelah settlements to the Kiriath-jearim genealogical network.
2.54: The sons of Salma: Bethlehem and the Netophathites, Atroth-beth-joab, and half of the Manahathites, the Zorites. Netophah (nᵉṭōp̄āh) is a town near Bethlehem whose inhabitants appear in Nehemiah’s list of returning exiles (Neh 7:26) and in the lists of David’s mighty men (2 Sam 23:28-29). The association of Netophah with the Bethlehem genealogical cluster confirms a tight network of related clans in the Bethlehem-Ephrathah district.
2.55: The families of the scribes (sōpᵉrîm) who lived at Jabez: the Tirathites, Shimeathites, and Sucathites. These are the Kenites who came from Hammath (ḥammāt), the father of the house of Rechab (bêt rēḵāḇ). The mention of Jabez (a place, not the Jabez of 4:9-10) as the home of scribal families connects the genealogy to professional-guild traditions. The Kenites (qênî) were Israel’s traditional metalworking allies (Jael was a Kenite, Judg 4:11); their appearance in this Judahite genealogical context reflects the absorption of non-Israelite artisan groups into Judah’s social structure. The Rechabites (rēḵāḇîm) of Jer 35 are their descendants, known for their Mosaic wilderness ethic.

3.17: The designation of Jeconiah (Jehoiachin) as hāʾăsîr — ‘the captive’ or ‘the prisoner’ — in the genealogy (v17) is the Chronicler’s recognition that the Davidic line passed through a deposed and imprisoned king. The word ʾāsîr (prisoner, captive) marks Jeconiah as the king taken to Babylon in 597 BCE (2 Kgs 24:8-16) where he remained in prison until Evil-merodach released him in 561 BCE (2 Kgs 25:27-30). The Chronicler’s post-exilic Davidic genealogy (vv17-24) continues through this prisoner — Shealtiel, Zerubbabel, and six more generations — demonstrating that the exile and imprisonment of the Davidic king did not sever the covenant line. Zerubbabel (v19) is the key post-exilic Davidic figure, governor of Judah under Persia (Hag 1:1; Zech 4:6-10) and ancestor of Jesus in Luke’s genealogy (Luke 3:27). Matt 1:11-12 includes Iechonias at the Babylon deportation as the hinge-point of the genealogy — the break and the continuity simultaneously — echoing the Chronicler’s own ambivalent designation: the ʾāsîr through whom the Messiah would come. The word ʾāsîr also carries the resonance of Isa 53:8 (meʿōṣer ûmimmišpāṭ luqqāḥ, ‘by oppression and judgment he was taken away’) — the Servant whose suffering is encompassed in the same vocabulary of imprisonment and removal.
3.1: The list of David’s sons born in Hebron (2 Sam 3:2-5 is the parallel): Amnon the firstborn by Ahinoam of Jezreel, Daniel the second (2 Sam 3:3 reads “Chileab” by Abigail of Carmel — the two names may refer to the same person or represent a textual variant; Chileab appears nowhere else in Samuel-Kings narratives, suggesting he died early or lived obscurely, while Daniel is the Chronicler’s form of his name). Amnon’s prominence as firstborn and his later rape of Tamar (2 Sam 13) and murder by Absalom are not recounted here; the genealogy preserves birth order without moral evaluation.
3.2: The third son: Absalom (ʾaḇšālôm — “father is peace”), son of Maacah daughter of Talmai king of Geshur. The fourth: Adonijah (ʾăḏōnîyyāhû — “YHWH is my lord”), son of Haggith. Both sons became David’s succession rivals: Absalom through armed rebellion (2 Sam 15-18) and Adonijah through attempted usurpation before Solomon’s anointing (1 Kgs 1). The genealogical list preserves the four mothers of David’s Hebron sons (Ahinoam, Abigail, Maacah, Haggith) — each a distinct political alliance, explaining the sons’ different bases of support.
3.3: The fifth: Shephatiah (šᵉp̄aṭyāh — “YHWH has judged”) by Abital; the sixth: Ithream (yitrᵉʿām — “abundance of people”) by his wife Eglah. The six Hebron sons and their six different mothers document the political complexity of David’s reign in Hebron (c. 1010–1003 BC). Each marriage represented an alliance with a different clan or region. The careful preservation of matrilineal data — unusual in biblical genealogy — reflects the importance of knowing which queen-mother stood behind each potential heir.
3.4: Six sons born in Hebron; David reigned there seven years and six months. He reigned thirty-three years in Jerusalem. The regnal summary of the Hebron period within the genealogy is unusual — genealogies typically do not incorporate regnal data. Its inclusion here marks the narrative significance of the transition from Hebron to Jerusalem and the corresponding shift in David’s genealogical productivity. The seven-year-six-month Hebron reign and thirty-three-year Jerusalem reign align with the summary in 2 Sam 5:5 and 1 Kgs 2:11.
3.5: The Jerusalem sons of David by Bathsheba (bat šebaʿ, daughter of Ammiel — 2 Sam 11:3 reads Eliam son of Ahithophel, which is the patronym Ammiel inverted in Hebrew; Ammiel = my people is God, Eliam = my God is people): Shimea (šimᵉʿāʾ), Shobab, Nathan (nātān), and Solomon (šᵉlōmōh) — four sons. Nathan is the Son of David who appears in Luke’s genealogy of Jesus (Luke 3:31) as opposed to Solomon in Matthew’s (Matt 1:6). The Chronicler’s listing of Nathan before Solomon (in birth order, as Shimea and Shobab precede both) confirms the separate genealogical lines that produced the two NT genealogical traditions.
3.6: Nine more sons: Ibhar (yiḇḥār — “he chooses”), Elishama, and Eliphelet. This list and the following verses contain some of the most complex textual issues in Chronicles: Elishama and Eliphelet appear twice in vv6-8, which some scholars attribute to a duplicate source incorporated by the Chronicler, others to two different sons bearing the same name (which does occur in ancient royal households). The parallel in 2 Sam 5:14-16 and 1 Chr 14:4-7 have slightly different lists, suggesting the Chronicler had multiple source traditions.
3.7: And Nogah (nōgah — “brilliance/shining”), Nepheg (nepeḡ, possibly “sprout/shoot”), and Japhia (yāp̄îaʿ — “shining/radiant”). Nogah does not appear in the parallel 2 Sam 5:14-16 list; its presence here and in 1 Chr 14:6 (but not 2 Samuel) is one of the textual variants between Samuel and Chronicles that may reflect a different source tradition rather than an addition. Nepheg and Japhia are unattested elsewhere outside the royal genealogy lists.
3.8: And Elishama, Eliada (ʾelyāḏāʿ — “God knows”), and Eliphelet — nine sons. The total of nine additional Jerusalem sons (v6-8), plus the four Bathsheba sons (v5), gives thirteen Jerusalem sons, bringing David’s total named legitimate sons to nineteen (six Hebron + thirteen Jerusalem). The Chronicler notes these are David’s sons “besides the sons of the concubines” (v9), indicating further children whose mothers are not named in the royal registry.
3.9: All these were David’s sons, besides the sons of the concubines (bᵉnê hāpîlagšîm). Their sister was Tamar (tāmār). Tamar’s name is inserted into the genealogical list — one of very few sisters mentioned in royal genealogy. Her name’s preservation alongside her brothers is a deliberate act of the genealogical tradition, remembering her despite (or because of) the narrative of violation (2 Sam 13) that she endured. The Chronicler records her existence without narrating her story, but the name alone carries its weight for any reader of 2 Samuel.
3.10: Solomon’s son was Rehoboam (reḥaḇᵉʿām — “the people is enlarged”); his son Abijah (ʾăḇiyyāh — “my father is YHWH”); his son Asa (ʾāsāʾ, possibly “healer”); his son Jehoshaphat (yᵉhôšāp̄āṭ — “YHWH has judged”). The royal genealogy from Solomon to Zedekiah (v10-16) runs through the same kings whose reigns are narrated in 1-2 Kings and 2 Chronicles. The Chronicler’s genealogy presents the succession as a single unbroken chain — the narrative of apostasy and faithfulness compressed into names and succession markers.
3.11: Joram (yôrām — “YHWH is exalted”) his son, Ahaziah (ʾăḥazyāh — “YHWH has grasped”) his son, Joash (yôʾāš — “YHWH has given/fire of YHWH”) his son. Joram was Jehoshaphat’s son who married Athaliah daughter of Ahab and Jezebel — the most direct intrusion of northern apostasy into the Davidic line. Ahaziah was killed by Jehu (2 Kgs 9:27); Joash (the child hidden in the temple, 2 Kgs 11) began a reform that later foundered. The genealogy preserves the succession without noting these crises.
3.12: Amaziah (ʾămaṣyāh — “YHWH is mighty”) his son, Azariah (ʿăzaryāh — “YHWH has helped”) his son, Jotham (yôtām — “YHWH is perfect/complete”) his son. Azariah is the king called Uzziah in Isaiah, Hosea, Amos, and Zechariah (cf. 2 Kgs 15:32; Isa 1:1); both names are used in Chronicles and Kings for the same king. His fifty-two-year reign brought prosperity and expansion before his presumptuous entry into the temple led to his leprosy (2 Chr 26:16-21). Jotham completed the temple outer gate while his leprous father lived in a separate house.
3.13: Ahaz (ʾāḥāz — abbreviated from “YHWH has grasped”) his son, Hezekiah (ḥizqiyyāhû — “YHWH strengthens”) his son, Manasseh (mᵉnaššeh — “causing to forget”) his son. The contrast between Ahaz (the king who faced the Syro-Ephraimite coalition and appealed to Assyria rather than YHWH, Isa 7) and his son Hezekiah (who trusted YHWH against Sennacherib) is one of the great theological contrasts in Kings/Chronicles. Manasseh reversed all of Hezekiah’s reforms (2 Kgs 21), though 2 Chr 33 records his captivity and repentance — not mentioned in Kings.
3.14: Amon (ʾāmôn, possibly related to the Egyptian deity Amun, a detail that fits his apostasy) his son, Josiah (yōʾšiyyāhû — “may YHWH give/sustain”) his son. Amon reigned two years and was assassinated by his own officials (2 Kgs 21:23-24). Josiah reversed his father’s apostasy in the greatest reform of any Judahite king, discovering the book of the Torah in the eighteenth year of his reign (2 Kgs 22:8). The genealogical succession from apostate Amon to reforming Josiah reflects the covenantal theology of Kings: righteousness is not biologically transmitted but is a divine gift within the Davidic framework.
3.15: The sons of Josiah: the firstborn Johanan, the second Jehoiakim (yᵉhôyāqîm — “YHWH raises up”), the third Zedekiah (ṣiḏqiyyāhû — “YHWH is righteousness”), the fourth Shallum (šallûm — “recompense/restored”). The textual complexity here is significant: Johanan (the firstborn) does not appear in the Kings succession narrative. Shallum is identified in Jer 22:11 as “Shallum son of Josiah, who reigned in place of his father Josiah” — making Shallum the same as Jehoahaz (2 Kgs 23:30-34), the fourth-born who was chosen king first. The Chronicler preserves birth order (Johanan first, Shallum fourth) while the historical succession followed popular choice rather than primogeniture.
3.16: The sons of Jehoiakim: Jeconiah (yᵉḵonyāh — also Coniah in Jer 22:24; Jehoiachin in Kings) his son, and Zedekiah his son. The “Zedekiah his son” here (Jehoiakim’s son) is distinct from Josiah’s son Zedekiah (v15) — this is either a confusion in the text or represents two distinct individuals sharing the name. Jeconiah (= Jehoiachin) reigned three months before the first Babylonian deportation (597 BC); Jeremiah’s oracle about Coniah (Jer 22:30 — “write this man down as childless”) is theologically resolved by Matthew’s genealogy, where Jesus descends through Jeconiah but receives the throne through divine adoption rather than biological succession.
3.18: And Malchiram (malḵîrām — “my king is exalted”), Pedaiah (pᵉḏāyāh — “YHWH has ransomed”), Shenazzar, Jekamiah (yᵉqamyāh — “YHWH establishes”), Hoshama (hôšāmāʿ — “YHWH has heard”), and Nedabiah (nᵉḏaḇyāh — “YHWH is willing/noble”). Seven sons of Jeconiah in exile (v17-18). The names include Hebrew theophoric names alongside Shenazzar, which is probably a Babylonian name (šin-ab-uṣur — “Sin protect the father” — cognate with Sheshbazzar, another early leader of the return). The combination of Hebrew and Babylonian names reflects the cultural reality of the Babylonian exile.
3.19: The sons of Pedaiah: Zerubbabel (zᵉrubbāḇel — “seed/shoot of Babylon” or “descendant born in Babylon”) and Shimei. The sons of Zerubbabel: Meshullam and Hananiah. Their sister was Shelomith. Zerubbabel is identified elsewhere as son of Shealtiel (Hag 1:1; Matt 1:12; Luke 3:27) rather than son of Pedaiah here. The discrepancy is usually resolved by levirate marriage: Pedaiah may have married his dead brother Shealtiel’s wife, making Zerubbabel biologically Pedaiah’s son but legally Shealtiel’s. Zerubbabel led the first return from exile (Ezra 2:2; 3:2) and supervised the rebuilding of the Second Temple foundation.
3.20: And Hashubah (ḥăšûḇāh — “YHWH has considered”), Ohel (ʾōhel — “tent”), Berechiah (bᵉreḵyāh — “YHWH blesses”), Hasadiah (ḥăsaḏyāh — “YHWH is loyal/gracious”), and Jushabhesed (yûšaḇḥeseḏ — “loving kindness is restored”, a theologically dense name) — five more. Zerubbabel’s sons include a remarkable name in Jushabhesed: “loving kindness/covenant loyalty is restored” is a name expressing hope for the restoration of YHWH’s ḥeseḏ in the post-exilic period — a theological statement in a name, consistent with the expectation that the Davidic line’s continuation through Zerubbabel signals YHWH’s ongoing covenantal commitment.
3.21: The sons of Hananiah: Pelatiah (pᵉlaṭyāh — “YHWH has saved/escaped”) and Jeshaiah (yᵉšaʿyāh — “YHWH is salvation”). Then the son of Rephaiah (rᵉp̄āyāh — “YHWH heals”), the son of Arnan (ʾarnān), the son of Obadiah (ʿōḇaḏyāh — “servant of YHWH”), the son of Shechaniah (šᵉḵanyāh — “YHWH dwells”). The list here is difficult syntactically — the repeated “son of” constructions have been read as a list of individuals (some MT reading) or as a genealogical chain (four consecutive generations). The grammatical ambiguity reflects either the difficulty of the source material or intentional genealogical compression.
3.22: The sons of Shechaniah: Shemaiah (šᵉmaʿyāh — “YHWH has heard”). The sons of Shemaiah: Hattush, Igal, Bariah, Neariah, and Shaphat — six. These six sons of Shemaiah complete the Shechaniah branch. Hattush appears in Ezra 8:2 as one of the leaders of the second return from exile under Artaxerxes — providing a chronological anchor for the genealogy’s endpoint. The Chronicler’s genealogy thus extends from David’s Hebron sons through the Babylonian exile and into the Persian-period return, connecting the Davidic promise to the post-exilic community.
3.23: The sons of Neariah: Elioenai (ʾelyôʿênay — “my eyes toward YHWH” — an unusual name expressing prayerful dependence), Hizkiah (ḥizqiyyāh — a short form of Hezekiah, “YHWH strengthens”), and Azrikam (ʿazrîqām — “my help has risen”) — three. The post-exilic names in this generation continue the theophoric pattern of hope and restoration: “eyes toward YHWH” expresses the posture of prayerful waiting that characterized the community of the return.
3.24: The sons of Elioenai: Hodaviah (hôḏawyāhû — “praise YHWH”), Eliashib (ʾelyāšîḇ — “God restores”), Pelaiah (pᵉlāyāh — “YHWH is wonderful”), Akkub (ʿaqqûḇ, possibly “protected by YHWH”), Johanan (yôḥānān — “YHWH is gracious”), Delaiah (dᵉlāyāhû — “YHWH has drawn up/lifted”), and Anani (ʿănānî — “YHWH is my cloud/presence”) — seven. The genealogy ends with seven sons of the last named generation, continuing approximately three or four generations beyond Zerubbabel. This places the genealogy’s endpoint in the early Persian period (c. 500-450 BC), consistent with the Chronicler’s likely date of composition. The name Anani (“my cloud”) as the final name in the Davidic genealogy resonates with the cloud of divine presence that accompanied Israel through the wilderness and filled Solomon’s temple — the genealogy ends with a name pointing toward the return of YHWH’s presence to his people.

7.23: The naming of Ephraim’s son Beriah carries a folk etymology: wayyiqrāʾ ʾet šᵉmô bᵉrîʿāh kî bᵉrāʿāh hāyᵉtāh bᵉḇêtô, ‘and he called his name Beriah because disaster had come to pass (bᵉrāʿāh hāyᵉtāh) in his house.’ The etymology is a paronomastic play: the name bᵉrîʿāh is heard as bᵉ-rāʿāh, ‘in/with calamity’ (from the root rāʿaʿ). The occasion is the death of Ephraim’s sons at the hands of the men of Gath (v21) — a genealogical fragment not attested elsewhere. The naming-in-grief practice appears throughout the patriarchal narratives: Rachel names her last son Ben-oni (‘son of my sorrow’) before Jacob renames him Benjamin (Gen 35:18); Phinehas’s wife names her son Ichabod (‘no glory’) at the loss of the ark (1 Sam 4:21). The Chronicler’s inclusion of the Beriah fragment is unusual in a genealogical list — it interrupts the sequence with a narrative moment — suggesting the Chronicler is drawing on an independent tradition about Ephraim’s household loss. Beriah’s name encodes the family history: his very existence is memorialized as born out of tragedy.
7.1: Issachar’s four sons (Tola, Puah, Jashub, Shimron) match Gen 46:13 and Num 26:23–25 almost exactly, confirming a stable Issacharite list. The Chronicler’s enumeration formula “four in all” (ʾarbaʿāh) is a recurring counting-close that marks the end of a first-generation list and sets up the descendant expansion that follows.
7.2: Tola’s six sons are introduced as rāʾšê lᵉḇêt ʾăḇôtām (heads of their ancestral houses), placing this genealogy within the military-administrative register of the Chronicler. The figure of 22,600 warriors in David’s day contrasts with Num 26:25 which gives 64,300 for Moses’ time; the reduction may reflect actual demographic change, or divergent source traditions. Tôlᵉḏôtām (‘their generations’) signals the Chronicler’s characteristic mode: genealogy and time-marking together.
7.3: Izrahiah’s five sons are all “leaders” (rāʾšîm). The explicit count (“five”) is unusual for a sub-list and signals that all five sons have equal standing as heads — no hierarchy among them. Izrahiah’s name means ‘YHWH shines forth’ (yizraḥyāh), a theophoric name that establishes an Issacharite lineage with explicit YHWH-devotion.
7.4: Thirty-six thousand troops “ready for war” (ʾanšê milḥāmāh) attached to Izrahiah’s sons; the reason given is “for they had many wives and sons.” The genealogical logic is demographic: many wives produce many sons, many sons produce large war-bands. The Chronicler’s genealogies function simultaneously as population registers and military census documents — a dual purpose inherited from Numbers’ tribal counts.
7.5: The phrase ʿal-kol-hityaḥaś (according to all the genealogical enrollment) is diagnostic of the Chronicler’s vocabulary: the root yāḥaś (to enroll in the genealogy) appears almost exclusively in Chronicles and Ezra-Nehemiah, reflecting the post-exilic concern with documented lineage as the basis for land-rights and temple participation. The grand total of 87,000 Issacharite warriors establishes the tribe as one of the most militarily significant in the Davidic census tradition.
7.6: Benjamin’s three sons here (Bela, Becher, Jediael) diverge from both Gen 46:21 (ten sons) and Num 26:38–40 (Bela, Ashbel, Ahiram, Shupham, Hupham, five). The Chronicler is drawing on a different Benjaminite source, possibly a tribal register compiled under different administrative conditions. The variation in Benjaminite genealogy across the Pentateuch and Chronicles is one of the most complex source-critical puzzles in the Hebrew genealogical tradition.
7.7: Bela’s five sons include Iri (whose sons Shuppim and Huppim appear in v12). Gibbôrê ḥayil (mighty warriors) is the Chronicler’s standard honorific for genealogical line-holders who are simultaneously clan-heads and soldiers. The count of 22,034 for Bela’s line situates this within the David-era military assessment framework established throughout ch7.
7.8: Becher’s nine sons include Anathoth — the place-name later attached to Jeremiah’s home village (Jer 1:1) and to one of the Levitical cities in Benjamin (Josh 21:18). The embedding of a place-name as a personal name suggests the Chronicler is recording a clan-founder: Becher’s son Anathoth was the ancestor who settled the town of Anathoth. The genealogical enrollment of Becher’s household is 20,200 mighty warriors.
7.9: The hityaḥaśām formula closes the Becher section, confirming 20,200 warriors. The Chronicler uses the reflexive Hithpael of yāḥaś to indicate self-registration in the genealogical record — a legal act of claiming membership in a tribe through documented enrollment, not simply birth-right. This implies that the tribal registers the Chronicler used were official administrative documents, not merely oral traditions.
7.10: Jediael’s son Bilhan has seven sons, the last being Ahishahar. The name Bilhan recalls the Horite clan leader of Gen 36:27 — a Edomite/Horite name appearing in Benjaminite genealogy, suggesting either name-borrowing across boundaries or historical intermarriage. Bilhan’s seven sons all serve as military commanders, continuing the administrative-register character of the chapter.
7.11: Jediael’s military total is 17,200, described as ṣōʾê ṣāḇāʾ lammilḥāmāh (those fit for going out to the army for battle) — a precise conscription formula. The three Benjaminite sub-lists (Bela, Becher, Jediael) together yield a massive northern Benjaminite warrior pool, anchoring Benjamin’s military prominence before the Saul narrative that follows later in the book.
7.12: Shuppim and Huppim are here sons of Ir (a Benjaminite name); yet the identical pair appears in Num 26:39 as Benjaminite clans and in Gen 46:21 as Benjaminite sons. The second part of v12 — “Hushim was the son of Aher” — is widely recognized as a displaced Dan fragment. In Gen 46:23, Hushim is the only listed son of Dan; ʾaḥēr (‘another’) may be a scribal placeholder for a damaged or omitted name (Dan), explaining why Dan is absent from the tribal genealogy list in ch7. The verse is textually problematic; the LXX and Syriac offer divergent readings.
7.13: Naphtali’s four sons (Jahziel, Guni, Jezer, Shallum) nearly match Gen 46:24 (Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer, Shillem) and Num 26:48–49; only ‘Shallum’ vs. ‘Shillem’ differs. The brief notation “from Bilhah” (bᵉnê bîlhāh) anchors Naphtali in the matrilineal tradition: both Dan and Naphtali were born to Jacob’s concubine Bilhah (Gen 30:7–8). The Chronicler does not explain the tradition; the parentage note links the text to Genesis for readers who know the narrative.
7.14: Manasseh’s genealogy through his Aramean concubine (pîlegšô hāʾarammît) reflects the historical and geographical reality of Gilead: Transjordanian Manasseh bordered Aram and shows evidence of substantial Aramean intermarriage in its population. Machir, Manasseh’s firstborn, was the father of Gilead — a territorial eponym: the region of Gilead takes its name from this genealogical ancestor (Num 26:29; Josh 17:1).
7.15: Machir takes wives for Huppim and Shuppim — the same names as the Benjaminite clan of v12, now appearing as women (or as male recipients of wives). The MT is syntactically difficult and translators disagree; some read “Huppim and Shuppim” as the names of Machir’s wives rather than as men for whom he obtained wives. His sister Maachah becomes his wife in some readings (endogamous marriage). The mention of Zelophehad who “had only daughters” connects to the landmark Num 27 case where Zelophehad’s daughters successfully petitioned for inheritance rights in the absence of male heirs.
7.16: Maachah bears Peresh (‘horseman’?) and Sheresh (‘root’); Sheresh’s sons are Ulam and Rakem. These names are not attested elsewhere in this genealogical combination. The repeated matrilineal focus (which woman bore which son) in the Manasseh genealogy is unusual and may reflect source material that preserved women’s names because inheritance passed through named female lines (cf. the Zelophehad daughters’ precedent, Num 27).
7.17: Ulam’s son Bedan completes the Gilead sub-line: Manasseh → Machir → Gilead → Ulam → Bedan. Bedan appears also as a judge in 1 Sam 12:11 (where some manuscripts read Barak); this may be a different Bedan, or a tradition identifying a Manassite war-leader with a judge’s career. The nested genealogical formula “son of Gilead son of Machir son of Manasseh” is the Chronicler’s way of establishing deep pedigree legitimacy for each sub-line.
7.18: Hammoleketh (‘the queen’; also spelled Hammolecheth) bears Ishhod, Abiezer, and Mahalah. Whether “hammoleketh” is a proper name or a title is debated; it could denote a woman who exercised royal or clan-leadership in Manasseh. Abiezer of Manasseh is the clan of Gideon (Judg 6:11, 24, 34) — this genealogical note therefore traces the great judge of the Midianite period to a named ancestral line within Manasseh through a woman whose name means ‘the queen.’
7.19: Shemida’s four sons (Ahian, Shechem, Likhi, Aniam) are otherwise unattested beyond Num 26:32 where Shemida is listed as a Manassite clan. Shechem as a personal name in Manasseh is historically plausible: Shechem (the city) was within Manassite territory and may have had Manassite clan associations before it became a Levitical city (Josh 21:21). The names in this sub-list have no parallels elsewhere, suggesting the Chronicler had access to a distinct Manassite administrative source.
7.20: Ephraim’s linear descent (Shuthelah → Bered → Tahath → Eladah → Tahath) is a segmented genealogy condensed into a linear form. The repeated name Tahath (two different men) reflects the practice of naming sons after grandfathers; the Chronicler or his source is careful to distinguish them by their position in the sequence. Shuthelah appears in Num 26:35–36 as an Ephraimite clan with its own sub-clans, confirming the antiquity of this line.
7.21: Ezer and Elead are killed by the men of Gath while raiding their herds. This is a narrative fragment embedded in the genealogy, with no parallel in any other biblical text. The phrase “because they came down to take their herds” (kî yārᵉḏû lāqaḥat ʾet miqnêhem) clarifies that the Ephraimites were the aggressors in a cattle raid; the Gittites’ counter-attack was a legitimate reprisal. The episode functions etiologically: it explains a gap in the Ephraimite genealogical line and accounts for a tradition of Ephraimite-Philistine hostility predating the main settlement period.
7.22: Ephraim’s extended mourning (“many days,” yāmîm rabbîm) and his brothers’ consolation (nāḥam Piel) are rare emotional notes in a genealogical list. The Piel of nāḥam is the same root used when Jacob’s sons attempt to comfort him after Joseph’s apparent death (Gen 37:35, where their comfort fails) and when Job’s friends come to comfort him (Job 2:11). The parallel with Jacob’s grief over a lost son is striking: in both cases, a patriarch mourns the loss of the generation below him; in both cases, the story continues despite the grief.
7.24: Sheerah (šᵉʾērāh, ‘female relative’ or a personal name from the root šʾr, remainder/kin) is the only woman in the Hebrew Bible explicitly credited with founding and building cities. She built Lower Beth-horon, Upper Beth-horon, and Uzzen-sheerah. Beth-horon (upper and lower) guarded the principal descent from the Benjaminite-Ephraimite highlands to the Aijalon plain and the coast; Joshua pursued the Amorite coalition through this pass (Josh 10:10–11). The third city, Uzzen-sheerah (ʾuzzên šᵉʾērāh, ‘ear/attentiveness of Sheerah’), memorializes her name in geography — a permanent monument in the landscape. The Chronicler preserves this tradition without comment or qualification, treating a woman’s city-founding as genealogically significant heritage.
7.25: Rephah and Resheph begin the linear descent from Ephraim toward Joshua. These names are otherwise unattested in this context; rešep is also the name of a Canaanite deity (fire/plague), but personal theophoric names involving Resheph appear in Egyptian documents from the New Kingdom period for Canaanite-Semitic individuals. The line passes through Tahan, Ladan, and then Ammihud.
7.26: Ladan → Ammihud → Elishama. Elishama was the tribal leader of Ephraim in the wilderness census (Num 1:10; 2:18; 7:48–53; 10:22) and the grandfather of Joshua in the genealogy the Chronicler is building. The Chronicler is providing Joshua with documented genealogical roots seven generations deep from Ephraim, grounding the military saviour of the conquest in a legitimate administrative lineage.
7.27: Elishama → Non → Joshua (yᵉhôšûaʿ, ‘YHWH saves/is salvation’).
