This is the only place in the Chronicler’s work where Joshua receives an explicit genealogy connecting him to Ephraim through named ancestors. The name yᵉhôšûaʿ is theophoric: the yᵉhô- prefix is the divine name and -šûaʿ is from the root yšʿ (to save, deliver). Moses renamed Hoshea (hôšēaʿ) to Yehoshua (Num 13:16) — the added theophoric element transforms ‘salvation’ into ‘YHWH is salvation.’ The name prefigures his role as the instrument of YHWH’s deliverance of Israel into the land.
7.28: Ephraim’s territorial inventory includes Bethel, Naaran, Gezer, Shechem, and Ayyah. Bethel, the site of Jacob’s vision (Gen 28:10–22) and later Jeroboam’s golden calf (1 Kgs 12:29), is here anchored in Ephraimite genealogical tradition as rightful Ephraimite territory. Gezer, a key Canaanite city-state in the Shephelah, was given to Solomon as a dowry (1 Kgs 9:16); its inclusion in an Ephraimite territorial list reflects the tribal claim that preceded the Solomonic arrangement. The territorial inventory follows Josh 16 closely.
7.29: Manasseh’s border cities — Beth-shean, Taanach, Megiddo, Dor — are precisely the cities that Manasseh failed to drive out in the early settlement period (Josh 17:11–13; Judg 1:27–28). The Chronicler’s inventory follows the same list as Joshua, preserving the historical record of the contested border. The closing phrase “these are the sons of Joseph son of Israel” brackets the entire Joseph-tribal section (Manasseh and Ephraim in ch7:14–29) under the Joseph patronymic, reflecting the Chronicler’s awareness that the two tribes constituted Joseph’s double-portion inheritance.
7.30: Asher’s sons (Imnah, Ishvah, Ishvi, Beriah, and sister Serah) match Gen 46:17 and Num 26:44–46 closely. Serah (seraḥ) is remarkable: a woman named across three distinct genealogical passages (Gen 46:17; Num 26:46; 1 Chr 7:30), an unusual longevity of tradition for a female ancestor. Post-biblical tradition identified Serah bat Asher as an immortal elder-woman who survived from the patriarchal period into the exodus — a midrashic elaboration of her unusual genealogical prominence.
7.31: Beriah’s sons are Heber and Malchiel; Malchiel is the “father of Birzaith” — another city-founding genealogical note (cf. Sheerah v24; Shemed v12 of ch8). bîrzayit may be an olive-oil town (the root zayit = olive). Heber’s descendants are extended further in vv32–39, making Heber’s line the main Asherite genealogical focus.
7.32: Heber fathers Japhlet, Shomer, Hotham, and their sister Shua. The inclusion of another named sister (Shua) mirrors the inclusion of Serah (v30); the Asherite genealogy preserves two generations of named women within the tribal register, which is rare. Shua appears elsewhere only as the name of Judah’s Canaanite wife (Gen 38:2), so the name crossed tribal boundaries.
7.33: Japhlet’s three sons (Pasach, Bimhal, Ashvath) are otherwise unattested. The name Japhlet echoes Japhlet’s border in Josh 16:3, possibly marking a Japhletite clan that held territory on the Ephraimite-Asherite border. None of the three sons have cognates in the surrounding lists.
7.34: Shomer’s sons include Ahi and Aram. The name Aram as a personal name in an Asherite genealogy is notable: Asher’s territory in the far north (coastal Galilee) bordered Aramean territory and showed Aramean cultural contact. The names in this sub-section are without parallel; the Chronicler is preserving source material that has no Pentateuchal correlate.
7.35: Helem (possibly another name for Hotham of v32 — the same name in slightly different vocalization) heads a four-son sub-list: Zophah, Imna, Shelesh, Amal. The uncertainty over Helem/Hotham identity is a typical feature of Chronicles’ genealogies where scribal transmission occasionally introduces name variants for the same individual.
7.36: Zophah’s six sons, beginning with Suah (possibly ‘sweeping away’) and Harnepher. Several names in this list appear to be of non-Semitic or mixed origin, consistent with Asher’s northern coastal location where Phoenician, Hurrian, and Canaanite naming conventions overlapped with Israelite ones.
7.37: The second half of Zophah’s sons: Bezer, Hod, Shamma, Shilshah, Ithran, and Beera. Bezer appears also as a city of refuge east of the Jordan (Deut 4:43; Josh 20:8); personal names and place-names frequently overlap in tribal genealogies because clan-founders named sites after themselves or vice versa. Ithran appears in a Horite genealogy (Gen 36:26) — another instance of a name crossing the Israelite-Edomite boundary.
7.38: Jether’s three sons: Jephunneh, Pispa, and Ara. Jephunneh is the name of Caleb’s father (Num 13:6), a famous Judahite; its appearance as an Asherite sub-clan head suggests either name-borrowing or a genuine genealogical overlap between the two tribes in the Negev-coastal region. Ara (perhaps from Aramaic/North Semitic) continues the pattern of names with possible non-Israelite origins in Asher’s genealogy.
7.39: Ulla’s three sons: Arah, Hanniel, Rizia. Arah appears in Ezra-Nehemiah as a returnee family name (Ezra 2:5; Neh 7:10), suggesting that the Asherite genealogy may have been used in the post-exilic period to verify the tribal credentials of families claiming Asherite origin. The Chronicler’s compilation thus served a living community function: establishing who belonged to which tribe for the purposes of land-allocation and cultic participation after the return from Babylon.
7.40: The Asher chapter closes with the military-census formula: “All these were sons of Asher, heads of ancestral houses, chosen men, mighty warriors (gibbôrê ḥayil), outstanding leaders. Their genealogical enrollment as warriors ready for war numbered 26,000 men.” The phrase rāʾšê hᵉnāśîʾîm (heads of the rulers/leaders) is unusually honorific. The 26,000 figure is the smallest of the tribal military totals in ch7, but the closing formula is the most elaborate, as if the Chronicler wanted to honor Asher’s northern frontier position with heightened ceremonial language.

8.33: The Chronicler names Saul’s sons as Jonathan, Malchishua, Abinadab, and ʾeš baʿal (Eshbaal, ‘man of Baal’); Jonathan’s son is named mᵉrîḇ baʿal (Merib-baal, ‘Baal contends/pleads’). These are the original theophoric names. The parallel passages in Samuel use ʾîšbōšet (Ishbosheth, ‘man of shame’) and mᵉpîḇōšet (Mephibosheth, ‘from the mouth of shame’) — systematic substitutions of bōšet (shame, disgrace) for baʿal. This scribal practice of replacing Baal-element theophoric names with bōšet is attested across Samuel and reflects the later theological aversion to preserving Baal-names even in personal names from the Saul-David period. The Chronicler preserves the older forms: the theophoric baʿal in these pre-monarchic and early monarchic personal names did not necessarily indicate Baal-worship — baʿal as a common noun means ‘lord’ or ‘master’ and was sometimes used of YHWH (Hos 2:16: tiqrᵉʾî ʾîšî wᵉlōʾ tiqrᵉʾî lî ʿôḏ baʿlî, ‘you will call me “My Husband” and no longer call me “My Baal”’ — YHWH himself once called baʿal). The Samuel substitutions are a later editorial censorship; the Chronicler’s retention of Eshbaal and Merib-baal reflects either access to earlier sources or a different editorial policy. The LXX often mediates between both traditions.
8.1: Benjamin’s sons in ch8 (Bela, Ashbel, Aharah, Nohah, Rapha) differ from Gen 46:21 (ten sons including Muppim, Huppim, Ard) and Num 26:38–40 (five with Ahiram, Shupham, Hupham). “Aharah” may be a scribal variant of “Ahiram” (transposition of consonants); “Nohah” and “Rapha” have no Pentateuchal parallel. The Chronicler is using a distinct Benjaminite source for ch8 — possibly a tribal register compiled independently of Genesis and Numbers — resulting in the two partially overlapping Benjaminite genealogies of ch7 and ch8.
8.2: The ordinal designations (firstborn, second, third, fourth, fifth) for Benjamin’s five sons establish primogeniture order for the tribal genealogy. The ordinal series is unusual: the Chronicler normally lists names without rank, but here the ranking parallels the format used in Num 26:38–40 where Benjamin’s sons are listed as “after their families.” The ordinal sequence anchors Bela as the genealogically primary line, which explains why Bela’s descendants receive the most extensive treatment in what follows.
8.3: Bela’s first three sons (Addar, Gera, Abihud) represent the first generation of the expansion. Gera is a recurring Benjaminite name: Ehud’s father (Judg 3:15), Shimei’s father (2 Sam 16:5), and here a clan in Bela’s line. The name’s repetition across generations and genealogical texts suggests it was a prestige clan name in Benjamin, recycled through multiple generations as a way of maintaining genealogical continuity with the founding ancestor.
8.4: Abishua, Naaman, and Ahoah continue Bela’s list. Naaman (nōʿamān, ‘pleasantness/grace’) is notably the name of the Aramean general healed of leprosy by Elisha (2 Kgs 5:1) — the same name in a Benjaminite tribal context confirms it was an Israelite as well as Aramean name. Naaman also appears in Num 26:40 as a Benjaminite clan, consistent with this genealogy’s overlap with Numbers.
8.5: Gera, Shephuphan, and Huram close Bela’s first sub-list. The second Gera in this list (v5 vs. v3) is either a second son named Gera or a textual error; the Chronicler’s tolerance for repeated personal names within the same family reflects actual ancient naming practice (naming after grandfathers). Shephuphan is a variant of Shupham/Muppim from Genesis and Numbers, confirming this list is partly parallel to and partly divergent from the Pentateuchal sources.
8.6: The sons of Ehud (ʾēhûḏ) — the famous left-handed judge (Judg 3:15) was a Benjaminite, son of Gera. Here Ehud’s descendants are identified as “heads of ancestral houses of the inhabitants of Geba.” Geba was a Benjaminite priestly city on the border with the Philistines (1 Sam 13:3, 16). The exile to Manahath (wayyigᵉlûm ʾel mᵉnaḥat) is otherwise unattested; Manahath is a Calebite place in the Shephelah (2:52, 54), suggesting an intra-tribal movement rather than a foreign exile.
8.7: Naaman, Ahijah, and Gera; “he removed them” (hûʾ higᵉlām) — the antecedent of “he” is syntactically ambiguous in the MT. Following the MT literally, Gera of Ehud’s line is the one who “removed them” to Manahath — possibly a forced resettlement of part of the Ehudite sub-clan, perhaps for punishment or administrative reorganization. The verse then notes Gera “fathered Uzza and Ahihud” after the relocation, continuing the genealogy past the disruption. LXX and Syriac smooth the syntax differently.
8.8: Shaharaim (‘double dawn’ or ‘the dawn-double’) is an otherwise unattested figure whose personal story is entirely unique to Chronicles. He “fathered children in the land of Moab after he had divorced his wives Hushim and Baara.” The verb šallaḥ (to send away, Piel) is the technical term for divorce. The detail that a Benjaminite patriarch settled in Moab and started a second family there is remarkable; it creates an Israelite family tradition in Moab parallel to the Ruth narrative, though moving in the opposite direction.
8.9: By his new wife Hodesh (‘the new moon’ — a lunar theophoric name) Shaharaim fathers seven sons. Jobab appears elsewhere as an Edomite king (Gen 36:33–34) and a Canaanite city-king (Josh 11:1); the name was shared across ethnic groups in the southern Levant. Zibia (ṣibyāʾ, ‘gazelle’) is a feminine name in its more common form (2 Kgs 12:1), suggesting that male and female forms of animal names were used interchangeably in the naming tradition.
8.10: Jeuz, Sachia, and Mirmah close Hodesh’s sons, designated “heads of ancestral houses.” Whatever the moral ambiguity of Shaharaim’s marital history, his sons by Hodesh achieve the honorific rāʾšê ʾābôt (heads of fathers’ houses) — the Chronicler records genealogical legitimacy without editorial judgment on the patriarch’s personal conduct. The genealogy reports; it does not adjudicate.
8.11: By his first wife Hushim, Shaharaim had also fathered Abitub and Elpaal before the divorce. Elpaal (‘God has performed’ or ‘El works’; ʾel pāʿal) becomes the primary genealogical line in vv12–18. The Chronicler tracks both sets of children from Shaharaim’s divided household, preserving the parallel branches even though they originated from different mothers in different countries.
8.12: Elpaal’s sons include Eber, Misham, and Shemed, with the editorial note that “Shemed built Ono and Lod with its towns.” Ono and Lod are in the Shephelah near the modern Tel Aviv coastal plain; Lod is present-day Lod/Lydda. These cities appear in Ezra 2:33 and Neh 11:35 as Benjaminite post-exilic settlement towns, so Shemed’s city-founding establishes the genealogical claim to these sites that the returning community would have exercised after the Babylonian exile. The formula “with its towns” (wᵉbᵉnōtêhā) is the standard territorial formula for a city with dependent villages.
8.13: Beriah and Shema are “heads of ancestral houses for the inhabitants of Aijalon, who drove out the inhabitants of Gath.” The Aijalon valley was Joshua’s battlefield (Josh 10:12); the connection to driving out Gath’s inhabitants is historically puzzling, since Gath is a major Philistine city far to the southwest. This may refer to a local Gath-enclave within Benjaminite territory (several places bore the name Gath, meaning ‘winepress’) or to a Philistine outpost in the Aijalon region. The verb bāraḥ (Hiphil: to drive out) marks this as a victory claim embedded in the genealogy.
8.14: Ahio, Shashak, and Jeremoth are listed without sub-grouping here — their family attribution comes in the following verses. The name Ahio (ʾaḥyô, ‘his brother’ or ‘YHWH is my brother’) is the same name as one of Abinadab’s sons who accompanied the ark-cart (2 Sam 6:3–4); the Benjaminite context here differs from the Gittite-Judahite context there.
8.15: Zebadiah, Arad, and Eder are three more Benjaminite heads. Arad (ʿărāḏ) is best known as the Canaanite city in the Negev (Num 21:1; Josh 12:14); its use as a personal name may reflect family tradition connected to that region, or it may be an entirely independent naming. The multiplicity of names without stories in these lists reflects the reality of genealogical records: most ancestors leave only a name, not a narrative.
8.16: Michael, Ishpah, and Joha are identified as “sons of Beriah” (connecting back to v13). Michael (mîkāʾēl, ‘who is like God?’) is the most theologically freighted personal name in this list; the rhetorical question embedded in the name is a devotional formula asserting YHWH’s incomparability (cf. Mic 7:18, mî ʾēl kāmôkā, ‘who is a God like you?’). The name appears for multiple individuals in Chronicles.
8.17: Zebadiah, Meshullam, Hizki, and Heber are “sons of Elpaal.” Meshullam (mᵉšullām, ‘rewarded/repaid’ or ‘at peace with God’) is one of the most common names in Ezra-Nehemiah, reflecting its post-exilic popularity; its appearance in this pre-exilic Benjaminite list shows the name had an older history. Hizki (ḥizqî, ‘my strength’ or ‘YHWH is strength’) is a shortened form of Hezekiah.
8.18: Ishmerai, Izliah, and Jobab close the sons of Elpaal. This section (vv17–18) provides nine sons for Elpaal across two verses, consistent with the Benjaminite pattern of large family clusters. Jobab appears here for the second time in ch8 (also v9), confirming its currency as a Benjaminite name at multiple genealogical levels.
8.19: Jakim, Zichri, and Zabdi are “sons of Shimei.” This Shimei is not identified further; multiple Shimeis appear in Chronicles and the surrounding biblical narrative (most famously David’s curser, 2 Sam 16:5–13). The Benjaminite Shimei sub-clans here have no direct narrative connection to the famous Shimei. Zichri (ziḵrî, ‘my memorial’ or from zāḵar, ‘to remember’) is a name asserting the parent’s hope that the child will be remembered — ironically appropriate for a genealogical register.
8.20: Elienai, Zillethai, and Eliel are the second trio of Shimei’s sons. Elienai (‘my eyes are toward God’) and Eliel (‘God is my God’ or ‘God, O God’) are theophoric names expressing devotion to El/God. The density of ʾēl-element names in this section (Michael v16, Elienai, Eliel) reflects a post-exilic naming fashion that the Chronicler has recorded without comment.
8.21: Adaiah, Beraiah, and Shimrath close Shimei’s sons. Adaiah (ʿăḏāyāh, ‘YHWH has adorned/testified’) is a priestly name common in Ezra-Nehemiah (Ezra 10:29, 39; Neh 11:5, 12). Its appearance here in a Benjaminite warrior genealogy shows that names popular in the Levitical-priestly sphere also circulated in the lay tribal register. Shimrath (šimrāt, ‘guarding’) uses the same root as Shemer/Shamer, recurring Benjaminite-Asherite names.
8.22: Ishpan, Eber, and Eliel are sons of Shashak. Eber (ʿēḇer, the ancestral eponym of the Hebrews in Gen 10:21–25) appears here as a Benjaminite personal name; the same name occurs in Elpaal’s list (v12). The repetition within ch8 of common names (Eber, Gera, Zichri, Eliel) across different sub-clans reflects either actual naming practice or the possibility that similar names in the sources were assigned to different branches in the compilation.
8.23: Abdon, Zichri, and Hanan — sons of Shashak (continuing the list from v22). Abdon is a judge’s name (Judg 12:13–15) and a Levitical city in Asher (Josh 21:30); its appearance as a Benjaminite sub-clan head underscores that judge-names circulated across tribal boundaries. Hanan (ḥānān, ‘gracious’) is a common shortened form of various theophoric names (Hannah, Hananiah).
8.24: Hananiah, Elam, and Antothijah close Shashak’s eleven sons (vv22–25). Hananiah (ḥananyāh, ‘YHWH is gracious’) is one of the most common theophoric names in the Hebrew Bible and appears prominently in the Babylonian captivity narrative (the Hebrew name of Shadrach, Dan 1:6). Antothijah (‘answer of YHWH’ or ‘supplications of YHWH’) is a unique name in the Bible, possibly reflecting a family tradition of naming a child born in response to prayer.
8.25: Iphedeiah and Penuel close Shashak’s sub-list. Penuel (pᵉnûʾēl, ‘face of God’) is the name Jacob gave to the site of his wrestling match with the angel (Gen 32:30); its use as a personal name takes the theophanic place-name into the private sphere. The two-name sub-list marks the end of Shashak’s documented line, with eleven sons recorded in total across vv22–25.
8.26: Shamsherai, Shehariah, and Athaliah are sons of Jeroham. Athaliah (ʿatalyhāh) is best known as the name of the queen who seized Judahite power after Ahaziah’s death (2 Kgs 11). Its appearance here as a masculine Benjaminite name is striking: the name was evidently gender-neutral, and the queen of Judah was named after a Benjaminite male ancestor. The name may derive from a root meaning ‘YHWH is exalted’ or ‘afflicted of YHWH.’
8.27: Jaareshiah, Elijah, and Zichri close Jeroham’s line. Elijah (ʾēliyyāh, ‘YHWH is my God’) is the same name as the Tishbite prophet (1 Kgs 17:1). Its use as a Benjaminite personal name confirms that the name predated the famous prophet and was not an honorific coined for him. The three-name closing formula for Jeroham’s sons echoes the three-name pattern used throughout this section of ch8.
8.28: “These were heads of ancestral houses throughout their generations — leading men who lived in Jerusalem.” The summary verse bridges the genealogical register to its geographic implication: these Benjaminite families are the established Jerusalem residents. The post-exilic context gives this note urgency: who has the legitimate claim to live in Jerusalem? The Chronicler answers by tracing Benjaminite Jerusalem residence to deep genealogical roots. Neh 11:7–9 provides a parallel list of Benjaminites in Jerusalem, confirming this demographic claim.
8.29: “The father of Gibeon (ʾăḇî giḇʿôn) lived at Gibeon; his wife’s name was Maachah.” The formula “father of [city-name]” designates the clan-founder whose descendants settled and controlled that city. Gibeon is the Benjaminite town famous for the covenant with Joshua (Josh 9), the Gibeonite massacre under Saul (2 Sam 21:1–9), and Solomon’s dream (1 Kgs 3:4–15). Its connection to the Saul genealogy that follows is direct: Saul’s family was rooted in Gibeon, which explains Saul’s culpability in the Gibeonite massacre.
8.30: Gibeon’s patriarch’s firstborn son was Abdon, followed by Zur, Kish, Baal, and Nadab. Kish will become Saul’s father (v33) — here Kish is listed third among five brothers, establishing the modesty of Saul’s pedigree (not from the firstborn line). Baal as a personal name in a YHWH-worshipping Israelite family poses the same interpretive challenge as Eshbaal in v33: the word baʿal means ‘master/lord’ and was not necessarily polytheistic in early Israelite usage (cf. Hos 2:16–17).
8.31: Gedor, Ahio, and Zacher are additional sons of the Gibeon patriarch. Gedor as a personal name echoes the Judahite place-name Gedor (4:4, 18); again, geographic and personal names overlap. Ahio appears in 2 Sam 6:3–4 as one of the men who drove the ark-cart from Kirjath-jearim; whether this is the same Ahio or a coincidence of name is uncertain. Zacher is a shortened form of Zechariah (zᵉḵaryāh, ‘YHWH remembers’).
8.32: Mikloth fathered Shimeah (šimʿāh, ‘YHWH has heard’); both “also lived in Jerusalem, across from their kinsmen.” The note of dual residence (Gibeon for the main family; Jerusalem for the Mikloth-Shimeah branch) reflects the reality that aristocratic families in tribal societies maintained households in multiple locations. The phrase “across from their kinsmen” (negeḏ ʾᵃḥêhem) suggests proximity without subordination: a branch family maintaining its identity in Jerusalem while acknowledging connection to the Gibeonite parent clan.
8.34: Jonathan’s son was Meribbaal (mᵉrîḇ baʿal, ‘Baal contends’ or ‘Baal pleads [the cause]’); Meribbaal fathered Micah. The parallel in 2 Sam 9 and 2 Sam 4:4 uses the Samuel editor’s substituted form Mephibosheth (mᵉpîḇōšet). Micah, Jonathan’s grandson, receives an extended genealogy in vv35–38, confirming that the Saul-Jonathan line continued into the post-monarchic period as a recognized Benjaminite family. The Chronicler’s interest in preserving this line may reflect a post-exilic Saulide community that appealed to it for tribal standing.
8.35: Micah’s four sons: Pithon, Melech, Tarea, and Ahaz. The naming of Micah’s son Ahaz is notable: Ahaz is the Davidic king who later brought Judah into crisis by his Assyrian alliances and altar-substitution (2 Chr 28). The Benjaminite Ahaz here precedes the Davidic Ahaz chronologically and is a different individual; the coincidence of names across tribal lines underscores that personal names in this period were not uniquely assigned.
8.36: Ahaz → Jehoadah → Alemeth, Azmaveth, Zimri; Zimri → Moza. Azmaveth (ʿazmāwet, ‘death is strong’ or ‘strong as death’) appears as a warrior in David’s army (2 Sam 23:31) and as a place-name near Jerusalem (Neh 12:29); the name’s military-mortuary meaning reflects the warrior ethos of the genealogy. Zimri is the name of the notorious Israelite king who killed Baasha’s line and reigned for seven days (1 Kgs 16:9–20); the name was pre-existent as a personal name, not coined for the king.
8.37: Moza → Binea → Rapha → Eleasah → Azel. The deep genealogy of the Meribbaal-Micah line (six additional generations after Jonathan) anchors the Saulide family in a continuous genealogical record stretching from the Gibeon patriarch (v29) through Saul’s grandchildren and beyond. The Chronicler’s interest in this detail reflects either a living Saulide community in the post-exilic period or an archival fidelity to complete whatever genealogical sources he had.
8.38: Azel’s six sons: Azrikam, Bocheru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan. The six sons “all sons of Azel” is an emphatic closing formula asserting that all six are legitimate, full sons and not step-children or servants. Obadiah (ʿōḇaḏyāh, ‘servant of YHWH’) and Ishmael (yišmāʿēʾl, ‘God hears’) are theophoric names attesting continued YHWH-devotion in the Saulide family well after the dynasty’s end.
8.39: Azel’s brother Eshek has three sons: Ulam (firstborn), Jeush (second), Eliphelet (third). The ordinal markers (firstborn, second, third) appear here and at the start of the chapter (v1–2), framing the whole with ordinal register at both ends. Eliphelet (ʾelyepelet, ‘God is deliverance/escape’) is a common name among David’s sons (2 Sam 5:16) and warriors (2 Sam 23:34), suggesting its currency across social levels in Benjaminite-Judahite society.
8.40: Ulam’s sons are “mighty warriors, skilled archers (dōrᵉkê qešet), with many sons and grandsons — one hundred and fifty in all.” Benjamin was the tribe of the skilled bowman (Judg 20:16: 700 left-handed slingers; 1 Chr 12:2: Benjaminite ambidextrous archers who joined David). The final note “all these were of the sons of Benjamin” closes ch8 and the entire two-chapter tribal survey (ch7–8) by re-anchoring the extended Saulide genealogy in its tribal identity. The closing formula “one hundred and fifty” descendants for Ulam alone demonstrates the Saulide family’s demographic vitality into the post-exilic period.

4.1: The genealogy resumes the sons of Judah from a different entry point than ch2 — Perez, Hezron, Carmi, Hur, and Shobal. The formula here is bᵉnê Yᵉhûdāh (sons of Judah), the standard opening for a new genealogical unit. The five names bridge the main Judahite lines descended from ch2, grouping together diverse sub-clans under the Judahite heading.
4.2: The genealogical verb hôlîd (hiphil of yālad) means “caused to beget” or “fathered” — the standard causative form for genealogical chains. The MKT renders it “fathered” throughout, preserving the Hebrew's emphasis on the active role of the father in the chain rather than the more neutral English “was the father of.”
4.3: The formula ʾăbî (father of) appears before place names throughout this chapter — ʾăbî Ētām, “father of Etam.” In genealogical contexts, ʾāḇ before a place name connotes “founder” or “settler” of that location. The MKT renders this “founder of” to capture the sense that these individuals established settlements, not merely that they had children named after towns.
4.4: Pᵉnûʾēl ʾăbî Gᵉdōr wᵉʿEzer ʾăbî Ḥûšāh — two founder-formulas in parallel. Pᵉnûʾēl may carry the resonance of the Jabbok crossing (Gen 32:30, “face of God”), though here it is a personal name. Ḥûšāh likely corresponds to the town from which Hushai the Archite came (2 Sam 15:32). The closing attribution “sons of Hur, firstborn of Ephrathah, father of Bethlehem” identifies this line's connection to Bethlehem through Hur.
4.5: Ašḥûr ʾăbî Tᵉqôaʿ — founder of Tekoa. Tekoa was the home of Amos the prophet (Amos 1:1) and the wise woman summoned by Joab (2 Sam 14:2). The two wives, Ḥelāh and Naʿărāh, introduce a brief chiastic genealogy: Naarah's sons in v6, Helah's in v7.
4.6: The sons of Naarah are listed first, then Helah's (v7) — the order reverses normal birth-order priority, perhaps reflecting a significance judgment by the Chronicler. The names are otherwise unattested and carry no clear etymology relevant to the commentary; the list preserves clan identity records that the post-exilic community needed for land and inheritance disputes.
4.7: The sons of Helah close the Ashhur sub-unit. Yiṣhar (from yāṣhar, “to shine/be bright”) appears also as a Levitical name in Exod 6:18. The Chronicler includes both branches of Ashhur's family, giving equal genealogical standing to both wives' descendants.
4.8: Qôṣ hôlîd ʾet-ʿAnûb — Koz fathered Anub. Qôṣ (thorn) is a name that may reflect the topography of the clan's territory. Zobebah (שֹׁובֵבָה, from šûḇ, “to return”) appears to be a personal or clan name unique to this verse. The formula mišpᵉḥôt ʾAḥarḥēl (clans of Aharhel) indicates the Chronicler is working from clan-registry sources in addition to personal genealogies.
4.9: Yaʿbēṣ (Jabez) is explicitly glossed by the narrator: ʾāmᵉrāh ʾimmô qārāʾtî šᵉmô yaʿbēṣ lēʾmōr kî yāladtî bᵉʿōṣeḇ — “his mother named him Jabez, saying, 'I bore him in pain/sorrow.'” The name yaʿbēṣ inverts the letters of ʿōṣeḇ (sorrow/pain) in a folk etymology. The Chronicler introduces Jabez mid-genealogy as “more honoured than his brothers” (niḵbad miʾaḥāyw) — a signal to the reader that an exceptional figure follows.
4.10: The prayer of Jabez is dense with literary technique. ʾim-bārēḵ tᵉḇārᵉḵēnî — the infinitive absolute (bārēḵ) before the finite verb (tᵉḇārᵉḵēnî) intensifies the plea: “if you would indeed bless me.” wᵉhirbîtā ʾet-gᵉḇûlî — “enlarge my territory,” using the hiphil of rāḇāh (to be many/great). The prayer closes with lᵉḇiltî ʿoṣbî — “that I might not have pain,” returning to the ʿōṣeḇ root of his name. The prayer is thus a lifelong response to the sorrow of his birth: he asks God to write a different story than his name predicted.
4.11: Kᵉlûḇ ʾăḥî Šûḥāh hôlîd ʾet-Mᵉḥîr — the formula is straightforward. Kᵉlûḇ is a variant spelling of Kālēḇ (Caleb), which may or may not be the same figure as the spy of Numbers 13. The genealogical chain here uses the standard hôlîd causative, tracking through Mechir to Eshton in v12.
4.12: Ešṭōn hôlîd ʾet-Bêt-rāpāʾ — Eshton fathered Beth-rapha (“house of healing/giant”). Ir-Naḥāš (city of serpent/bronze) is otherwise unknown geographically but likely a Judahite settlement. ʾanšê Rēkāh (men of Recah) indicates the Chronicler is again working from guild or clan-settlement records whose names have not survived elsewhere.
4.13: bᵉnê Qᵉnaz — Kenaz was an Edomite clan chief (Gen 36:11) who became assimilated into Judah, so Othniel the Kenizzite (Judg 1:13; 3:9-11), Israel's first judge, descended from a non-Israelite ancestor. The name Othniel is possibly from ʿōz (strength) + ʾēl (God). The reference here is brief; the fuller picture comes from Judges.
4.14: Gêʾ-Ḥărāšîm — “Valley of Craftsmen,” from ḥārāš (craftsman, artisan, metalworker). The phrase kî ḥărāšîm hāyû (“for they were craftsmen”) explains the place name — a settlement of artisans in the service of the Judahite kingdom, perhaps supplying military equipment. This is one of the Chronicler's incidental socioeconomic footnotes preserved nowhere else.
4.15: bᵉnê Kālēḇ ben-Yᵉphunneh — Caleb son of Jephunneh, the spy who gave a positive report (Num 13:30; 14:6-9). This Caleb is distinct from the Calebites of v11-12 who may or may not be the same genealogical line. The name Elah (אֵלָה, terebinth tree) is a common theophoric/nature name in the HB.
4.16: The sons of Jehallelel: four names, none of whom appear elsewhere. The genealogical function is territorial — recording which sub-clans held Judahite land in the Chronicler's administrative framework. The formula bᵉnê X without the hôlîd chain indicates a horizontal listing of clan members rather than a vertical father-son chain.
4.17: The genealogy of Ezrah introduces Miriam — a female name notable in this context. The Chronicler occasionally inserts women's names into otherwise male genealogies when they were clan founders or significant mothers (cf. Shelomith in 2 Chr 11:20). Mered (rebellion) appears to have taken two wives: an Egyptian wife (v18) and a Jewish wife (v18b), giving him two distinct lines of descendants.
4.18: ʾiššātô hayᵉhûdîyyāh — “his Jewish wife” — the explicit ethnic marker “Judahite/Jewish” (yᵉhûdîyyāh) is needed because Mered had an Egyptian wife (named Bithiah/Bityah in v18, “daughter of Yah”). The name Bithiah is possibly a Hebraized form honoring a pious Egyptian woman, or it may reflect an Israelite name given at conversion — the daughter of Pharaoh identified in Exod 2:5-10 is sometimes connected with this Bithiah in rabbinic tradition.
4.19: The sons of Hodiah's wife, “sister of Naham” — Hodiah married a woman whose clan identity comes through her brother, not her father. Kᵉîlāh haGarmî (Keilah the Garmite settlement) — Garmî is of uncertain origin; possibly connected to gerem (bone/substance) as a place name. Eštemoa hamaʿăḵātî — Eshtemoa the Maachathite — the Maachathites were a people east of the Jordan (Deut 3:14), so this entry records mixed-ancestry clans within Judah.
4.20: Sons of Shimon and Ishi: a brief four-name and two-name list. Ben-Ḥānān (son of gracious one) and Ben-Zōḥēt (son of Zoheth) use ben (son of) as part of the actual name — these are not genealogical formulas but hypocoristic names that incorporate ben as a prefix, similar to Benjamin.
4.21: bᵉnê Šēlāh ben-Yᵉhûdāh — Shelah was Judah's third son (Gen 38:5), whose line was passed over at Tamar's double deception to allow Perez and Zerah to take precedence. The inclusion here rehabilitates Shelah's descendants as legitimate Judahites. Bêt-ʿăḇodāt-bûṣ — “house of linen workers” or “house of the guild of linen workers.” The ʿăḇôdāh (service/work) term applied to a textile guild suggests organized craft production, perhaps in royal service.
4.22: This verse contains a textual difficulty: wᵉyāšuḇî Lehem — the MT reads “and they returned to Lehem (Bethlehem).” The LXX reads differently; some scholars emend to a place name, others retain a verb meaning “returned.” The note “the records are ancient” (wᵉhaddᵉḇārîm ʿattîqîm) is the Chronicler's own acknowledgment that his sources for this material are antiquarian — he is working from old clan registers, not living memory.
4.23: hāyôṣᵉrîm — “the potters” (from yāṣar, to form/shape, the same root used for YHWH forming man in Gen 2:7). The phrase yōšᵉḇê-šām ʿim-hammelek bimlāʾktô — “they dwelt there in the king's service” — indicates royal workshops. The Chronicler preserves evidence of royal craft guilds operating under patronage from a Judahite king, a practice well-attested in ANE administrative texts.
4.24: bᵉnê Šimʿôn — the shift to Simeon's genealogy is significant. Simeon received no separate tribal territory in Canaan but was absorbed within Judah's allotment (Josh 19:1-9). The Chronicler's inclusion of Simeon validates their continued existence as a tribe even within Judah's geographic territory. The five names here differ slightly from Gen 46:10 and Num 26:12-14 — normal variation in genealogical transmission.
4.25: The tôlᵉdôt chain for Simeon (Shallum, Mibsam, Mishma) uses the standard linear formula: bᵉnô X bᵉnô Y — “his son X, his son Y” — indicating a direct father-to-son chain rather than the sibling-list of v24. Mibsam also appears as an Ishmaelite name (Gen 25:13), suggesting a possible tribal overlap or shared naming tradition.
4.26: The chain continues through Mishma's descendants. bᵉnê Mišmāʿ — sons of Mishma — then lists three consecutive bᵉnô links: Hamuel, Zaccur, Shimei. This is the deepest Simeonite genealogy preserved in the HB and likely drawn from clan records maintained through the Assyrian and Babylonian periods.
4.27: Šimʿî šiššāh-ʿāśār bānîm wᵉšēš bānôt — Shimei had sixteen sons and six daughters. The unusual female count (bānôt = daughters) is recorded here and again stands out in the genealogical framework. The theological note follows: “his brothers did not multiply greatly, and their whole clan did not equal the sons of Judah.” The comparison to Judah frames the Simeonite genealogy as an explanation for Simeon's absorption into Judah — smaller population means less territorial claim.
4.28: The settlement list for Simeon: Beer-sheba, Moladah, Hazar-shual — cities at the southern edge of Judah's territory, in the Negev. Beer-sheba was the southernmost city of the traditional settlement (“from Dan to Beer-sheba”). The phrase establishes the Simeonites as desert-edge settlers rather than highland farmers like Judah.
4.29: Bilhah, Ezem, Tolad — southern Negev settlements listed in Josh 15:29-30 and 19:3-4 as Judahite and Simeonite towns respectively. The Chronicler's list slightly diverges from Joshua in names and spelling, reflecting either textual transmission variation or different administrative periods of settlement.
4.30: Bethuel, Hormah, Ziklag — Hormah (ḥormāh, “devoted to destruction”) was where Israel was routed after the failed Kadesh incursion (Num 14:45; 21:3). Ziklag became David's base during his time with the Philistines (1 Sam 27:6; 30:1). The Chronicler's inclusion of Ziklag in the Simeonite list establishes the tribal claim that David later inherited by grant.
4.31: ʿad-mᵉlōk Dāwîd — “until the time of David” — a chronological marker establishing the antiquity of these settlement records. The Chronicler is saying that these were the Simeonite cities prior to the Davidic reorganization of tribal territories. The formula “these were their cities” closes the settlement list formally.
4.32: Five ḥăṣērôt (villages/enclosures) are listed. The Hebrew ḥāṣēr (pl. ḥăṣērôt) typically denotes unwalled settlements — hamlets or encampments — in contrast to fortified ʿārîm (cities). The Simeonite settlements were thus smaller, unwalled communities in the semi-arid southern zone.
4.33: ûmišpaḥōtām (and their genealogical records) — the formal close of the Simeonite list with the phrase “they maintained their genealogical records” (yiḥaśśᵉmû lāhem). This is the Chronicler's notation that these clan lists come from official genealogical registers (yaḥas, genealogy/registration), not oral tradition.
4.34: A new list begins with Meshobab and eleven additional Simeonite leaders. The transition from settlement list to leader list reflects different source materials: vv28-33 came from a geographic-administrative record; vv34-43 come from a clan-leadership register. The leaders' names are given with patronymics to several generations depth, indicating their importance.
4.35: wᵉYôʾēl wᵉYēhûʾ ben-Yôšiḇyāh ben-Śᵉrāyāh ben-ʿĂśîʾēl — a four-generation patronymic for Jehu, establishing his distinguished ancestry within the Simeonite leadership. The depth of genealogy (four generations) signals significant status; ordinary clan members warranted only one or two generations of identification.
4.36: Seven more names in the Simeonite leadership roster. ʾĔlyôʿênay (my eyes are toward YHWH) is a theophoric name reflecting post-exilic piety; its presence in this list suggests the register was updated or supplemented during the restoration period when such names became common.
4.37: Zîzāʾ ben-Šipî ben-ʾAllôn ben-Yᵉdāyāh ben-Šimrî ben-Šᵉmaʿyāh — six-generation patronymic for Ziza, the deepest in this section. Six generations of tracing suggests either an exceptionally prominent ancestor or a clan that took great pride in establishing its genealogical credentials in the post-exilic period.
4.38: habbāʾîm bᵉšēmôt — “those listed by name” — a formal designation for officially registered clan heads. The phrase wᵉnāśîʾîm bᵉmišpᵉḥōtām (leaders in their clans) establishes their administrative role. wᵉḇêt ʾăḇōtêhem pāraṣ lārōḇ — their families multiplied greatly — the expansion of Simeon in the following verses is presented as blessing (the verb pāraṣ, “to burst forth/spread,” is used of rapid growth, as in Gen 28:14 for Jacob's descendants).
4.39: wayyēlᵉkû lᵉmôḇôʾ Gᵉdōr — “they journeyed to the entrance of Gedor.” The location is uncertain — possibly Gerar (reading with LXX) in Philistine territory. lirʿôt migrᵉhem — “to find pasture for their flocks” — the motive is pastoral expansion, not military aggression. The Simeonite movement is framed as a search for grazing land, not conquest.
4.40: wayyimṣᵉʾû mirʿeh šāmēn wāṭôḇ — “they found rich, good pasture” — the language echoes the description of the Promised Land (Deut 8:7-9). šeqeṭet wĕšalᵉwāh — “peaceful and quiet” — the two adjectives together describe ideal pastoral conditions. miḥām (Ham) as the former inhabitants signals Canaanite origin; the displacement of Ham's descendants for Israel's tribes echoes the original conquest narrative.
4.41: hakkᵉtûḇîm bᵉšēmôt — “listed by name” (same formula as v38) — specifying these are the officially registered Simeonite leaders. The campaign is dated to Hezekiah king of Judah, providing a synchronization point with the Judean royal chronology. wayyakkûm (they struck them down) and wayyaḥᵉrîmûm (they devoted them to destruction) — the ḥērem verb (ḥāram in hiphil) applies the holy-war ban to the surviving Canaanite enclaves in the Negev.
4.42: ḥămiššāh meʾôt ʾîš — five hundred men, a smaller raiding party for the Mount Seir campaign. The four leaders (Pelatiah, Neariah, Rephaiah, Uzziel) are named; they are sons of Ishi (cf. v20). Mount Seir was Edomite territory (Gen 32:3; 36:8), suggesting Simeonite expansion eastward as well as southward, filling the vacuum left by Assyrian disruption of established populations.
4.43: wayyakkû ʾet-šᵉʾērît happālēṭāh laʿămālēq — “they struck down the remaining Amalekites who had escaped.” Saul had failed to complete the destruction of Amalek (1 Sam 15); David had driven them back (1 Sam 27:8; 30); now Simeonite clan warriors complete what royal campaigns had not. wayyēšᵉḇû šām ʿad hayyôm hazzeh — “they have lived there to this day” — the narrator's present-time anchor for the Simeonite settlement in Edomite territory.

5.1: hûʾ habᵉḵôr — Reuben was the firstborn (bᵉḵôr), yet the birthright (bᵉḵōrāh) was not his to keep. The indictment is precise: bᵉḥalᵉlô yᵉṣûʿê ʾāḇîw — “when he defiled his father's couch/bed.” ḥillēl (piel of ḥālal) means to profane, desecrate, or defile — the same root as ḥōl (common/profane) as opposed to qādôš (holy). The act (Gen 35:22, sleeping with Bilhah) is not described but its legal consequence is: the bᵉḵōrāh was transferred to Joseph's sons.
5.2: The Chronicler articulates a distinction between two aspects of the firstborn's inheritance: haśśᵉrārāh — the ruler/prince role — went to Judah (nagîd in some translations; here the term śar = prince/chief), while the bᵉḵōrāh (birthright = double inheritance share) went to Joseph. This two-track resolution explains the parallel importance of Judah (the royal tribe) and Joseph (the most numerous tribe) in the later history: neither invalidates the other because they received different dimensions of the firstborn's rights.
5.3: The standard list of Reuben's four sons matches Gen 46:9 and Exod 6:14 closely. Ḥanôḵ, Pallûʾ, Ḥeṣrôn, wᵉKarmî — the genealogical formula resumes after the theological aside of vv1-2. The bᵉnê Rᵉʾûḇên bᵉḵōr Yiśrāʾēl opening restates Reuben's formal position even as the narrative has explained why that position carries no privilege here.
5.4: The chain from Joel continues through eight generations (bᵉnô X — his son X, seven times). The chain uses the possessive suffix construction (bᵉnô = his son) to mark direct father-son descent, distinguishing it from the lateral bᵉnê (sons of) lists. Joel's line is the one that reaches Beerah who went into exile (v6), suggesting Joel's family was the most prominent Reubenite clan in the Assyrian period.
5.5: The chain continues: bᵉnô Mîḵāh bᵉnô Rᵉʾāyāh bᵉnô Baʿal. The name Baʿal in an Israelite genealogy is not unusual in the pre-monarchic and early monarchic period (cf. Eshbaal/Ishbosheth; Merib-baal/Mephibosheth) — baʿal simply meant “lord/master” and was applied to YHWH before the prophets specifically condemned its identification with the Canaanite deity.
5.6: ûḇᵉʾērāh bᵉnô ʾăšer hᵉglāh Tilgat-pilneser melek ʾAššûr — Beerah son of Baal, whom Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria deported. The name Tilgat-pilneser (Tiglatpileser) is a Hebraized form of the Akkadian Tukulti-apil-Ešarra (my trust is in the son of Esharra), reflecting the Assyrian throne name. The Chronicler specifies he was nāśîʾ laRᵉʾûḇēnî — a leader/prince of the Reubenites — explaining why his deportation is historically significant; removing the chief destabilized the whole tribe.
5.7: wᵉʾeḥāyw lᵉmišpᵉḥōtāyw — his kinsmen/relatives, listed by clan. The phrase bᵉhityaḥaśśām lᵉtôlᵉdôtām — “enrolled by genealogy in their generations” — specifies the administrative process: official genealogical registration (hitpael of yāḥas, to enroll by genealogy). Yᵉʾîʾēl hārōʾš wᵉZᵉḵaryāhû — Jeiel the chief (head) and Zechariah, the two senior Reubenite leaders.
5.8: wûḇelaʿ ben-ʿAzaz ben-Šema ben-Yôʾēl — Bela with a three-generation patronymic, connecting him to the same Joel line as Beerah (v6). He “settled in Aroer as far as Nebo and Baal-meon” — Aroer was on the Arnon river (Deut 2:36), Nebo was the mountain from which Moses viewed Canaan (Deut 34:1), and Baal-meon was a Moabite town (Num 32:38). Bela's clan held the core Reubenite territory east of the Jordan.
5.9: wĕlimdᵉmāʿ nāšāʾ midbārāh — “he settled eastward to the edge of the desert”, reaching as far as the Euphrates (hanNāhār = the River, a Hebrew idiom for the Euphrates). kî miknᵉhem rāḇû bᵉʾereṣ Gilʿād — the reason given is the growth of their livestock requiring expanded territory. The Chronicler frames territorial expansion as a natural consequence of blessing (population/livestock growth), not as military aggression.
5.10: ûḇimê Šāʾûl — in the days of Saul: a chronological anchor for the first Reubenite military engagement, the war against the Hagrites (Hagrîʾîm). The Hagrites were a pastoral people of the Syro-Arabian steppe, possibly connected to Hagar/Hagarites (Ps 83:6). wayyipᵉlû bᵉyāḏām — “were defeated” (lit. fell by their hands) — using the standard military defeat idiom. wayyēšᵉḇû bᵉʾohalêhem — they settled in their tents/encampments, occupying the Hagrite territory.
5.11: ûḇᵉnê Gāḏ lᵉnegeḏ šāḵᵉnû — the sons of Gad lived “opposite them” (lit. “facing them”), in Bashan. Bāšān was the fertile northern plateau east of the Sea of Galilee, famous for its cattle (Deut 32:14; Ps 22:12). The Gadites held the southern portion of Transjordan while the Reubenites held the central and northern zones. ʿad Śalkāh — to Salecah, the easternmost limit of Bashan (Deut 3:10), marking the extent of Gadite settlement.
5.12: The Gadite leadership roster: Yôʾēl hārōʾš wĕŠāpām hamišneh — Joel the chief and Shapham the second-in-command. The term hamišneh (the second, the deputy) is an administrative title used throughout Chronicles for the second-ranking officer in a hierarchy (cf. 2 Chr 28:7 for the deputy king). This shows the Chronicler working from administrative rosters, not just genealogical records.
5.13: Seven Gadite clan members listed — “their relatives by family” (ûʾăḥêhem lᵉḇêt ʾăḇōtêhem). The number seven is complete and is perhaps intentional as a formulaic grouping. Miḵāʾēl, Mᵉšullām, Šeḇaʿ, Yôray, Yāḵān, Zîʿāʾ, wᵉʿEḇer šiḇʿāh — seven in all, as the text confirms.
5.14: ʾēlleh bᵉnê ʾĂḇîḥayil ben-Ḥûrî — these are sons of Abihail son of Huri, then traced through six generations: Huri, Jaroah, Gilead, Michael, Jeshishai, Jahdo, and Buz. The depth of genealogy (six ancestral links) establishes the family's antiquity and prominence in the Gadite tribal structure, comparable to the deep Simeonite pedigrees in ch4.
5.15: Aḥî ben-ʿAḇdîʾēl ben-Gûnî rōʾš lᵉḇêt ʾăḇōtām — Ahi son of Abdiel son of Guni, head of their father's house. The three-generation patronymic for Ahi establishes him as the senior Gadite clan head. rōʾš lᵉḇêt ʾăḇōtām (head of the father's house) is the standard administrative term for the leading patriarch of an extended family unit (bêt ʾāḇ).
5.16: wayyēšᵉḇû bᵉGilʿād bᵉḇāšān ûḇibnōtêhā — they lived in Gilead, in Bashan and its towns (bᵉnôt = daughter-towns, i.e., dependent villages). ûḇᵉkol-migrᵉšê Šārôn ʿal-tôṣᵉʾōtām — and throughout the pasturelands of Sharon to their borders. The Sharon mentioned here is likely not the coastal Sharon plain but a Transjordanian region of the same name (cf. the plural mentions in 2 Chr 4:16 of LXX; Isa 33:9).
5.17: kullām hitya_ḥasᵉsû bîmê Yôtām melek-Yᵉhûdāh ûḇîmê Yārāḇᵉʿām melek-Yiśrāʾēl — all were enrolled by genealogy during the reigns of Jotham king of Judah and Jeroboam king of Israel. This provides a double synchronization: Jotham (southern) and Jeroboam II (northern), both reigning approximately 782–740 BCE. The Chronicler dates his genealogical source to this period — a time of relative prosperity when administrative census-taking was feasible.
5.18: bᵉnê-Rᵉʾûḇên wᵉGāḏ waḥăṣî šēḇeṭ Mᵉnašše — the three Transjordanian tribes act as a military coalition. ʾanšê ḥayil (men of valor/strength) — the standard Chronicler term for capable warriors. The description of military capability is detailed: they carry shield and sword, draw the bow, and are trained in war (lāmᵉdê milḥāmāh). The census-like enumeration (“forty-four thousand seven hundred and sixty, ready for service”) reflects the administrative precision of a military muster roll.
5.19: wayyilaḥămû ʿim-haHagrîʾîm wᵉYᵉṭûr wᵉNāpîš wᵉNôḏāḇ — the coalition fought against the Hagrites, Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab. Jetur and Naphish are sons of Ishmael (Gen 25:15; 1 Chr 1:31), identifying the enemies as Ishmaelite desert tribes. Nodab is otherwise unknown. The alliance of Ishmaelite and Hagrite (possibly descendants of Hagar) groups represents the primary pastoral-nomadic competition for the Transjordanian steppe.
5.20: wayyēʿāzᵉrû ʿălêhem — “they were helped against them” — the niphal of ʿāzar (to help), implying divine assistance. The reason is explicit: kî ḥāʿăqû ʾel-hāʾĕlōhîm bammilḥāmāh — “for they cried out to God in the battle.” wayyēʿāter lāhem — “and he granted their plea” — the niphal of ʿātar (to pray/entreat), meaning to be entreated, to respond to prayer. The causal chain is theological: prayer → divine response → victory.
5.21: The spoil is enumerated in detail: 50,000 camels, 250,000 sheep, 2,000 donkeys, 100,000 persons. The numbers follow the convention of large-scale military victory accounts in Chronicles, where enumeration signals completeness of triumph. The comparison with similar totals in tribal conquest narratives (Num 31:32-35) shows the Chronicler drawing on a consistent literary pattern for describing total victory.
5.22: kî mēʾĕlōhîm hammilḥāmāh — “for the war was from God” — a theological attribution formula distinctive to the Chronicler. In parallel passages in Samuel-Kings, divine causality is present but often less explicit; Chronicles surfaces it as the primary explanation. wayyēšᵉḇû taḥtêhem — “they settled in their territory” — occupying the Hagrite steppe lands until the exile (haggôlāh), which ended the Transjordanian settlements.
5.23: ûḇᵉnê ḥăṣî šēḇeṭ Mᵉnaššeh — the half-tribe of Manasseh, the third Transjordanian tribe. mippārān ʿad Baʿal-ḥermôn wĕŠᵉnîr wĕhar Ḥermôn — from Bashan northward to Baal-hermon, Senir, and Mount Hermon. Hermon (also called Senir by the Amorites, Deut 3:9) was the northern limit of territory taken from Og of Bashan, establishing the extent of Manassite control.
5.24: Seven Manassite clan heads listed as ʾanšê ḥayil ʾanšê šēmōt — “men of valor, men of renown” — a double honor title that echoes the description of David's mighty men (ch11). The use of the same heroic vocabulary links the Transjordanian chiefs to the idealized warrior tradition of the Davidic era.
5.25: wayyimʿᵃlû ʾĕlōhê ʾăḇōtêhem — “they were unfaithful to the God of their fathers” — the verb māʿal (to act unfaithfully/treacherously) is the Chronicler's key term for covenant violation that brings divine judgment (used extensively throughout Chronicles; cf. 2 Chr 26:16; 28:19, 22; 29:6; 36:14). wayyiznû — “and prostituted themselves” — zānāh (to commit sexual immorality) in the metaphorical sense of covenant apostasy, following Hosea's influential use of the marriage metaphor for YHWH-Israel relations (Hos 1:2; 4:12).
5.26: wayyāʿer ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl ʾet-rûaḥ Pûl melek-ʾAššûr — “the God of Israel stirred up (ʿûr hiphil) the spirit (rûaḥ) of Pul king of Assyria.” The verb ʿûr (to rouse, stir, incite) is used throughout Chronicles for divine initiative — YHWH rouses kings and peoples to accomplish his purposes (2 Chr 21:16; 36:22-23). Pul is Tiglath-pileser III's throne name used in Babylonian records (2 Kgs 15:19). The identification hûʾ Tilgat-pilneser (that is, Tiglath-pileser) is the Chronicler's own gloss explaining the two names for the same king. wayyiglēm — “he exiled them” — the Reubenites, Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh were deported to Halah, Habor, Hara, and the river Gozan, the standard Assyrian exile destinations (also named in 2 Kgs 17:6 for the northern exile).

13.11: The place-name pereṣ ʿuzzāh (‘Breach of Uzzah’) that David gives to the threshing floor of Chidon encodes a double irony. The name ʿuzzāh derives from the root ʿōz (strength, might) — the man’s name literally means ‘YHWH’s strength’ or simply ‘strength.’ Uzzah is killed at the moment of deploying that strength: reaching out (wayyišlaḥ yādô) to steady the Ark. The verb pāraṣ (to breach, break out, burst forth) — wayyippeṣ bô ʾelōhîm, ‘God broke out against him’ — names the violent, boundary-crossing force of divine holiness when it encounters unauthorized contact. The pereṣ (breach) that kills Uzzah is the Ark asserting its own holiness against human management. The same verb pāraṣ appears in 14:11 (YHWH breaks through David’s enemies = Baal-perazim) and 15:13 (David’s explanation of the Uzzah incident: pāraṣ bānû YHWH, ‘YHWH broke out against us’). The Chronicler deliberately uses this root across all three chapters to create a theological signature: pāraṣ is YHWH’s characteristic verb — he breaks boundaries, whether against enemies (14:11) or against unauthorized approach (13:11). The Ark-cart episode is not merely a liturgical error; it is a failure to reckon with the pāraṣ-character of YHWH’s holiness.
13.1: David’s consultation with the commanders before any action is the Chronicler’s signature editorial addition to the Samuel source (2 Sam 6:1 simply says David gathered thirty thousand). The deliberative assembly — leaders of thousands and hundreds, then “every leader” (kol nāśîʾ) — frames the ark-retrieval as a national covenantal act requiring unanimous consent, not a unilateral royal initiative. The Chronicler consistently portrays legitimate Davidic action as communally ratified.
13.2: David’s speech to the assembly introduces the conditional double-clause: ʾim ʿālêḵem ṭôḇ ûmin-YHWH ʾĕlōhênû (‘if it seems good to you, and if it is of the LORD our God’). Both human assent and divine warrant are required. The formulation reflects the Chronicler’s political theology: legitimate religious action needs both popular consensus and covenantal authorization. David’s appeal to “our brethren remaining in all the territories of Israel” is another Chronicler addition, emphasizing the all-Israel character of the project.
13.3: The charge to retrieve the ark is grounded in a critique of Saul’s reign: “for we did not inquire of it (lōʾ ḏᵉrašnûhû) during Saul’s days.” The verb dārash (to seek, inquire of) is the Chronicler’s most characteristic theological verb: seeking YHWH through his appointed means (ark, temple, prayer) is the mark of a faithful king, and its absence is the diagnostic of Saul’s failure. The Chronicler will return to this failure explicitly at Saul’s death (10:13–14: “Saul died because he was unfaithful to the LORD… and he did not inquire (dāraš) of the LORD”).
13.4: “All the assembly agreed to do so, for the thing seemed right to all the people.” The unanimous affirmation (“all” appearing twice) is the Chronicler’s signal of an approved covenantal act. Compare the Solomonic temple dedication (2 Chr 5:3, 6; 7:8) where “all Israel” similarly ratifies the sanctuary act. Unanimous assemblies in Chronicles mark moments when human decision aligns with divine will.
13.5: David assembles Israel from Shihor of Egypt to the entrance of Hamath — the ideal geographical extent of the land of promise (Num 34:5, 8; Josh 13:3; 1 Kgs 8:65). The Chronicler is using the outer boundary formula to signal that the ark-retrieval is a defining national moment requiring the full breadth of covenant Israel. Samuel’s parallel simply says “thirty thousand chosen men of Israel.” The Chronicler’s scope is theological, not military.
13.6: Baalah is given as an alternative name for Kiriath-jearim (‘city of forests’). The Baalah name (from baʿal, ‘lord/mistress’; feminine form = ‘the mistress’) reflects the site’s Canaanite prehistory; Josh 15:9–10 uses both names interchangeably. The ark had been at Kiriath-jearim for twenty years (1 Sam 7:2), hosted in the house of Abinadab. The destination is named in full: “to bring up from there the ark of God, the LORD who is enthroned above the cherubim, upon whom his name is called.” The throne-above-cherubim formula (cf. 1 Sam 4:4; 2 Sam 6:2; Ps 80:1) is the ark’s highest theological description.
13.7: The fatal error: the ark is placed on a “new cart” (ʿăgālāh ḥᵃḏāšāh). This was the Philistine method of returning the ark after their capture (1 Sam 6:7–8), not the Mosaic ordinance. Num 7:9 specifies that the Kohathite Levites must carry the most holy things “on their shoulders” (bakkātēp); the ark was explicitly excluded from ox-cart transport. David adopts the Philistine technological solution rather than the Mosaic liturgical ordinance, and the consequences follow immediately. Ch15:13–15 (already in the commentary) explains the principle David eventually articulates.
13.8: The celebratory procession with “all their might” (bᵉḵol ʿōz) and the full ensemble of instruments — songs, harps (kinnōrôt), lyres (nᵉḇālîm), tambourines (tuppîm), cymbals (mṣiltayim), and trumpets (ḥăṣōṣᵉrôt) — is the Chronicler’s expanded version of Samuel’s similar but shorter list. The musical catalogue anticipates the full Levitical orchestra that will be formally organized in ch15–16 for the successful ark-transport. The exuberant worship before the ark is not wrong; the method of carrying is.
13.9: The threshing floor of Chidon (kîḏôn) is named Nachon’s threshing floor in the Samuel parallel (2 Sam 6:6). The variant may reflect different regional traditions or textual transmission. The verb for the oxen’s stumbling (šāmaṭ, ‘to let fall/shake loose’) is rare; it occurs only here and in 2 Sam 6:6. Uzzah’s response is instinctive: he “reached out his hand” (wayyišlaḥ yadô) to steady the ark — the same verb used for the forbidden extending of hands toward holy objects (Exod 19:13; cf. 2 Sam 6:6–7).
13.10: YHWH’s anger kindles against Uzzah (wayyiḥar ʾap YHWH bᵉʿuzzāh) and he strikes him down. The narrative offers the explanation “because he put his hand to the ark” — but the deeper cause, which David later articulates (15:13), is the improper mode of transport. The immediate cause is Uzzah’s touch; the structural cause is the violated ordinance. The tension between these two levels of causation is part of what makes the episode theologically difficult: Uzzah’s instinct was protective, but it violated the boundary. The holiness of the ark was not suspended by good intentions.
13.12: David’s fear (wayyîrāʾ dāwîḏ ʾet hāʾĕlōhîm bayyôm hahûʾ) marks the shift from anger (v11) to reverent awe. The fear of God (yirʾat ʾĕlōhîm) is the appropriate response to the holiness-encounter; anger is the first human response, fear the subsequent and correct one. The question “How can I bring the ark of God to me?” (êḵ ʾāḇîʾ ʾēlî ʾēt ʾărôn hāʾĕlōhîm) is not answered here but in the entire procedural preparation of ch15. The question is not rhetorical; it demands a cultic answer.
13.13: David diverts the ark to the house of Obed-edom the Gittite (gittî, ‘from Gath’). The name Obed-edom means ‘servant of Edom’ or possibly ‘worshipper of Edom’; the gittî epithet marks him as connected to Gath (a Philistine city), making him either a Levite from Gath-rimmon (a Levitical city, Josh 21:24) or a Gittite who had joined Israel (like Ittai the Gittite in 2 Sam 15:19). The Chronicler later identifies Obed-edom as a Korahite Levite (15:18, 21; 16:38), resolving the ambiguity in favor of Levitical qualification.
13.14: The ark remains three months with Obed-edom, and “the LORD blessed the household (bêt) of Obed-edom and all he had.” The blessing formula echoes the Abrahamic covenant-language (Gen 12:3; 22:17); the ark functions as a portable locus of Abrahamic blessing. The three-month period is significant: in Levitical liturgical practice, three months marks the completion of a quarter; the period of blessing serves as evidence that the ark is not intrinsically dangerous but becomes so only when handled in violation of its ordinances. This blessing motivates David’s decision to complete the transfer (14:1 and following, then ch15).

14.11: David’s name for the site of his victory over the Philistines — baʿal pᵉrāṣîm, ‘Lord of breakings-through/breaches’ — uses the same root pāraṣ that appeared in 13:11 (‘Perez-uzzah’) and will reappear in 15:13. David’s own gloss is: pāraṣ ʾelōhîm ʾet ʾōyᵉḇay bᵉyāḏî kᵉpereṣ mayim, ‘God has broken through my enemies by my hand like a breaking-through of water.’ The image is a flash flood — the pereṣ mayim (water-breach) being the sudden, irresistible bursting of a dam or a wadi in flood. The Philistines’ gods are abandoned on the field (v12) and burned — the decisive reversal from Eben-ezer (1 Sam 4) where the Ark was captured by the Philistines. Now the Ark is safely housed in Jerusalem (ch13 having failed; ch15 will succeed) and YHWH’s pāraṣ falls on the Philistines instead. The Chronicler’s placement of the Baal-perazim victory between the two Ark-procession accounts (ch13: failure; ch15: success) is a theological bracket: the same God who broke out against Uzzah (for inappropriate approach) breaks through the Philistines (for encroachment on Israel). Pāraṣ is not capricious — it is YHWH’s consistent boundary-enforcement, whether the boundary being crossed is ritual or national.
14.1: Hiram king of Tyre sends cedar timber, stonemasons, and carpenters to build David’s palace. The Chronicler places this episode immediately after the Obed-edom interlude (ch13) and before the successful ark transfer (ch15), creating a three-part sequence: failed holiness-action → divine affirmation through gentile blessing → proper cultic procedure. In Samuel (2 Sam 5:11) this comes before the ark narrative. The Chronicler’s resequencing emphasizes that David’s political consolidation and his cultic restoration develop in parallel.
14.2: David perceives (wayyēḏaʿ) that YHWH has “established him as king over Israel” (hēḵîn ʾōtô YHWH lᵉmelek). The root kûn (to be established, prepared, firm) is one of the Chronicler’s key theological terms: the dynasty is “established” (17:14; 22:10; 28:7); the temple is “prepared for” (22:5). David’s recognition that his kingdom is “exalted on high (hanniśśəʾāh lᵉmaʿlāh) for the sake of his people Israel” frames political success as theocentric: the kingdom is elevated for Israel’s sake, not David’s.
14.3: David takes more wives in Jerusalem and fathers sons and daughters. The Chronicler reproduces this notice from 2 Sam 5:13 without the moralistic qualification found in Deut 17:17 (“he must not take many wives”). The notice functions primarily as a genealogical-historical record. The lack of editorial comment is characteristic of the Chronicler’s method: he records facts about David’s household without theological judgment, reserving such judgments for specifically cultic failures.
14.4: The names of David’s Jerusalem-born sons (Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon) match 2 Sam 5:14. Nathan and Solomon are both significant in the Chronicler’s narrative: Nathan is the prophet who delivers the dynastic oracle (ch17), and Solomon is the designated temple-builder. The inclusion of both in the genealogy establishes their credentials as sons of David born in his royal capital.
14.5: Ibhar, Elishua, and Elpelet — three of David’s sons whose names appear in slightly different forms in the Samuel parallel (2 Sam 5:15 has Elishua and Elpalet; 1 Chr 3:6–8 provides a third list). The name Elishua (ʾelîšûaʿ, ‘God is salvation/wealth’) is theophoric; Elpelet (ʾelpelet, ‘God is deliverance’) occurs twice in the full son-list (3:6, 8), suggesting either two sons with the same name or a textual duplication.
14.6: Nogah, Nepheg, and Japhia. Nogah (nōgāh, ‘brightness/shining’) is unique to the Chronicles list; it does not appear in the parallel 2 Sam 5:15, though it occurs in 1 Chr 3:7. This may reflect the Chronicler having access to a more complete source list than the Samuel tradition preserved.
14.7: Elishama, Beeliada, and Eliphelet. Beeliada (bᵉʿalyāḏāh, ‘Baal knows’ or ‘Baal has known’) has the Baal element as a theophoric component; the Samuel parallel uses Eliada (ʾelyāḏāh, ‘God knows’, 2 Sam 5:16), substituting ʾēl for baʿal. This is the same scribal phenomenon as Eshbaal/Ishbosheth and Merib-baal/Mephibosheth (8:33–34): the Chronicler preserves the original theophoric name, while Samuel substitutes a non-Baal element. That David named a son Beeliada is historically plausible: the name baʿal = ‘lord/master’ was not always polytheistic in early monarchic usage.
14.8: When the Philistines hear of David’s anointment as king over all Israel, they mobilize to search for him (wayḥaqqᵉšûhû). The Philistines recognize that the united monarchy represents a new strategic threat; their pre-emptive mustering is a rational military response. The Chronicler’s note that David “went out against them” frames the king as proactive in his defense, not merely reactive.
14.9: The Philistines spread out (wayyipšṭû) in the Valley of Rephaim. The Rephaim-valley (southwest of Jerusalem) bears the name of the pre-Israelite giant clans; it was associated with Canaanite and later Philistine military activity. The geographic choice of the Rephaim valley invokes the background of the giant-warrior tradition against which Israel’s champions were measured (cf. Goliath of Gath; the Philistine giants of 20:4–8).
14.10: David inquires of God (wayyiš’al dāwîḏ bēʾlōhîm) before the first battle. The šāʾal bě formula (to inquire of God through an oracle) is paired throughout Chronicles with dārash ʾet YHWH (to seek YHWH): together they constitute the full act of seeking divine guidance before royal military action. YHWH responds with a direct promise: “Go up, for I will hand them over to you (ʾettennēm bᵉyāḏeḵā).” The divine assurance before battle is what distinguishes legitimate Israelite warfare from autonomous aggression.
14.12: The Philistines had abandoned their gods (ʾelōhêhem) at Baal-perazim, and David gave the command to burn them with fire (wayyiśśarᵉpûm bāʾēš). This diverges significantly from 2 Sam 5:21: “David and his men carried them away” (wayyiśśāʾēm). The Chronicler has David fulfil the Deuteronomic ordinance (Deut 7:25: “You shall burn the carved images of their gods with fire”) rather than the Samuel-tradition of appropriating them. Whether Samuel’s version reflects an older practice of taking captured cult objects (as trophies or neutralized divine power) or is a simple narrative variant is debated; the Chronicler’s version aligns with Mosaic law.
14.13: The Philistines repeat their campaign in the valley — the same location, a second attempt. The doubling of the battle narrative (vv8–12 and vv13–16) is a literary structure emphasizing that the first victory was not decisive; YHWH’s guidance is needed again for the follow-up. David does not assume that the first oracle covers the second engagement.
14.14: God instructs David not to go straight up (lōʾ taʿăleh ʾaḥᵃrêhem) but to “circle around” (hāsēb) to come at them from the balsam groves. The tactical command is specific and counter-intuitive; a frontal pursuit would be the natural follow-up. The indirect approach requires trusting the oracle over military instinct. Hāsēb (Hiphil of sāḇaḇ, ‘to turn/encircle’) is used of the encirclement of Jericho (Josh 6:3), suggesting a pattern of divinely-specified unconventional tactics.
14.15: “When you hear the sound of marching (qôl haṣṣᵉʾādāh) in the tops of the balsam trees, then go out to battle, for God has gone out before you (yāṣāʾ hāʾĕlōhîm lipnêḵā) to strike the Philistine army.” The ṣᵉʾādāh (march, step) in the treetops is the auditory signal of the divine vanguard. The balsam trees (bᵉkāʾîm, possibly ‘weeping trees’ from bākāh, a resinous tree) served as the signal medium. The formula “God has gone out before you” is the holy-war assurance: YHWH fights in the vanguard, and Israel follows the divine movement into battle.
14.16: David’s obedience (“David did as God commanded him”) produces the decisive result: the Philistines are struck “from Gibeon to Gezer.” The geographic extent — from Gibeon (northwest of Jerusalem) to Gezer (the Shephelah) — describes a southwest trajectory, driving the Philistines from the central highlands back toward the coast. This is the reversal of the Philistine advance of v9; the valley campaign ends with Israelite control of the approach routes to Jerusalem.
14.17: “The fame (šēm) of David went out into all the lands, and the LORD put the fear (paḥad) of him upon all nations.” The divine agency is explicit: it is YHWH who generates the fear among the nations, not David’s military power alone. Paḥad (terror, dread) is the holy-war fear that YHWH sends ahead of his champion (cf. Exod 15:16; Deut 2:25; Ps 105:38). The chapter closes with David’s reputation as a divinely-established phenomenon, setting up the second ark-transport attempt in ch15 from a position of confirmed royal legitimacy.

15.13: David’s retrospective explanation of the Uzzah disaster — kî lōʾ dᵉrašnûhû kammišpāṭ, ‘because we did not seek him according to the ordinance’ — introduces the specific legal failure: the Ark was carried on a cart (ʿāgālāh, 13:7) rather than on poles by Levites (Num 4:15; 7:9). The verb pāraṣ appears again: pāraṣ bānû YHWH ʾelōhênû, ‘YHWH our God broke out against us.’ The Chronicler’s use of pāraṣ in all three chapters (13:11; 14:11; 15:13) is structural: the Uzzah incident (unauthorized pāraṣ) is explained by David in 15:13 using the same vocabulary, with the Baal-perazim victory (authorized pāraṣ) sandwiched between as a theological contrast. The Philistines’ gods were ‘carried away’ (nāśāʾ) and burned (14:12) — the Ark must be ‘carried’ (nāśāʾ) by the Levites (15:2, 15) on poles, not transported like cargo. The linguistic echo is deliberate: the verb nāśāʾ (to carry, lift, bear) governs both episodes, distinguishing the proper bearing of the Ark from the improper transport. The kammišpāṭ (‘according to the ordinance’) that David names as missing is the Mosaic regulation the Ark-cart circumvented — the failure was not ignorance but inattentiveness to the specific form YHWH had ordained.
15.1: David builds houses for himself in the city of David (bāttîm, the plural may indicate a palace complex) and “prepared a place for the ark of God, pitching a tent for it.” The Chronicler’s emphasis on David’s specific preparation of the ark’s site contrasts with the casual new-cart method of ch13. The tent (ʾōhel) anticipates the permanent structure Solomon will build; it is a provisional sanctum that takes the Mosaic tabernacle-form (tent over the ark) as its model. The preparation of a proper dwelling precedes the proper transfer.
15.2: David articulates the principle he had violated: “No one ought to carry the ark of God except the Levites, for the LORD has chosen them to carry the ark of the LORD and to minister before him forever.” The Chronicler adds this theological explanation (absent from Samuel) as the hinge between the failure of ch13 and the success of ch15. The warrant is the divine election of the Levites (bāḥar bāhem YHWH) for this specific task, making the Levitical monopoly on ark-transport a matter of covenantal appointment, not administrative preference.
15.3: David assembles all Israel at Jerusalem to bring up the ark — the same universal-assembly formula as ch13:5 but now properly grounded in cultic preparation. The repetition of the all-Israel assembly signals a fresh start: the previous attempt, though motivated by similar national intent, lacked the proper liturgical form. The assembly this time will be disciplined by the Levitical procedure David is about to outline.
15.4: David brings together the sons of Aaron and the Levites — the combined priestly and Levitical corps. The sons of Aaron (the priests) are named first, establishing the hierarchy: priests supervise, Levites carry. The subsequent verses (5–10) enumerate six Levitical subdivisions, each with its chief and a numerical contingent, giving the transfer the character of a military muster as well as a liturgical procession.
15.5: From the sons of Kohath: Uriel the chief with 120 kinsmen. The Kohathites were the Levitical sub-clan specifically charged with carrying the most holy objects including the ark (Num 3:30–31; 4:15). Their enumeration first in the list signals that they will take the lead role in the ark-transport, now executed correctly according to their Mosaic mandate.
15.6: From the sons of Merari: Asaiah the chief with 220 kinsmen. The Merarites were responsible for the structural frames, bars, and pillars of the tabernacle (Num 3:36–37); in this context they participate in the processional escort. The three major Levitical families (Kohath, Merari, Gershon) each contribute to the ark-transfer, creating a complete Levitical complement for the national procession.
15.7: From the sons of Gershom: Joel the chief with 130 kinsmen. The Gershonites carried the tabernacle coverings and hangings (Num 3:25–26); their inclusion in the ark-procession ensures the full Levitical unity. Joel’s appearance as a Gershomite leader is consistent with the Asaphite tradition (Asaph was a Gershonite, 6:39), and the Joel named here may be the same as Asaph’s father (cf. 6:36).
15.8: From the sons of Elizaphan: Shemaiah the chief with 200 kinsmen. Elizaphan was a Kohathite leader in Numbers (Num 3:30) who is given his own sub-clan registration here by the Chronicler, indicating the Levitical sub-division had grown to warrant separate administrative listing by David’s time. The 200-member contingent is the second largest after Merari, reflecting Elizaphan’s expanded clan.
15.9: From the sons of Hebron: Eliel the chief with 80 kinsmen. The Hebronites were a Kohathite sub-clan (Num 3:27; 26:58) associated with the town of Hebron (David’s first capital); their participation in the Jerusalem ark-procession bridges David’s Hebron and Jerusalem periods. The 80-member cohort is the smallest in this list, but the Hebronite sub-clan will play a role in later administrative assignments (26:30–32).
15.10: From the sons of Uzziel: Amminadab the chief with 112 kinsmen. Uzziel was another Kohathite sub-clan (Num 3:27); Amminadab as Uzzielite leader balances the Kohathite presence (Uriel, Elizaphan, Uzziel all contributing Kohathite sub-divisions). The Levitical census of six subdivisions totals 862 men for the ark-procession — a substantial escort that contrasts with the informal procession of ch13.
15.11: David summons the priests Zadok and Abiathar, and the six Levitical chiefs listed in vv5–10. The dual-priest formula (Zadok and Abiathar) reflects the historical situation of David’s reign where two priestly lines coexisted (Zadok was the Aaronic-Eleazar line; Abiathar was the Eli-Ithamar line). The Chronicler preserves this dual priesthood without comment; it will be resolved by Solomon’s dismissal of Abiathar (1 Kgs 2:27, 35).
15.12: David’s command to the priests and Levites: “Sanctify yourselves (hitqaddᵉšû), you and your fellow Levites, and bring up the ark of the LORD, the God of Israel, to the place I have prepared for it.” The Hithpael of qāḏaš (to sanctify oneself) is the standard preparatory ritual before cultic action: washing, abstention from ordinary activities, and ritual purity. The command links the success of the second ark-transport to the proper ritual preparation absent from the first attempt.
15.14: “So the priests and the Levites sanctified themselves to bring up the ark of the LORD, the God of Israel.” The narrative confirmation that the priests and Levites obeyed David’s sanctification command closes the preparatory sequence. The obedience to the cultic prescription matches David’s obedience to the battle prescription in ch14 (“David did as God commanded him,” 14:16), creating a parallel between military and liturgical obedience as the twin modes of Davidic faithfulness.
15.15: “And the Levites carried the ark of God on their shoulders with the poles on it, as Moses had commanded (kᵃʾăšer ṣiwwāh mōšeh) according to the word of the LORD.” The fulfilment formula kᵃʾăšer ṣiwwāh mōšeh is the Chronicler’s mark of proper cultic procedure; it appears throughout his narrative to signal alignment between Davidic-Solomonic action and Mosaic prescription. The poles (mōṭôt) and shoulder-carrying (bakkātēp) correspond precisely to Exod 25:14–15 and Num 7:9. The correction of ch13’s error is complete: new cart → Levitical shoulders.
15.16: David instructs the Levitical chiefs to appoint singers and musicians from among their fellows. The organization of the Levitical music corps is the Chronicler’s major addition to the ark-transport narrative (absent entirely from Samuel). The instruments specified — lyres (nᵉḇālîm), harps (kinnōrôt), and cymbals (mᵉṣiltāyîm) — correspond to the instruments of David’s first (failed) procession (13:8), but now properly organized under Levitical authority rather than as spontaneous popular celebration.
15.17: The three great Levitical musicians are appointed: Heman son of Joel (Kohathite; 6:33–38), Asaph son of Berechiah (Gershonite; 6:39–43), and Ethan son of Kushaiah (Merarite; 6:44–47). These three represent the three Levitical families and will become the founding masters of Israelite cultic music, with their descendants serving as the official singers of the first and second temples. Psalm titles bearing “Of Asaph” (Pss 73–83) and “Of Heman” (Ps 88) and “Of Ethan” (Ps 89) reflect the antiquity of this musical guilds’ tradition.
15.18: The second-rank Levitical singers include Zechariah and twelve others. The fourteen names of the second rank include Obed-edom the Gittite (who had hosted the ark, 13:14), now formally enrolled as a Levitical gatekeeper-singer. His inclusion resolves the ambiguity of his ethnic identity: the Chronicler places him here as a Levite, regardless of his gittî designation. The gatekeepers (šōʿărîm) had both cultic and security functions.
15.19: Heman, Asaph, and Ethan appointed to sound bronze cymbals (mᵉṣiltāyîm nᵉḥōšet). The three masters sound the cymbals — the percussive-signalling instrument — while their subordinates play the string instruments. The bronze cymbals (vs. the hand-held cymbals) were large instrument used for ritual signalling and processional cadence. The Chronicler’s technical precision about instrument types is characteristic of his detailed interest in Levitical musicology.
15.20: Eight men play lyres (nᵉḇālîm) set to Alamoth (ʿal ʿălāmôt). ʿĂlāmôt (lit. ‘maidens/young women’) is a musical term appearing also in Ps 46 (title: “according to Alamoth”), apparently indicating a higher vocal or instrumental register — possibly soprano or treble pitch. The technical musical term demonstrates that the Chronicler (or his sources) preserved genuine liturgical performance notation.
15.21: Six men play harps (kinnōrôt) set to the Sheminith (ʿal haššᵉmînît). Haššᵉmînît (lit. ‘the eighth’) appears in Ps 6 and Ps 12 titles and likely indicates the lower octave or bass register (an octave below Alamoth). The paired Alamoth/Sheminith directions suggest a two-register antiphonal or harmonic arrangement for the procession, matching the two groups of instruments in the Levitical orchestra.
15.22: Chenaniah (kᵉnanyāhû, ‘YHWH establishes’) is the Levitical master of the music (śar hallᵉwiyyîm hammassāʾ) who directed the singing (“because he was skilled,” kî ḇîn hûʾ).
