The word massāʾ normally means ‘burden/load’ but here has been taken to mean ‘the lifting of the voice in song’ or ‘the musical performance.’ The phrase kî ḇîn hûʾ (‘because he was discerning/expert’) uses bîn, which appears in the maśkîl psalm titles (Ps 32, 42, etc.) and refers to skilled interpretive understanding. The music director is thus characterized not just as technically proficient but as one with cultic insight.
15.23: Berechiah and Elkanah are gatekeepers (šōʿărîm) for the ark. The two gatekeepers flanking the ark provide security-escort alongside the musical procession. Elkanah is also a Kohathite name (6:23, 25–26) and Berechiah is Asaph’s father (6:39); these may be different individuals of the same name or the same figures appearing in multiple roles.
15.24: Seven priests blow trumpets (ḥăṣōṣᵉrôt) before the ark. Shebaniah, Joshaphat, Nethanel, Amasai, Zechariah, Benaiah, and Eliezer form the trumpet corps. The Mosaic prescription in Num 10:8 designates trumpet-blowing as an exclusively priestly function: “The sons of Aaron the priests shall blow the trumpets.” The Chronicler’s careful distinction between the Levitical singers/string-players and the priestly trumpet-blowers reflects this Pentateuchal hierarchy. Obed-edom and Jehiah also serve as gatekeepers, completing the full cultic personnel roster for the procession.
15.25: The successful ark-transport begins: “So David, the elders of Israel, and the commanders of thousands went to bring up the ark of the covenant of the LORD from the house of Obed-edom with joy (bᵉśimḥāh).” The combination of civil leaders (elders), military leaders (commanders of thousands), and cultic leadership (Levites) represents the full Israelite polity in unified action. The joy that marks this procession contrasts with the ambiguous celebration of ch13 that ended in death; proper order enables authentic celebration.
15.26: “Because God helped (ʿāzar) the Levites who were carrying the ark,” they sacrificed seven bulls and seven rams. The divine assistance formula (kî ʿāzar hāʾĕlōhîm ʾet hallᵉwiyyîm) is the Chronicler’s theological signature for the moment the holiness-danger is safely managed: God helps the Levites precisely because they are following the proper procedure. The seven-seven sacrifice is the perfect completion number for a covenant act (cf. Gen 21:28–30; 1 Kgs 18:33).
15.27: David’s linen robe (mᵉʿîl bûṣ, a fine-linen outer garment) and the linen ephod (ʾēpôḏ bāḏ) are his cultic vestments for the procession. The Samuel parallel (2 Sam 6:14) mentions only the linen ephod; the Chronicler adds the linen robe, aligning David more fully with the high-priestly vestments. Bûṣ (fine white linen) is the material of the temple curtains (2 Chr 3:14) and the priestly garments (2 Chr 5:12); its use for David’s procession-robe places him in the cultic register of the Levitical-priestly service. The singers and Levites are also in linen, making the procession a uniformly white-linen liturgical event.
15.28: “So all Israel brought up the ark of the covenant of the LORD with shouting (ûbᵉtᵉrûʿāh), with the sound of the ram’s horn (qôl šôpār), with trumpets (ḥăṣōṣᵉrôt), and with cymbals (mᵉṣiltāyîm), making music on lyres (nᵉḇālîm) and harps (kinnōrôt).” The celebratory inventory of instruments echoes 13:8 but with the proper liturgical supervision now in place. The tᵉrûʿāh (the great shout or blast) was used for both battle (Num 10:9) and cultic celebration (Num 10:10), marking this as a holy-war procession in which YHWH enters his sanctuary in triumph.
15.29: Michal daughter of Saul sees David leaping and dancing (mᵉraqqēḏ ûmᵉśaḥēq) and “despised him in her heart (wayyibbez lāh bibbāh).” The Chronicler reproduces this closing verse from 2 Sam 6:16 verbatim but omits the Samuel sequel (2 Sam 6:20–23) where David and Michal argue and she is declared barren. The Chronicler’s truncation avoids the domestic conflict, focusing instead on the contrast between the Saul-line (represented by Michal’s contempt) and the David-line (represented by uninhibited cultic joy). The dynastic implication is preserved — Saul’s descendants view David’s worship as undignified — without the punitive sequel.

11.1: ʿaṣmᵉḵā ûḇāśārᵉḵā (‘your bone and your flesh’) is the OT’s covenant-kinship formula (Gen 2:23 Adam of Eve; Judg 9:2; 2 Sam 5:1). The elders appeal to shared descent to legitimate David’s kingship over all Israel after Saul’s collapse — the same formula the parallel passage (2 Sam 5:1) uses. Chronicles omits all of David’s Hebron years and Ishbosheth’s rival reign, moving immediately to this moment of national unity.
11.2: ʾattāh hûʾ hammôṣîʾ wᵉhammēḇîʾ (‘you were the one who led out and brought in Israel’) — military idiom for commanding an army (Num 27:17; 1 Sam 18:13). The elders recall David’s past military leadership under Saul as grounds for accepting him as king — he was the real commander all along.
11.3: wayyikrōt dāwid lāhem bᵉrît biḥeḇrôn lipnê YHWH — David cuts a covenant with the elders ‘before the LORD’ at Hebron. The Davidic kingship is established as a covenant act witnessed by YHWH, not merely a political agreement. kᵉḏiḇar YHWH bᵉyaḏ šᵉmûʾēl — ‘according to the word of YHWH by the hand of Samuel’ — Chronicles frames the event as prophetic fulfillment from the start.
11.4: yᵉrûšālaim hiʾ yᵉḇûs — ‘Jerusalem, that is Jebus’ — Chronicles identifies Jerusalem by its pre-conquest name (Judges 19:10-11). The Jebusites had held the city since the conquest. The Chronicler begins David’s reign with the capture of YHWH’s eventual throne city.
11.5: lōʾ tāḇôʾ hēnnāh — ‘you shall not come in here’ — the Jebusites’ taunt; in 2 Sam 5:6 they add ‘even the blind and the lame will ward you off’. Chronicles omits the taunt content and focuses on the outcome: wayyilkaḏ dāwid ʾet maṣṣaḏat ṣiyyôn — ‘David captured the stronghold of Zion’ — establishing the theological center of the Davidic kingdom.
11.6: The appointment formula: kol makkēh yᵉḇûsî bārîʾšônāh yihyeh lᵉrōʾš wᵉlᵉśār — ‘whoever strikes the Jebusites first shall become head and commander.’ This is a meritocratic military appointment. Joab (ben-ṣᵉrûyāh — always identified by his mother, suggesting known status) wins the position. The parallel in 2 Sam 5:8 is obscure; Chronicles’ version is clearer about the merit-based appointment.
11.7: miṣṣaḏāh dāwid — ‘from the stronghold, David [dwelt]’ — the fortress that had been Jebus becomes the ‘City of David.’ The renaming establishes the theological and dynastic identity of Jerusalem. The Chronicler uses this transition as the foundation of the temple-building narrative that dominates chs. 22–29.
11.8: Joab is credited with repairing sheʾār hāʿîr (‘the rest of the city’) while David builds from the Millo inward. hammillōʾ is likely a terraced fill structure supporting the city expansion (cf. 2 Sam 5:9). The collaboration of king and general in urban construction shows the Chronicler’s interest in organized communal building — anticipating the temple project.
11.9: wayyēlek dāwid hālôḵ wᵉḡāḏôl wᵉYHWH ṣᵉḇāʾôt ʿimmô — ‘David kept going and growing, and the LORD of hosts was with him.’ The divine-warrior formula (YHWH ṣᵉḇāʾôt = LORD of armies) grounds David’s expansion not in military genius but in divine presence. This is the Chronicler’s theological explanation for all Davidic success.
11.10: rāʾšê haggibborîm — ‘chiefs of the mighty men’ — introduces the famous warrior roster. ʾăšer ḥizzᵉqû-ʿimmô ḇamlûḵātô — ‘who strengthened themselves with him in his kingship’ — the Chronicler frames the Mighty Men as co-participants in establishing the Davidic kingdom, consistent with his overall emphasis on unified all-Israel support for David.
11.11: jashobeam ben-ḥakmonî — head of the three (or thirty, depending on text). The phrase rōʾš haššālišîm is disputed: ‘chief of the thirty’ (MT šᵉlōšîm) vs ‘chief of the three’ (LXX and some MSS šᵉlōšāh); the parallel in 2 Sam 23:8 has ‘Josheb-basshebeth’ a different name for possibly the same person. His feat: slaying 300 with a single spear-thrust. The number is symbolic of overwhelming martial prowess.
11.12: ʾeleʿāzār ben-dôdô haʾăḥōḥî — Eleazar son of Dodo the Ahohite, ‘one of the three mighty men.’ His story follows in v. 13-14. The Ahohites were a Benjaminite sub-clan (8:4). These three men are haggibborîm at the highest tier — an elite within the elite.
11.13: The battle at Pas-dammim (here; 2 Sam 23:11 has Lehi) where the Israelites fled from the Philistines over a barley field. The geographical difference between the parallel accounts may reflect different battle sites or text corruption. wayyāqōmû bᵉtôḵ haḥelqāh wayyaṣṣîlûhā — ‘they took their stand in the middle of the field and saved it’ — collective action; v. 14 shows it was Eleazar specifically.
11.14: wayyaṣṣîlem YHWH tᵉšûʿāh gᵉḏôlāh — ‘and YHWH saved [them] with a great salvation.’ The military victory is attributed entirely to YHWH. tᵉšûʿāh (salvation/deliverance) is the same word-root as yēšûaʿ (Joshua/Jesus). This divine-salvation formula in battle contexts establishes the theological register of all Davidic warfare.
11.15: Three of the Thirty go to David at the cave of Adullam while the Philistines are encamped in the valley of Rephaim. The rock (haṣṣûr) is identified with Adullam. The detail that maṣṣaḇ pᵉlištîm bᵉḇêt leḥem — ‘the Philistine garrison was at Bethlehem’ — marks how completely the enemy had occupied David’s home territory, heightening the poignancy of his longing.
11.16: David is ‘in the stronghold’ (bammᵉṣûḏāh) while Philistine neṣîḇ (garrison/prefect) holds Bethlehem. The contrast between the cave-king and the occupied homeland generates the narrative tension that leads to David’s thirst and its extraordinary fulfillment.
11.17: mî yašqēnî mayim mibôr bêt-leḥem — ‘Who will give me water to drink from the well of Bethlehem at the gate?’ This is an offhand expression of longing, not a command — which makes the three warriors’ response more extraordinary. The well at the gate was a civic landmark; the gate = the place of legal assembly and public life.
11.18: The three break through the Philistine garrison, draw water from Bethlehem’s well, and bring it to David. David’s response: wayyissᵉḵēhû lAYHWH — ‘he poured it out to YHWH.’ This is a libation — treating the water as too holy to drink because it was obtained at the risk of blood. The act transforms water into a sacred offering, converting an act of loyalty into an act of worship.
11.19: ḥālîlāh lî mēʾĕlōhay — ‘God forbid it of me / my God, may I not do this.’ ḥālîlāh is a strong profanation-avowal formula (Gen 44:7; Josh 22:29; 1 Sam 12:23). hᵉdām hāʾănāšîm hāʾēlleh bᵉnapšôtām — ‘the blood of these men at the cost of their lives (napšôtām).’ David refuses to consume what was purchased at the risk of life — a profound meditation on value, sacrifice, and the sacred cost of devotion.
11.20: ʾăḇîšay ʾăḥî yôʾāḇ — Abishai brother of Joab. He ‘lifted up his spear against three hundred and killed them.’ He is among haššᵉlōšāh (the three) but his status is complex: v. 21 says he was ‘more honored than the two’ (than Eleazar and Jashobeam? or the other two of the second set of three?). The text is difficult; the Chronicler seems to distinguish two tiers of three.
11.21: min-haššᵉlōšāh nikkāḇāḏ — ‘more honored than the three’ — but Abishai ‘did not attain to the first three’ (wᵉʾel-haššᵉlōšāh lōʾ-bāʾ). He was śar (officer/captain) of the three. The hierarchical ranking within the Mighty Men reflects a formal military structure.
11.22: bᵉnāyāhû ben-yᵉhôyāḏāʿ ben-ʾîš-ḥayil — ‘Benaiah son of Jehoiada, son of a valiant man.’ His three exploits: (1) slaying two ʾărîʾēl of Moab (lion-like men or ‘altar-hearths’ of Moab — meaning debated); (2) slaying a lion in a pit on a snowy day (bᵉyôm haššāleg); (3) slaying a large Egyptian (v. 23). These are listed in ascending difficulty.
11.23: Benaiah kills an Egyptian ‘a man of measure’ (ʾîš middāh — very tall, same formula as Goliath, 1 Chr 20:6) with the Egyptian’s own spear. wayyigzōl ʾet haḥănît miyyaḏ hammiṣrî wayyahaṣṣᵉhû baḥănîtô — ‘he wrested the spear from the Egyptian’s hand and killed him with his own spear.’ The reversal motif (turning an enemy’s weapon against him) appears also in David vs. Goliath and has symbolic resonance: the victor uses the enemy’s own power for his destruction. [Existing entry preserved]
11.24: ʾēlleh ʿāśāh bᵉnāyāhû — ‘these things Benaiah did’ — formal summary of v. 22-23. wayyᵉhî-lô šēm bᵉšᵉlōšāh haggibborîm — ‘he had a name among the thirty mighty men’ (MT ‘three’ — text uncertain as at v. 11). His fame is specifically named, not just assumed.
11.25: Benaiah ‘was honored beyond the thirty’ (nikkāḇāḏ hûʾ min-haššᵉlōšîm) but did not attain to the Three. David set him over his bodyguard (mišmaʿtô). This administrative note shows the Chronicler’s interest in the organization of David’s court — which directly relates to his preparations for temple governance.
11.26: The roster of ‘valiant men of the armies’ (gibbôrê haḥăyālîm) begins. These are the Thirty (roughly) — a second tier below the Three. ʾăśāhʾēl ʾăḥî yôʾāḇ — Asahel brother of Joab, who famously died in 2 Sam 2 pursuing Abner. His inclusion here, alongside Elhanan of Bethlehem, begins a list that will end with Uriah the Hittite (v. 41).
11.27: Shammoth the Harorite and Helez the Pelonite — geographical epithets identify their tribal origins. The parallel in 2 Sam 23:25-26 has slightly variant names (Shammah the Harodite / Helez the Paltite). The differences likely reflect copyist variants in proper names, a common phenomenon in these lists.
11.28: Ira son of Ikkesh the Tekoite and Abiezer the Anathothite. Anathoth is the priestly town later famous as Jeremiah’s birthplace (Jer 1:1). The warrior list crosses geographic lines — Tekoa, Anathoth, and many other towns — showing David’s coalition of all-Israel support.
11.29: Sibbecai the Hushathite — who will appear in ch. 20:4 killing Sippai the giant. Ilai the Ahohite. The Hushathites are a Judahite sub-group (1 Chr 4:4). The recurrence of Ahohite warriors (cf. v. 12) suggests a clan with a strong martial tradition.
11.30: Maharai the Netophathite and Heled son of Baanah the Netophathite. Netophah is near Bethlehem (Ezra 2:22); the two Netophathites together suggest geographic clustering within the list.
11.31: Ithai son of Ribai of Gibeah — Gibeah is Saul’s hometown (1 Sam 10:26), so this Benjaminite warrior serves David even from the house of Saul’s city. Benaiah the Pirathonite (Ephraim, Judg 12:15). The list’s geographic breadth — from Gibeah to Pirathon — reflects all-Israel participation.
11.32: Hurai of the brooks of Gaash and Abiel the Arbathite. ‘Brooks of Gaash’ references the area where Joshua was buried (Josh 24:30). The Arbathite is from Beth-arabah (Josh 15:6), near the Dead Sea rift.
11.33: Azmaveth the Baharumite (2 Sam 23:31 has ‘Barhumite’ — likely variant of the same place, Bahurim, 2 Sam 3:16) and Eliahba the Shaalbonite (from Shaalabbin, Dan’s territory, Josh 19:42). Both names appear only in the Mighty Men lists.
11.34: Jonathan son of Shage the Hararite and ‘the sons of Hashem the Gizonite’ — Gizon is otherwise unattested in the OT; possibly a scribal corruption. The Hararites appear several times in the list (vv. 34-35), suggesting a warrior clan from the hill country.
11.35: Ahiam son of Sacar the Hararite and Eliphal son of Ur. The 2 Sam 23:33 parallel has ‘Ahiam son of Sharar the Hararite’ — the variant between Sacar and Sharar is a typical copyist confusion of kāp̄ and rēš. These variants are numerous throughout the parallel lists and generally have no theological significance.
11.36: Hepher the Mecherathite (unattested elsewhere) and Ahijah the Pelonite. The Pelonite appears again at v. 27 (Helez). The frequent ‘-ite’ demonymic forms indicate these warriors are identified by their home towns or clans, not by patronymics — suggesting some are from small or obscure communities.
11.37: Hezro the Carmelite (from Carmel in Judah, 1 Sam 25:2, Nabal’s home) and Naarai son of Ezbai. The Carmelite connection links to Abigail’s homeland. These warriors are from the Judean heartland.
11.38: Joel brother of Nathan — if Nathan here is David’s prophet, this would link a warrior directly to a royal courtier. More likely a different Nathan. Mibhar son of Hagri (2 Sam 23:36 reads differently — possibly scribal). The Hagrites were a trans-Jordanian people (1 Chr 5:10); a Hagrite’s son among David’s elite shows the ethnically inclusive character of the warrior band.
11.39: Zelek the Ammonite — an Ammonite among David’s elite warriors. Naharai the Berothite, armor-bearer of Joab son of Zeruiah. The presence of an Ammonite (enemy nation) and a servant of Joab in the Thirty reflects the diverse and personally loyal character of David’s military family.
11.40: Ira the Ithrite and Gareb the Ithrite — two warriors from the same clan appearing together. The Ithrites were a Judahite sub-group (2 Sam 23:38).
11.41: ʾûriyyāh haḥittî — Uriah the Hittite appears at the very end of the elite warrior roster. In 2 Sam 23:39, he is the thirty-seventh and last named. This placement is almost certainly intentional in the literary ordering — Uriah the Hittite, the most loyal of all the Mighty Men, the one David had murdered. The Chronicler reproduces the list including Uriah; he omits the Bathsheba narrative (as he omits David’s failures generally) but includes Uriah’s name in the honor roll.
11.42: Adina son of Shiza the Reubenite, chief of the Reubenites, and thirty with him. The Reubenite contingent has its own sub-commander. This entry marks the transition to a broader military survey that extends beyond the original core group.
11.43: Hanan son of Maacah and Joshaphat the Mithnite. Maacah is a place-name (Aram-maacah, north of Dan) as well as a personal name. These names appear only here.
11.44: Uzzia the Ashterathite (from Ashtaroth in Bashan, trans-Jordan — Manassite territory), Shama and Jeiel sons of Hothan the Aroerite (Aroer, different from Gad’s Aroer — possibly the Judean Aroer of 1 Sam 30:28).
11.45: Jediael son of Shimri and Joha his brother the Tizite. ‘Tizite’ is unattested elsewhere; possibly a corrupt form of another place-name.
11.46: Eliel the Mahavite; sons of Elnaam: Jeribai and Joshaviah; Ithmah the Moabite. Another Moabite (cf. Zelek the Ammonite, v. 39) in David’s corps — showing the expanding ethnic scope of the Davidic coalition. The Mahavite clan is otherwise unknown.
11.47: Eliel, Obed, and Jaasiel the Mesobaite conclude the list. ‘Mesobaite’ is unattested; possibly a place-name. The list of Mighty Men (vv. 26-47) totals approximately thirty-seven names, though some are grouped. The Chronicler uses this list to show all-Israel participation in David’s kingdom from the start — consistent with his broader theological agenda.

12.1: wᵉʾēlleh habbāʾîm ʾel-dāwid bᵉṣiqlag — ‘and these are those who came to David at Ziklag.’ Ziklag was the Philistine city given to David during his exile (1 Sam 27:6). bᵉʿôḏ ʿāṣûr mippᵉnê šāʾûl ben-qîš — ‘while still restrained from before Saul son of Kish’ — the fugitive period. The Chronicler includes this section to show that David’s all-Israel coalition began forming before he was king, predating any political opportunity.
12.2: middōšᵉqê qešet — ‘armed with the bow, right-handed and left-handed in hurling stones.’ The Benjaminite warriors’ ambidexterity is a military specialty noted also in Judg 20:16 (the men of Gibeah who could sling a stone to a hair’s breadth). Their joining David from Saul’s own tribe is particularly significant — Benjamin loyalty shifting from Saul to David.
12.3: The list of Benjaminite warriors begins with Ahiezer and Joash sons of Shemaah the Gibeathite. Gibeah, again, is Saul’s city. These Benjaminites from Saul’s hometown came to David, the ultimate statement of cross-tribal loyalty. Jeziel and Pelet sons of Azmaveth — Azmaveth also appears in the Mighty Men list (11:33).
12.4: Ishmaiah the Gibeonite is noted as ‘mighty among the Thirty and over the Thirty’ — i.e., ranking within the elite corps already established. Jeremiah, Jahaziel, Johanan, and Josabad the Gederathite fill out the contingent. The overlapping names with the Mighty Men list suggest the two lists derive from the same military administrative source.
12.5: Eluzai, Jerimoth, Bealiah, Shemariah, and Shephatiah the Haruphite. The Haruph clan is mentioned in Nehemiah 7:24 as returnees from exile — linking these pre-exile warriors to the post-exile community the Chronicler is addressing.
12.6: Elkanah, Isshiah, Azarel, Joezer, and Jashobeam the Korahites. The Korahites were a Levitical guild associated with temple music and gatekeeping (1 Chr 6:22; 9:19). Their appearance in a warrior list suggests the boundary between cultic and military roles was permeable in the early monarchy.
12.7: Joelah and Zebadiah sons of Jeroham from Gedor. Gedor is a Judahite town (4:4) — a further note of Judahite support for David supplementing the Benjaminite contingent.
12.8: ûmin-haggāḏî niphrᵉḏû ʾel-dāwid lamlᵉḏbārāh — ‘from the Gadites there separated themselves to David in the wilderness.’ The desert/wilderness period references David’s time in the Judean wilderness (1 Sam 22-26). These Gadites, from trans-Jordan, crossed to join the fugitive king. gibborê ḥayil anšê ṣāḇāʾ lamilḥāmāh maʿarᵉḵê ṣinnāh warōmaḥ — ‘valiant men, warriors for war, trained to handle shield and spear.’ ûp̄ᵉnêhem pᵉnê ʾaryēh — ‘their faces were like the faces of lions’ — the lion-face simile for fierce warriors.
12.9: The Gadite commanders are listed in order: Ezer the first, Obadiah the second, Eliab the third. The ordinal numbering (hārîʾšônāh, haššᵉnî, haššᵉlîšî, etc.) continues through v. 13, imposing a clear hierarchical structure. This military ranking system shows sophisticated administrative organization during David’s fugitive period.
12.10: Mishmannah the fourth, Jeremiah the fifth — the list continues. The Gadite names recur in the trans-Jordanian genealogies of ch. 5. The Chronicler links these warriors back to their tribal genealogies, showing the all-Israel framework of the David narrative.
12.11: Attai the sixth, Eliel the seventh. The name Eliel appears multiple times in these lists (11:46-47; 12:11) — likely different individuals sharing a common theophoric name (‘my God is God’).
12.12: Johanan the eighth, Elzabad the ninth. The list of Gadite officers reaches its tenth at v. 13. The eleven-man named unit reflects a military decimal structure (cf. the tribal warrior counts in chs. 7 and 12:23-37).
12.13: Jeremiah the tenth, Machbannai the eleventh. The complete Gadite officer list has eleven men. Verse 14’s summary notes they ranged from commanders of a hundred to commanders of a thousand, showing these were mid-level officers, not common soldiers.
12.14: min-bᵉnê-gāḏ rāʾšê haṣṣāḇāʾ — ‘from the sons of Gad, commanders of the army.’ The superlative: haqqāṭōn lᵉmeʾāh wᵉhaggāḏôl laʾelep̄ — ‘the least over a hundred and the greatest over a thousand.’ This telescoping of magnitude is a rhetorical device emphasizing the quality of the entire contingent.
12.15: hēmmāh ʿāḇrû ʾet-hayyardēn baḥōḏeš hārîʾšôn — ‘they crossed the Jordan in the first month’ — during spring flooding (kî mālaʾ ʿal kol-gᵉḏôtāyw — ‘when it had overflowed all its banks’). Crossing a flood-stage Jordan to join David was an act of extraordinary commitment. The crossing motif echoes the Exodus/conquest Jordan-crossings as a theological parallel.
12.16: wayyāḇōʾû min-bᵉnê binyāmîn wîhûḏāh — ‘and there came from the sons of Benjamin and Judah to the stronghold.’ The double reference — Benjamin (Saul’s tribe) and Judah (David’s tribe) coming together — signals the beginning of the reunification that will culminate at Hebron.
12.17: David’s interrogation of the arriving men: ʾim-lᵉšālôm bāʾtem ʾēlay lᵉʿāzᵉrēnî — ‘if you have come to me in peace to help me.’ But if they have come to betray him to his enemies: yōkaḥ ʾĕlōhê ʾăḇôtênû — ‘the God of our fathers will see and rebuke.’ David commends the judgment of betrayal to God rather than taking pre-emptive violence — showing the theological restraint that characterizes his fugitive period.
12.18: wᵉrûaḥ lāḇᵉšāh ʾet-ʿămaśay — ‘the Spirit clothed Amasai.’ The verb lāḇaš (to clothe) with Spirit-clothing is the same idiom used for Gideon (Judg 6:34) and Zechariah (2 Chr 24:20). The Spirit ‘wearing’ a person rather than ‘entering’ or ‘coming upon’ them is distinctive. Amasai then speaks a poetic declaration: lᵉḵā dāwid wᵉʿimmᵉḵā ben-yišay šālôm šālôm lᵉḵā — ‘Yours, David, and with you, son of Jesse! Peace, peace to you.’ The three-fold šālôm is a covenantal blessing formula.
12.19: Some Manassites defect to David as he goes with the Philistines to fight Saul (the battle of Jezreel/Gilboa context, 1 Sam 29). The Philistine lords dismissed David out of concern he might defect. bᵉḇôʾô ʿim-pᵉlištîm — ‘when he came with the Philistines’ — the Chronicler does not explain the awkward context (David as Philistine vassal) but includes the result: Manassites joined him.
12.20: The Manassite defectors named: Adnah, Jozabad, Jediael, Michael, Jozabad (second), Elihu, Zillethai — ‘heads of thousands’ (rāʾšê hāʾălāpîm) in Manasseh. These are senior military commanders changing allegiance from Saul to David, a major political shift.
12.21: wᵉhēmmāh ʿāzᵉrû ʾet-dāwid ʿal-haggdûḏ — ‘they helped David against the raiding band.’ The reference is to the Amalekite raid on Ziklag (1 Sam 30), where David recovered his people and plunder. kî gibbôrê ḥayil kull̄ām — ‘for they were all mighty warriors.’
12.22: kî lᵉʿēt yôm bᵉyôm yāḇōʾû ʿal-dāwid lᵉʿāzᵉrô ʿaḏ-lᵉmaḥăneh gāḏôl kᵉmaḥănēh ʾĕlōhîm — ‘for day by day they came to David to help him, until it was a great army like the army of God.’ The phrase maḥănēh ʾĕlōhîm (army/camp of God) appears elsewhere only in Gen 32:2 (Jacob’s encounter with the angels). The Chronicler implicitly compares David’s growing coalition to the divine army — a remarkable theological claim about the Davidic enterprise.
12.23: wᵉʾēlleh misparê rāʾšê hāʾāḥûzāh lissᵉḇāʾ — ‘and these are the numbers of the armed forces.’ bāʾû ʾel-dāwid ḥeḇrônāh lᵉhāsēb malḵût šāʾûl ʾēlāyw kᵉpî YHWH — ‘to turn the kingdom of Saul to him according to the word of YHWH.’ This framing makes the Hebron anointing not a political event but the fulfillment of divine decree. The military census that follows (vv. 24-37) shows the scope of the all-Israel coalition.
12.24: Judah: 6,800 warriors bearing shield and spear. The relatively small number for Judah (compared to other tribes) may reflect that Judah had already been with David for years; the others are newly joining. The specific weapon groupings (ṭōmᵉkê ṣinnāh wārōmaḥ — shield-and-spear bearers) represent organized infantry units.
12.25: Simeon: 7,100 ‘mighty men of valor for the war’ (gibborê ḥayil laṣṣāḇāʾ). Simeon was geographically integrated into Judah (Josh 19:1) and often acts as Judah’s partner (Judg 1:3). Their substantial number shows strong tribal support.
12.26: Levi: 4,600. Levites are counted in a military list, which is unusual — they are normally exempt from warfare (Num 1:47-53). The Chronicler’s inclusion of Levites in the Hebron coalition reflects his emphasis on priestly/cultic participation in establishing the Davidic order that will culminate in the temple.
12.27: The Aaronites specifically: Jehoiada the leader with 3,700 men. wᵉʿimmô yᵉhôyāḏāʿ hannāśîʾ lᵉʾahărōn — ‘and with him Jehoiada, prince of the house of Aaron.’ Aaron’s priestly house is thus explicitly part of the Davidic military coalition — the priestly and kingly orders are aligned from the beginning.
12.28: wᵉṣādôq naʿar gibbôr ḥayil ûbêt ʾāḇîw śārîm ʿeśrîm ûšnayim — ‘and Zadok, a young man mighty of valor, and his father’s house: twenty-two commanders.’ This is the first mention of Zadok in Chronicles. His presence at David’s anointing at Hebron anticipates his later appointment as chief priest (1 Chr 15:11; 2 Sam 8:17). The Chronicler introduces him here to show the Zadokite priesthood’s foundation in the original Davidic covenant moment.
12.29: Benjamin: 3,000. But with a significant qualification: wᵉrōḇ mēhem ʿaḏ-hēnnāh šōmᵉrîm mišmeret bêt šāʾûl — ‘for the majority of them had still been keeping watch over the house of Saul.’ The small number of Benjaminites reflects the complexity of the transition — Benjamin is Saul’s tribe and many remained loyal to the Saulide house. The three thousand who came to David represent a significant but minority shift within the tribe.
12.30: Ephraim: 20,800, described as gibborê ḥayil anšê šēmōt lᵉbêt ʾăḇôtām — ‘mighty men of valor, famous men in their fathers’ houses.’ The phrase anšê šēmōt (‘men of names/fame’) is unusual — these are warriors of established reputation, not anonymous troops.
12.31: Half-tribe of Manasseh (west): 18,000, specifically named by name (niqḇû bišmôt) — a special notation suggesting these were individually identified in the administrative record. Their 18,000 represents substantial Manassite commitment to the Davidic transition.
12.32: ûmibᵉnê yiśśāḵār yôḏᵉʿê bîn laʿittîm laḏaʿat mah-yāʿăśeh yiśrāʾēl — ‘and from the sons of Issachar, those who understood the times, to know what Israel should do.’ This famous phrase (yôḏᵉʿê bîn laʿittîm) attributes to Issachar a quality of political-theological wisdom — understanding the ‘times’ (ʿittîm) means discerning the moment when David’s kingship was the proper order of things. kol-ʾăḥêhem ʿal-pîhem — ‘and all their brothers were under their command’ — Issachar’s wisdom leaders guide the whole tribe.
12.33: Zebulun: 50,000 warriors, specifically commended as lōʾ bᵉlēḇ wālēḇ — ‘not of double heart’ (literally ‘not with heart and heart’). The double heart (lēḇ wālēḇ) signifies divided allegiance (cf. Ps 12:2). Zebulun’s complete commitment is distinguished from Benjamin’s divided loyalty (v. 29). The large number and the commendation make Zebulun the paradigm warrior-tribe at Hebron.
12.34: Naphtali: 1,000 commanders, 37,000 shield-and-spear warriors. The large base force with named commanders suggests Naphtali had a well-organized military structure. The north was strongly Davidic here.
12.35: Dan: 28,600 armed for war. Dan’s territory was in the far north (following their migration in Judges 18), making their participation a statement of all-Israel support from the most geographically remote southern tribe’s counterpart.
12.36: Asher: 40,000 warriors fit for war (bᵉʿōrᵉkê milḥāmāh — arranged/ordered for war). Asher, another northern coastal tribe, contributes substantially. The numbers mount as the list moves north, reaching a crescendo of pan-Israel military commitment.
12.37: Trans-Jordanian contingent: Reuben, Gad, and half-tribe of Manasseh (east): 120,000 with all manner of weapons of war (kol-kᵉlê milḥāmāh). This is the largest single block count. The trans-Jordanian tribes — geographically separated from the west by the Jordan — are fully committed. Their inclusion fulfills the geographic scope of the Davidic kingdom (‘all Israel’).
12.38: kol-ʾēlleh anšê milḥāmāh ʿōzrê maʿărāḵāh bᵉlēḇāḇ šālēm bāʾû ḥeḇrônāh lᵉhamlîḵ ʾet-dāwid — ‘all these men of war, keeping ranks, came to Hebron with a whole heart to make David king over all Israel.’ The phrase lēḇāḇ šālēm (whole/undivided heart) is the Chronicler’s signature expression for the proper covenantal posture (cf. 1 Chr 28:9; 29:19; 2 Chr 15:17; 16:9). wᵉgam kol-šᵉʾērît yiśrāʾēl lēḇ ʾeḥāḏ — ‘and also all the rest of Israel was of one heart to make David king.’ The one-heart formula is the Chronicler’s ideal of national unity.
12.39: wayyihyû-šām ʿim-dāwid šᵉlōšet yāmîm ʾōḵlîm wᵉšōtîm — ‘they were there with David three days, eating and drinking.’ The three-day covenant feast at Hebron echoes covenant meal patterns (Gen 31:54; Exod 24:11). Shared eating seals the covenant of kingship. The Chronicler’s interest in communal celebration here anticipates the great liturgical celebrations of chs. 15-16 and Solomon’s temple dedication.
12.40: wᵉgam qᵉrôḇêhem ʿaḏ-yiśśāḵār wᵉzᵉḇûlun wᵉnaptālî mᵉḇîʾîm leḥem ʿal-ḥămōrîm — ‘and also those nearest to them, as far as Issachar and Zebulun and Naphtali, brought bread on donkeys.’ The list of provisions — bread, figs, raisins, wine, oil, oxen, sheep — represents a banquet of all-Israel abundance. The provision-list functions as a symbol of national solidarity: every major food item is contributed, from grain products to livestock to wine. The feast is not just political but a covenant celebration in the fullest sense.

9.1: wᵉyithyaḥasû — the verb yāḥas (to be enrolled/registered by genealogy) appears in the Hithpael reflexive here: wayyithyaḥasû kol yiśrāʾēl, ‘and all Israel enrolled themselves.’ The Hithpael signals community self-identification — the act of registering in the genealogies is active, not merely assigned. sēper malkê yiśrāʾēl — ‘the book of the kings of Israel’ is the Chronicler’s most frequently cited source (appearing with variations throughout 1–2 Chronicles); it is distinct from canonical Kings. bᵉmaʿalām — ‘because of their unfaithfulness’ (Qal infinitive construct of māʿal); this word will recur as the Chronicler’s verdict on Saul in 10:13 — the exile of Judah and the death of Saul are both attributed to the same word-root, binding national history to royal failure under one interpretive category.
9.2: nᵉtînîm — ‘temple servants,’ literally ‘the given ones’ (Qal passive participle of nātan, to give). The form encodes the category: persons given over to temple service, likely descendants of captives assigned as support staff for the Levites (Num 31:47; Josh 9:27; Ezra 2:43-54). The term occurs 17× in Ezra-Nehemiah and 2× in Chronicles, marking a service class whose origins predate the exile but whose community identity is reconstituted in the post-exilic settlement. The listing of Israelites, priests, Levites, and Nethinim as four distinct groups reflects the Chronicler’s consistently stratified view of the worshipping community.
9.3: The enumeration of settlers from Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, and Manasseh in Jerusalem is theologically significant: Chronicles includes northern-tribe representation in the restored Jerusalem community. Samuel-Kings largely writes off the north after the 722 BCE Assyrian deportation; the Chronicler envisions a reunified Israel around the Davidic-Levitical center. The grammatical structure is a simple listing with wayyēšᵉḇû (waw-consecutive Qal imperfect of yāšaḇ, ‘to settle/dwell’) — the narrative past tense marking the settlement as a completed, definitive act.
9.4: A genealogical line from Perez son of Judah through five patronymic links to Uthai — the ben-chain establishes legitimacy of settlement for the post-exilic community. The Perez lineage is the Davidic sub-clan (Ruth 4:18-22; 1 Chr 2:4-15), giving this first settler special prestige. No linguistic variants from the parallel in Neh 11:4.
9.5: šîlōnî — the ‘Shilonites’ are descendants of Shelah son of Judah (1 Chr 2:3; Num 26:20), not Shiloh the city. The designation habbe̊kôr (‘the firstborn’) applied to Asaiah signals priority of standing within this clan.
9.6: bᵉnê zeraḥ — the Zerahite sub-clan of Judah (Zerah son of Judah, twin of Perez); Jeuel and kinsmen totaling 690. The Zerahites are the lineage of Achan/Achar (2:6-7), but here they appear as legitimate settlers — the genealogy includes even the troubled line.
9.7: Benjaminite genealogical line to Sallu: meshullām ben hōḏawyāh ben hassᵉnûʾāh — three patronymic links. Parallel in Neh 11:7 has slight variations (mᵉšullam ben yôʿēd ben pᵉdāʾyāh ben qôlāʾyāh ben maʿaśēyāh ben ʾîṯîʾēl ben yᵉšaʿyāh), suggesting either different individuals or textual variants in the transmission. The Chronicler’s shorter list may preserve an earlier form.
9.8: Three more Benjaminite settlers listed with genealogical depth: Ibneiah through Jeroham; Elah through Uzzi and Michri; Meshullam through a longer chain including Shephatiah, Reuel, and Ibnijah. The variety of lineage depths (one, two, four links) reflects varying completeness in the source registers rather than editorial inconsistency.
9.9: ʾālpayim šᵉlōšâ wᵉḥāmišîm wᵉtišʿāh — 956 Benjaminite heads of ancestral households. The summary phrase kol ʾēlleh ʾănāšîm rāʾšê ʾāḇôt (‘all these were heads of ancestral households’) closes the genealogical unit with its standard conclusion formula, marking completeness of the register.
9.10: The priestly roster begins with three names — Jedaiah, Jehoiarib, Jachin — without full genealogical chains. Jedaiah and Jehoiarib are the two most prominent priestly families in the post-exilic period (Neh 12:6; 1 Chr 24:7); Jehoiarib heads the first of the 24 priestly divisions (24:7). The brevity of this entry contrasts with the depth of Azariah’s genealogy in v11.
9.11: nāgîd bêt hāʾĕlōhîm — Azariah is identified as ‘the chief officer (nāgîd) of the house of God.’ The term nāgîd (leader, ruler, prince) is the Chronicler’s preferred title for the highest-ranking temple official — the same word used of David as YHWH’s designated king (1 Chr 17:7). The long genealogical chain (six links back to Zadok) establishes Azariah’s Zadokite legitimacy — the Zadokite priesthood had the exclusive claim to temple service in the Chronicler’s (and Ezekiel’s) theology (Ezek 40:46; 44:15).
9.12: Adaiah son of Jeroham with four links; Maasai son of Adiel with three links. The genealogical variety and different chain lengths likely reflects different source documents with varying levels of completeness. The Neh 11:12-14 parallel preserves different versions of some of these names.
9.13: gibbôrê ḥayil mᵉlāʾḵat bêt hāʾĕlōhîm — ‘capable men for the service/work of the house of God.’ The phrase gibbôrê ḥayil (men of valor/capable men) is usually military terminology (Judg 11:1; 1 Sam 16:18); its application to priestly service here underscores that temple work is treated with the same gravity as military service. Total: 1,760 — a substantial priestly corps for the restored community.
9.14: The Levitical roster opens with Shemaiah son of Hasshub through to Hashabiah — assigned from the sons of Merari. The three Levitical clans (Gershon, Kohath, Merari) each provide settlers; Merari appears first here, which the Chronicler likely follows from his source registers without editorial significance.
9.15: Four Levites listed: Bakbakkar, Heresh, Galal, and Mattaniah — the last with a four-link chain through Micah and Zichri to Asaph. Asaph is one of the three great Levitical music guilds (with Heman and Jeduthun/Ethan); the Asaphite line is particularly prominent in Chronicles and in Psalm superscriptions.
9.16: Obadiah son of Shemaiah through Galal to Jeduthun — another guild connection (Jeduthun/Ethan is the third guild founder). Berechiah son of Asa son of Elkanah — note ʾĕlqānāh, the name also borne by Samuel’s father (1 Sam 1:1), situating this Levite in the same tribal and vocational stream. Their residence in ḥaṣrê nᵉṭôpātî (‘the villages of the Netophathites’) reflects the Levitical settlement pattern in Judahite towns (Neh 12:28).
9.17: šōʿᵃrîm — ‘gatekeepers,’ literally ‘those who keep the gate’ (participle of šāʿar). The four names — Shallum, Akkub, Talmon, Ahiman — with their kinsmen form the core gatekeeper corps. rōʾš — Shallum is ‘the chief’ (head) of the gatekeepers; the same title will reappear in v26 for the four ‘chief gatekeepers’ (hāšōʿᵃrîm lāʾarbaʿ). The Chronicler devotes more attention to the gatekeepers than any other biblical source, reflecting their post-exilic institutional importance.
9.18: šaʿar hammelek qēḏmāh — ‘the king’s gate to the east.’ The Eastern Gate was the primary royal access to the temple mount; ‘formerly’ (ʿad hēnnāh, literally ‘until now’ or ‘up to this point’) signals continuity from the pre-exilic gatekeeper establishment into the post-exilic restoration — the same families, at the same gates. maḥᵃnôt bᵉnê lēwî — ‘the Levitical camps’: the wilderness camp arrangement (Num 2:17; 3:21-38) is invoked to establish the Levitical gatekeeping tradition as Mosaic in origin.
9.19: šōmᵉrê sippôt hāʾōhel — ‘guardians of the thresholds of the tent.’ The word sap/sippāh (threshold, doorstep) is used of the temple threshold with particular solemnity (Ezek 9:3; 10:4); it is the boundary between the holy and the profane. The Korahites are specifically identified: Shallum son of Kore son of Ebiasaph son of Korah — the same lineage as Ps 84:10 (‘I would rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my God than dwell in the tents of wickedness’), where the Korahite authorship of the Psalms of Korah is itself a statement of Korahite identity as threshold-servants. The phrase kaʾăḇôtêhem (‘as their fathers had been’) is the key word: gatekeeping is inherited priestly vocation, not appointment.
9.20: pînᵉḥās ben ʾelʿāzār nāgîd ʿălêhem lᵉpānîm — ‘Phinehas son of Eleazar was formerly their chief/leader’ (nāgîd, the same term used for temple leaders in v11). The additional phrase YHWH ʿimmô — ‘and YHWH was with him’ — is the highest approbation formula in the Deuteronomistic and Chronicler’s vocabulary (used of Joshua, David, and Solomon with the same language); it validates the entire Korahite gatekeeper tradition by anchoring it to the most celebrated priestly act in Israelite history (Phinehas’s zeal at Peor, Num 25:7-13; Ps 106:30-31).
9.21: Zechariah son of Meshelemiah at the entrance of the ʾōhel môʿēd — ‘the tent of meeting,’ the technical term for the wilderness tabernacle. The use of this archaic designation (rather than ‘house of God’) situates the post-exilic gatekeeper function within the continuous trajectory from wilderness to temple — a characteristic Chronicler rhetorical move. This Zechariah will appear again as one of the lead gatekeepers in 1 Chr 26:2,14.
9.22: mithyaḥᵃsîm — ‘enrolled in their genealogical records,’ the same Hithpael of yāḥas as v1 — the chapter is framed by the verb of genealogical self-enrollment. Total: 212. dāwid wîšᵉmûʾēl hārōʾeh — ‘David and Samuel the rōʾeh (seer) established them.’ The title rōʾeh (seer, from rāʾāh, to see) is the archaic term for a prophet (1 Sam 9:9 explicitly glosses it: ‘the one now called a prophet was formerly called a seer’). The Chronicler designates Samuel as hārōʾeh — not hannāḇîʾ — preserving the older title; Samuel’s own books use both. The claim that David and Samuel jointly ‘established’ (yissᵉdûm, Piel of yāsaḏ, to found/establish) the gatekeeping posts roots the institution in the two figures who stand at the hinge of Israelite history.
9.23: šaʿarê bêt YHWH lᵉbêt hāʾōhel — ‘the gates of the house of YHWH — the house of the tent.’ The phrase apposits the Solomonic temple (‘house of YHWH’) with the wilderness tabernacle (‘the tent’) — the Chronicler’s standard collapsing of temporal distance between the two sanctuaries to assert continuity. lᵉmišmārôt (by guard-stations, by rotations) is the organization principle for all temple service in Chronicles (see 1 Chr 24-26).
9.24: Gatekeepers at the four compass points: mизrāḥ yāmāh ṣāpôn negeḇ — east, west, north, south. The four-direction scheme places YHWH’s house at the cosmological center. The gatekeeper arrangement mirrors the tribal encampment around the tabernacle (Num 2), where each direction had its assigned tribes — the temple is the wilderness sanctuary translated into permanent architecture.
9.25: ʾaḥêhem — ‘their fellow Levites’ in villages came to serve šiḇʿat hayyāmîm — ‘every seven days.’ The seven-day rotation for the subsidiary gatekeepers from surrounding villages reflects the same principle as the priestly divisions (24 courses, each serving one week twice per year). The verb yāḇôʾû (Qal imperfect, iterative sense) marks repeated action — this is the ongoing pattern of temple staffing.
9.26: gᵉḇîrîm — ‘the four chief gatekeepers were entrusted.’ The word gᵉḇîrîm (great men, men of strength) indicates status within the gatekeeper corps. bāṭûaḥ — ‘entrusted’ (Qal passive participle of bāṭaḥ, to trust/rely); the entrusting of the storerooms and treasuries to the chief gatekeepers reflects an institutional trust-arrangement with formal accountability.
9.27: wayyālînû — ‘they lodged the night around the house of God’ (Qal imperfect, narrative past). The Levitical night-watch at the temple is attested in Ps 134:1 (‘all you servants of YHWH who stand in the house of YHWH by night’) — a Korahite psalm likely connected to this gatekeeping institution. ûlāhem miftaḥ — ‘to them belonged the opening’ of the temple every morning; the gatekeepers had exclusive custody of the keys and the first access — and the accountability.
9.28: wᵉqāṣṯ mēhem ʿal kᵉlê hāʿăḇōḏāh — ‘Some of them were over the vessels of service.’ The detailed accounting system — vessels counted in and out (bᵉmispār yᵉḇîʾûm ûbᵉmispār yôṣîʾûm) — reflects the stringent temple accountability recorded in Ezra 1:9-11, where Cyrus’s vessels are inventoried precisely. The Chronicler’s interest in temple vessels as sacred objects requiring careful custody appears repeatedly (1 Chr 28:13-14; 2 Chr 36:7).
9.29: The category of those appointed over furnishings and holy vessels (kᵉlê haqqōḏeš) includes oversight of fine flour, wine, oil, incense, and aromatic spices — all the consumable elements of the daily grain offerings, wine libations, and incense. The Levitical management of these commodities reflects a developed temple economy requiring specialized knowledge of ritual quantities and purity standards.
9.30: wûmin bᵉnê hakkōhᵃnîm — ‘from among the sons of the priests’ some prepared the spice blend (mirsaḥat habbe̊šāmîm). The spice/incense preparation was specifically a priestly — not Levitical — task; Exod 30:34-38 prescribes the incense formula and prohibits its preparation or use by anyone other than priests for the altar. This verse marks the boundary between Levitical and priestly administrative domains.
9.31: ʾemûnāh — ‘trusted responsibility’ or ‘faithfulness’ (from ʾāman, to be firm, faithful). Mattithiah’s role over the flat cakes (maḥᵃḇattôt) is described as bᵉʾemûnāh — with faithfulness, in a position of trust. The Chronicler’s emphasis on institutional faithfulness (ʾemûnāh) in temple service reflects the same value that Hezekiah will apply to his treasury oversight (2 Chr 31:12,15,18) — the temple economy requires moral as well as ritual fidelity.
9.32: The Kohathite kinsmen responsible for the rows of bread (leḥem hammaʿărāḵet, ‘showbread,’ literally ‘bread of the arrangement/rows’) — the twelve loaves set out before YHWH each Sabbath (Lev 24:5-9). The twice-weekly change of showbread required careful preparation; this verse identifies a specific Kohathite sub-family holding hereditary responsibility for it.
9.33: hāmᵉšōrᵉrîm rāʾšê ʾāḇôt halᵉwiyyîm — ‘These are the singers, heads of Levitical clans.’ pᵉtûrîm ʿal kol ʾăḇōḏāh — ‘exempt from all other service.’ The word pāṭar (to release, exempt) is relatively rare; the singers’ exemption from the other Levitical administrative duties reflects the Chronicler’s elevation of temple music as primary, not supplementary, worship. They are day-and-night (layl wᵉyômām ʿalêhem bᵉmᵉlāʾḵāh) — the continuous nature of the singers’ service establishes the temple as a place of unceasing worship.
9.34: The closing summary formula for the Levitical leaders: rāʾšê hāʾāḇôt lalwiyyîm lᵉtôlᵉdôtām rāʾšîm — ‘heads of the Levitical ancestral houses throughout their generations, leaders.’ The phrase yāšᵉḇû bîrûšālāim (‘they lived in Jerusalem’) closes the settler list and serves as a geographical anchor — these Levitical institutions are Jerusalem-based, not provincial.
9.35: The Saul genealogy (vv35-44) is a near-verbatim repetition of 8:29-38, where it was the conclusion of the Benjaminite genealogy. Its reappearance here is a literary technique — the genealogy serves as a runway into the narrative of Saul’s death in ch10. The founder of Gibeon, yᵉʿîʾēl (Jeiel), is introduced with his wife Maacah; Gibeon will be significant as the site of the tabernacle and high place in David’s era (1 Chr 21:29; 2 Chr 1:3).
9.36: The sons of Jeiel/Gibeon: Abdon the firstborn, then five more names including Kish, Ner, and Nadab. The key names in this list are Ner (father of Abner, commander of Saul’s army) and Kish (father of Saul — v39). The genealogy is working toward the explanation of Saul’s lineage from the founder of Gibeon.
9.37: Four more sons: Gedor, Ahio, Zechariah, Mikloth. Ahio appears in 13:7 as one of the men guiding the cart carrying the ark — a member of the Gibeah community that has a role in the subsequent ark narrative. The name connections between genealogy and narrative are characteristic Chronicler technique.
9.38: Mikloth fathered Shimeam (šimʾām); the note that ‘they also lived in Jerusalem across from their kinsmen’ (wᵉhēm ʾap hēm negeḏ ʾaḥêhem yāšᵉḇû bîrûšālaim ʿim ʾaḥêhem) indicates this Benjaminite branch maintained connection to the Jerusalem community alongside their Gibeah base — a feature that perhaps explains their survival through the Saul catastrophe.
9.39: wᵉnēr hôlîd ʾet qîš wᵉqîš hôlîd ʾet šāʾûl — the genealogical chain reaches its destination: Ner → Kish → Saul. The Hiphil hôlîd (‘begat/fathered’) is the standard genealogical verb. Saul’s four sons are listed: Jonathan, Malchishua, Abinadab, and Eshbaal — note that ʾeš bāʿal (‘man of Baal’) is the Chronicler’s form; Samuel uses îš bōšet (‘man of shame,’ a polemical substitution for the Baal element). The Chronicler retains the original theophoric form without the Deuteronomistic bowdlerization.
9.40: mᵉrîḇ bāʿal — Jonathan’s son is called ‘Meribbaal’ here; Samuel uses ‘Mephibosheth.’ Both the Chronicler’s form (mᵉrîḇ, ‘the one who contends with Baal’ or ‘Baal pleads’) and Samuel’s substitution (mᵉpîḇōšet, ‘from the mouth of shame’) preserve the theophoric element awkwardly — the Chronicler avoids the euphemism, consistent with his retention of Eshbaal in v39.
9.41: The sons of Micah son of Meribbaal: Pithon, Melech, and Tahrea — three names only; 1 Chr 8:35 adds a fourth (‘Ahaz’) while 9:41 has three. This is a textual variant between the two versions of the same genealogy within Chronicles itself, suggesting the source documents had slightly different traditions. The Masoretes supply ‘Ahaz’ in the margin but the BHS text of 9:41 lacks it.
9.42: The descent from Ahaz: ʾāḥāz hôlîd ʾet yāʿrāh — ‘Ahaz fathered Jarah.’ Note the name yāʿrāh (Jarah) here versus yehôʿaddāh in 8:36 — another variant between the two genealogical forms, again pointing to divergent source traditions rather than authorial error. The chain Jarah → Alemeth, Azmaveth, Zimri → Moza continues the Saulide line past the dynasty’s political death.
9.43: Moza fathered Binea; then rᵉpāyāh (Rephaiah) → ʾelʿāsāh (Eleasah) → āṣēl (Azel). The three-generation chain from Binea to Azel continues the Saulide genealogy six generations past Saul himself — into the post-exilic period, suggesting the Saulide line survived and was tracked by the Chronicler’s sources even though it held no political power after David.
9.44: Azel’s six sons conclude both the genealogy and chapter 9. The closing formula ʾēlleh bᵉnê āṣēl (‘these are the sons of Azel’) mirrors the chapter’s opening genealogical register. The Saulide line continues beyond the catastrophe narrated in ch10 — the genealogy provides the frame of survival around the narrative of death. Exactly the same list appears in 8:38.

10.1: wayyinnāgᵉpû yiśrāʾēl — ‘And Israel was struck/defeated’ (Niphal of nāgaph). The Niphal passive frames Israel as the recipient of military calamity, not the agent — YHWH stands behind the defeat. wayyippᵉlû ḥᵃlālîm — ‘falling as slain/pierced ones’ (ḥālāl, the slain in battle, often with connotations of dishonor). har haggîlbōaʿ — Mount Gilboa is the site; the Chronicler’s version of the battle (parallel to 1 Sam 31) is briefer, omitting the prior night at Endor (1 Sam 28), because the Chronicler’s purpose is the theological verdict in vv13-14, not the narrative details.
10.2: wayyadbᵉqû pᵉlištîm — ‘the Philistines pressed close after/clung to Saul’ (Qal imperfect of dāḇaq, usually used for covenant loyalty, as in Ruth 1:14 or Deut 4:4). The ironic use of dāḇaq for the enemy’s pursuit — the word that describes righteous clinging to YHWH (Deut 10:20; 11:22) — is perhaps not intentional but sharpens the scene: Saul is overtaken by those who ‘cling’ to him in violence. Jonathan, Abinadab, Malchishua — the three sons who die with their father.
10.3: wattiḵbad hammiḥlāmāh ʾel šāʾûl — ‘the battle pressed hard against Saul’ (literally ‘was heavy’; the idiom of kāḇēḏ for intensive or burdensome pressure). wayyimṣāʾûhû hammorîm — ‘the archers found/overtook him.’ The root māṣāʾ (to find) used of the archers reaching Saul is suggestive: Saul has been ‘found out,’ located in his moment of abandonment. wayyāḥel mēhammôrîm — ‘and he was wounded by the archers’ (Qal imperfect of ḥûl, to writhe, be in anguish, or in this context to be wounded/pierced).
10.4: hāʾărālîm hāʾēlleh — ‘these uncircumcised ones.’ The word ʿārēl (uncircumcised) is the ethnic-covenantal boundary marker used throughout Samuel for Philistines as the quintessential covenant outsiders. Saul’s fear is that they will hitʿallᵉlû bô — ‘abuse/mock/make sport of him’ (Hithpael of ʿālal); the same verb is used of Israel’s abuse by Egypt (Exod 10:2). wayyirrā mallʾōd — ‘and he was very afraid’ (Qal imperfect of yārēʾ). The armor-bearer’s refusal to kill the king (lōʾ ʾāḇāh, ‘he was not willing’) reflects the inviolability of the royal person — even in extremis, striking the anointed is unthinkable (cf. David’s repeated refusals, 1 Sam 24:6; 26:9).
10.5: The armor-bearer fell on his own sword after Saul — the same word wayyāmot (‘and he died’) for both, the king and his servant. The Chronicler’s terse account omits the detail that Saul fell on his own sword first (1 Sam 31:4); the Chronicler says simply wayyiqaḥ haḥereḇ wayyippōl ʿālêhā — ‘and he took the sword and fell upon it.’ The armor-bearer’s identical death (wayyāmot) is noted without further comment — he followed his king in death as in service.
10.6: wayyāmot šāʾûl ûšᵉlōšet bānāw — ‘So Saul died, and his three sons died.’ The Chronicler adds wᵉkol bêtô yaḥdāw — ‘and all his household together’ — a phrase not in 1 Sam 31:6, which says instead ‘and all his men.’ The Chronicler’s ‘all his household’ underscores the totality of the dynastic collapse; it echoes the language of divine judgment against faithless houses in Kings (1 Kgs 21:22; 2 Kgs 9:8). The Meribbaal/Mephibosheth narrative is thus largely absent from Chronicles because the Chronicler has written the Saulide household as extinct — the ch9 genealogy preserving the line is presented as post-narratival continuation.
10.7: wāyaʿazᵉḇû ʾet ʿārêhem wayyānusû — ‘they abandoned their cities and fled.’ The verb ʿāzaḇ (to abandon, forsake) is significant: the same verb used of Israel’s forsaking of YHWH (Deut 28:20; 1 Chr 28:9) is here applied to Israel’s abandonment of their cities — the two abandonments are related in the Deuteronomistic-Chronicler theology. wayyāḇōʾû ḥaplištîm wayyēšᵉḇû bāhem — ‘the Philistines came and settled in them’; the invader’s occupation of abandoned cities is the concrete embodiment of the covenant curse (Deut 28:30-33).
10.8: lᵉpōšēṭ ḥᵃlālîm — ‘to strip the slain’ (literally ‘to strip those pierced/fallen’). The stripping of fallen warriors for their weapons and valuables was standard ANE battlefield practice; the same act performed on Saul’s body is a desecration because of his royal status. The Philistines ‘found’ (wayyimṣᵉʾû) Saul and his sons — the same māṣāʾ (to find) as in v3, framing the entire battle sequence: the archers ‘found’ Saul in the battle, the Philistines ‘found’ his body on the field.
10.9: wayYāsîrû ʾet rōʾšô — ‘they stripped him, took his head.’ Decapitation of a fallen enemy was a trophy act with deep ANE symbolism (David took Goliath’s head, 1 Sam 17:54). lᵉḇaśśēr ʾet ʿaṣabbêhem — ‘to bring the good news (bāśar) to their idols (ʿaṣabbêhem).’ The word ʿaṣāḇ for idols is pejorative (the same root as ‘pain/toil’ — Ps 16:4: ‘those who run after another god multiply their sorrows [ʿaṣṣᵉḇōtām]’). The irony is precise: the Philistines bring gospel-news (bāśar, the positive announcement) to objects of grief — the victory news delivered to pain-objects.
10.10: bêt ʾĕlōhêhem — ‘the house of their gods.’ The Chronicler places the armor in the general ‘temple of their gods’ and Saul’s head ‘in the temple of Dagon’ (bêt dāgôn). 1 Sam 31:10 reverses the distribution — armor in ‘the house of Ashtaroth,’ body fastened to the wall of Beth-shan. The Chronicler’s focus on Dagon is theologically pointed: Dagon is the god before whom the captured ark humiliated itself (1 Sam 5:1-5), and now the Philistines hang Saul’s head in Dagon’s house — the same temple that will eventually bear witness to the ark’s power. The theological juxtaposition is part of the Chronicler’s consistent anti-idolatry polemic.
10.11: wayyišmᵉʿû kol yōšᵉḇê yāḇêš gilʿāḏ — ‘when all the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead heard.’ The men of Jabesh-gilead have a special loyalty to Saul: he rescued them from Nahash the Ammonite at the beginning of his reign (1 Sam 11:1-11), and they repay that loyalty now. The Chronicler does not explain this bond — it is assumed the reader knows the backstory from Samuel.
10.12: kol gibbôrê ḥāyil — ‘all the valiant men’ — the same phrase used for capable priestly workers in 9:13. Here it describes the Jabesh-gilead warriors who make the night journey to retrieve Saul’s body: wayyiqqᵉḥû ʾet gūpat šāʾûl — ‘they took the corpse (gûpāh) of Saul.’ ûyqibrû ʾet ʿaṣmôtêhem — ‘they buried their bones.’ The shift from ‘bodies’ (1 Sam 31:12: ʾet gᵉwiyyātō) to ‘bones’ in Chronicles may reflect a later stage (bones after burning/decomposition), or a textual variant. The oak at Jabesh (hāʾēlāh) serves as a funerary marker — trees as burial markers are attested in Gen 35:8 (Deborah buried under the ‘oak of weeping’).
10.13: wayyāmot šāʾûl bᵉmaʿᵃlô — ‘Saul died because of his unfaithfulness (maʿal).’ This is the Chronicler’s unique contribution over 1 Samuel — the entire death narrative has been building to this theological verdict. Māʿal (unfaithfulness, covenant breach, treachery) is the Chronicler’s signature evaluative term: it appears 24 times in Chronicles versus only 3 times in the parallel Samuel-Kings material. It denotes specifically the violation of the sacred, a betrayal of covenant with YHWH that carries corporate consequences. Three specific acts of maʿal are identified: (1) ʾăšer māʿal bîdbar YHWH — not keeping YHWH’s command (the Amalekite disobedience, 1 Sam 15); (2) wᵉgam lišʾôl bāʾôḇ lîdrôš — consulting a medium to seek guidance (dāraš); and (3) implicitly from v14, failing to seek YHWH directly. The word māʿal brackets both the Saul death narrative and the exile summary of v1 (bᵉmaʿalām) — exile and royal death share the same root cause.
10.14: wᵉlōʾ dāraš bîYHWH — ‘and he did not seek YHWH’s guidance.’ The verb dāraš (to seek, inquire, require) is the Chronicler’s evaluative criterion for kings: those who dāraš YHWH are blessed (Asa, 2 Chr 14:4; Jehoshaphat, 17:3; Hezekiah, 31:21; Josiah, 34:3); those who do not are judged. Samuel-Kings uses the verb occasionally; the Chronicler systematizes it as the primary criterion of royal faithfulness. The specific charge here — Saul consulted a medium to seek (dāraš) guidance instead of seeking YHWH — places the medium consultation not merely as a ritual violation but as the misuse of the seeking-faculty itself. wayyᵉmîtēhû — ‘therefore he [YHWH] put him to death’ — YHWH is the subject of Saul’s death; the Philistines are the instrument but YHWH is the agent. wayYassēb ʾet hamᵉlûḵāh lᵉdāwid ben yišay — ‘and turned the kingdom to David son of Jesse’ — the kingship transfer is divine action (sāḇaḇ, Hiphil, to turn/redirect), not merely political succession. The chapter ends not with Saul but with David — the dynasty toward which the Chronicler has been orienting the entire genealogical preface.

21.1: The census narrative’s opening differs in one critical word from its Samuel parallel: where 2 Sam 24:1 reads wayyōsep ʾap YHWH laḥărōt bᵉyiśrāʾēl wayyāset ʾet dāwîḏ bāhem, ‘again the anger of YHWH was kindled against Israel, and he incited David against them,’ the Chronicler writes wayyaʿamod śāṭān ʿal yiśrāʾēl wayyāset ʾet dāwîḏ limpōr ʾet yiśrāʾēl, ‘and Satan stood up against Israel and incited David to number Israel.’ The Chronicles version substitutes śāṭān for the divine agency of Samuel. The word śāṭān appears here without the definite article (unlike Job 1-2 and Zech 3:1-2 where it is haśśāṭān, ‘the adversary’) — some read it as a proper name, others as the noun ‘an adversary.’ The substitution reflects a theological development in the post-exilic period: the distinction between divine sovereignty (YHWH ultimately permits) and proximate agency (a hostile adversarial being incites) that the book of Job articulates and that becomes more developed in 2nd Temple Judaism. The Chronicler does not contradict Samuel: YHWH remains sovereign (the plague is YHWH’s judgment; the staying of the plague is YHWH’s mercy, 21:15). The Chronicler disaggregates the causation that Samuel telescopes. The trajectory from haśśāṭān in Zechariah (3:1-2, contemporary with the Chronicler’s era) to the NT’s diabolos and satanas passes through this terminological shift.
21.22: David’s request to purchase the threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite — tᵉnāh lî mᵉqôm haggōren, ‘give me the place of the threshing floor’ — establishes the site that 2 Chr 3:1 will identify as Mount Moriah: wayyāḥel šᵉlōmōh liḇnôt ʾet bêt YHWH bîrûšālaim bᵉhar hammōriyyāh, ‘Solomon began to build the house of YHWH in Jerusalem on Mount Moriah.’ The Chronicler makes explicit what the Samuel account leaves implicit: the threshing floor purchased under plague-judgment is the same mountain as the Moriah of Gen 22:2, where YHWH commanded Abraham, lᵉḵ lᵉḵā ʾel ʾereṣ hammōriyyāh wᵉhaʿălēhû šām lᵉʿōlāh, ‘go to the land of Moriah, and offer him there as a burnt offering.’ The convergence is theologically loaded: the site where YHWH stayed Abraham’s knife and provided the substitute sacrifice (Gen 22:13-14), and where YHWH’s destroying angel was stayed (1 Chr 21:15-16), becomes the site of the permanent temple where sacrifice is offered in perpetuity. The ram caught in the thicket (Gen 22), the plague-stayed angel over the threshing floor (1 Chr 21), and the daily burnt offerings on the temple altar (2 Chr 2:4) form a single sacrificial sequence. The name YHWH-yireh (‘YHWH will provide’, Gen 22:14) is the theology of the site — a provision that the NT reads as anticipating the ultimate sacrifice (John 1:29; Heb 10:5-10).
21.2: śᵉʾû ("count, muster") is the census verb; mibᵉʾēr šebaʿ ʿad-dān ("from Beersheba to Dan") reverses the conventional south-to-north idiom — the Chronicler writes from a Jerusalem-centered perspective rather than the northern vantage of the Deuteronomistic History. Joab and the "army commanders" (śārê hāʿām) are the census agents; the military framing reveals David's purpose — this is a war-preparation census rather than a Mosaic cultic numbering.
21.3: Joab's objection deploys a formal wish-formula: yôsep YHWH ʿal-ʿammô meʾâ pᵉʿāmîm ("may YHWH add to his people a hundredfold") — prayer and protest simultaneously. The rhetorical question ʾăḏōnî hammelek ʾăḥîhem ("are they not all, my lord the king, his servants?") frames the census as a category error: YHWH's people cannot be numbered as a military asset. The verb ʾāšām (incur guilt) at the end carries full legal weight — Joab predicts cultic violation, not just political miscalculation.
21.4: wayyiḥzaq dᵉḇar-hammelek ʿal-yôʾāḇ — "the king's word prevailed over Joab" — the verb ḥzq (be strong, overpower) signals inappropriate coercion. David's royal command overrides prophetic-priestly caution; this is the Chronicler's structural explanation for why discipline followed: leadership failed to heed legitimate counsel. wayyēleḵ yôʾāḇ wayᵉhî bᵉḵol-yiśrāʾēl wayyāšāḇ yᵉrûšālim — the census circuit encompassed all Israel before reporting back, suggesting weeks of travel.
21.5: The numbers differ significantly from 2 Sam 24:9 (800,000 Israel / 500,000 Judah vs. 1,100,000 / 470,000 here). Chronicles' higher Israel figure may reflect a different counting methodology (including military reserves or all eligible males), or may preserve a variant source. The Chronicler does not harmonize the discrepancy; both texts stand in the canon as complementary witnesses. šōlēp ḥereḇ ("able to wield a sword") confirms the military census framing — this is warrior-counting, not Mosaic covenant numbering.
21.6: Levi and Benjamin's exclusion from the census is the Chronicler's addition to the Samuel parallel. wᵉlēwî wᵉḇinyāmîn lōʾ pāqad bᵉtôḵām — Levi was never numbered in Mosaic censuses (Num 1:49; 2:33); Benjamin's exclusion may reflect the fact that the future temple site at Ornan's threshing floor lay within Benjamin's tribal territory (Josh 18:28), making Benjamin's people the custodians of sacred space rather than military conscripts. Joab's act of mercy toward these two tribes foreshadows the temple-centered restoration of both.
21.7: wayyēraʿ bᵉʿênê hāʾĕlōhîm — "it was evil/displeasing in the eyes of God." The narrator's use of generic hāʾĕlōhîm here rather than the covenant name YHWH may mark this as a straightforward moral evaluation before the personal covenant dimension emerges in God's disciplinary response. wayyak ʾet-yiśrāʾēl — "he struck Israel" — the collective punishment falls on the people David numbered, not only on David. Covenantal solidarity means the nation bears the consequence of its leader's hubris.
21.8: David's confession: ḥāṭāʾtî mᵉʾōd ("I have sinned greatly") uses the intensifying adverb mᵉʾōd to mark the gravity of the admission. The petition haʿăḇēr nāʾ ʾet-ʿăwōn ʿaḇdᵉḵā uses ʿāḇar ("cause to pass over") for forgiveness — a spatial metaphor for removing guilt from one's account. kiskalatî mᵉʾōd — "I have acted very foolishly" — sāḵal refers to foolishness that causes harm, the wisdom-tradition antonym of understanding. David recognizes his failure as both moral sin and practical folly.
21.10: YHWH's three-choice oracle delivered through Gad (ḥōzeh dāwîd — "David's seer") exemplifies the prophetic intermediary role in Chronicles: YHWH communicates discipline options rather than simply imposing punishment unilaterally. The phrase šālaš ʾānī nōṭeh ʿāleḵā ("I am stretching/extending three things toward you") — nāṭâ describes both extending a hand in offer and extending a hand in punishment, creating a deliberate ambiguity: choosing is itself an act within God's sovereign frame.
21.11: Gad's enumeration begins with bāḥar-lᵉḵā ʾaḥat mēhem — "choose one of them for yourself" — the language of personal selection. The three options — famine, military defeat, plague — correspond precisely to the covenant curse triad of Deut 28 and Lev 26 (sword, famine, pestilence), though reversed in order. The census sin against the covenant people is answered by covenant-curse options; discipline fits the offense structurally.
21.12: šāloš šānîm rāʿāb (three years famine) / šᵉlōšâ ḥŏdāšîm (three months before enemies' sword) / šᵉlōšet yāmîm ḥereḇ YHWH wᵉdeḇer (three days YHWH's sword and plague) — the three options scale inversely: more human control (famine, long but survivable) → less human control (war, determined by enemies) → no human control (divine plague, the briefest but most total). The interplay of timescales creates a theology of divine initiative: YHWH's direct hand moves fastest and most decisively.
21.13: David's response is the theological heart of the chapter: naple nāʾ bᵉyad-YHWH kî-raḇbîm raḥămāyw — "let me fall into YHWH's hand, for his mercies are great." raḥămîm (mercies, plural of intensity) is the compassion-word rooted in reḥem (womb) — YHWH's mercies are maternal, tender, and abundant. The opposite refusal — ûḇᵉyad-ʾādām ʾal-ʾeppōl ("but let me not fall into human hands") — reflects the wisdom that human enemies show less mercy than the God whose character is defined by covenant faithfulness (ḥesed and raḥămîm).
21.14: wayyittēn YHWH deḇer bᵉyiśrāʾēl — the plague falls immediately and directly. šiḇʿîm ʾelep ʾîš (seventy thousand men) — precisely those counted in the census die, an ironic fulfillment of Joab's warning. The act of counting them for military purposes ends with those counted removed from Israel's military potential. The Chronicler's narrative insists that national strength flows from covenant fidelity, not demographic calculation.
21.15: The theophany at Jerusalem is one of Chronicles' most dramatic scenes: wayyišlaḥ hāʾĕlōhîm malʾāḵ lîrûšālim lᵉšaḥătāh — God sends an angel to destroy Jerusalem. Then the pivot: wayyinnāḥem YHWH ʿal-hārāʿâ — YHWH relented/repented of the disaster; nāḥam is the strongest anthropomorphic term for divine change of emotional state. The command rab-lāk ʿᵊtāh haref yādᵉḵā ("Enough! Now stay your hand") is spoken by YHWH directly to the angel — a command within the divine council that reverses the destruction. The threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite is identified as the site where the angel stands.
21.16: wayyiśśāʾ dāwîd ʾet-ʿênāyw wayyarᵉʾ ʾet-malʾak YHWH ʿōmēd bên hāʾāreṣ ûḇên haššāmayim wᵉḥarḇô šᵉlûpâ bᵉyādô — David sees the angel standing between earth and heaven, sword drawn, extended over Jerusalem. The spatial positioning — between spheres — marks the angel as a liminal figure operating at the intersection of divine and human realms. David and the elders respond with full covenant penitence: wayyipᵉlû ʿal-pᵉnêhem (fell on their faces) in śaqqîm (sackcloth) — the full mourning posture.
21.17: David's second confession explicitly inverts the guilt structure: ʾănî hûʾ ʾăšer ḥāṭāʾtî wᵉhārēaʿ hārēʿōtî — "I am the one who sinned and acted wickedly" — hārēʿōtî is infinitive absolute + perfect for emphatic confirmation of guilt. The contrast hāʾēlleh haṣṣōʾn mah ʿāśû ("these sheep — what have they done?") employs the shepherding metaphor (David as shepherd-king) to argue for redirecting punishment from people to king. The petition ûbi-yādᵉḵā tihi-nāʾ ʾet-yādᵉḵā bî — "let your hand be against me and my father's house" — offers self and dynastic substitution for the people's suffering.
21.18: The angel's instruction to Gad demonstrates the Chronicler's chain-of-command theology: YHWH → angel → prophet → king; yaʿal dāwîd lᵉhāqîm mizbēaḥ lYHWH ("David is to go up to build an altar to YHWH") transforms the site of the plague's arrest into the site of permanent sacrifice. The threshing floor (gōren ʾornān hayᵉḇûsî) is a liminal space — where harvest grain is separated from chaff — that becomes the dividing line between destruction and mercy, between the old Jebusite past and the Israelite sacred future.
21.19: wayyaʿal dāwîd biḏḇar-gāḏ ʾăšer dibber bᵉšem YHWH — "David went up in obedience to Gad's word, spoken in YHWH's name." The Chronicler's notation that the prophetic word was "spoken in YHWH's name" (bᵉšem YHWH) is a technical phrase for validated prophecy (cf. Deut 18:20–22). David's immediate obedience after his confession contrasts sharply with the fatal hesitation of the census — repentance here is demonstrated by action, not merely expressed in words.
21.21: The meeting of David and Ornan has formal protocols: Ornan saw David coming, emerged from the threshing floor (mitten-haggōren), and prostrated himself face-down (wayyištaḥû lᵉdāwîd ʾappayim ʾārᵉṣâ). The double gesture — exit from the floor + full prostration — marks the transfer of sacred space authority from the current occupant to the divinely-designated purchaser. Ornan's role as a Jebusite (a non-Israelite) who held the site that would become the temple mount is a subtle theological statement: YHWH's purposes can be served through any people's land transactions.
21.23: Ornan's extraordinary generosity — oxen for burnt offering, threshing sledges for wood, wheat for grain offering — offers to supply everything David needs for free. hakōl nātattî ("all of it I give") is emphatic. The verb nātan (give) signals the gift economy that Ornan proposes; David's counter-refusal (lōʾ-ʾeqqaḥ in v24) establishes the purchase economy as the only valid basis for worship. The theological principle is explicit: YHWH's altar cannot be built on someone else's generosity — the worshiper must personally bear the cost.
21.24: David's foundational statement: lōʾ-ʾeqqaḥ ʾăšer-lᵉḵā lYHWH wᵉhaʿălôt ʿōlôt ḥinnām — "I will not take what is yours for YHWH, or offer burnt offerings that cost me nothing." The word ḥinnām ("for free, at no cost") is the key term; it appears in prophetic indictments of meaningless worship (Mal 1:10) and here establishes that genuine worship requires genuine sacrifice. This principle governs the entire temple-building enterprise: David's preparations (ch22), Solomon's labor, the people's freewill offerings (1 Chr 29) — none of it is costless generosity; all of it is chosen sacrifice.
21.25: šēš-mēʾôt šeqel zāhāb miśqāl — six hundred shekels of gold by weight. The 2 Sam 24:24 parallel records fifty shekels of silver for the threshing floor and oxen alone; the Chronicles figure reflects purchase of the full temple site (a much larger area) and is thus not contradictory. Gold rather than silver signals the purchase's sacred significance — this is not a normal real-estate transaction but the acquisition of holy ground. The payment establishes legal title that no future Jebusite claim could challenge.
21.26: The divine response is unambiguous: wayyanswheû bāʾēš min-haššāmayim ʿal mizbah hāʿōlâ — "he answered him with fire from heaven on the altar of burnt offering." Fire from heaven is the sign of divine acceptance in the three great sanctuary inauguration moments: Moses' tabernacle (Lev 9:24), David's altar here, and Solomon's temple (2 Chr 7:1). The Chronicler deliberately echoes all three, constructing a theological chain: one divine fire, three sacred sites, one continuous story of YHWH dwelling with his people. The angel's sword sheathes in the same verse as the fire falls — destruction gives way to acceptance.
21.27: wayyōʾmer YHWH lammalʾāḵ wayyāšeḇ ḥarbô ʾel-nādānāhh — "the LORD commanded the angel, and he returned his sword to its sheath." The sheathing of the drawn sword (ḥereḇ šᵉlûpâ) that appeared in v16 creates a narrative bracket: plague began with drawn sword / ends with sheathed sword. The altar's dedication simultaneously ends the plague and establishes the permanent cult site — one act accomplishes two things: communal reconciliation with YHWH and the identification of the holy place.
21.28: The summary statement locates David's sacrifice at the Ornan threshing floor as a direct response to divine answer: kî rāʾâ kî-ʿānāhû YHWH bᵉgōren ʾornān hayᵉḇûsî — "because he saw that YHWH had answered him there." The theology is spatial: YHWH answered at this specific site, marking it as the locus of effective intercession. The threshing floor is no longer Ornan's economic space but YHWH's answer-site. Sacrifice at this location will henceforth be the normative response to YHWH's self-disclosure.
21.29: The parenthetical note about the tabernacle at Gibeon (ûmiškan YHWH ʾăšer-ʿāśâ mōšeh bammidbār wᵉmizbah hāʿōlâ bāʿēt hahîʾ bāmâ bᵉgiḇʿôn) serves a critical theological function: it explains why David could not simply go to the legitimate Mosaic worship center. The tabernacle and its altar were the authorized cult site, but the angel's presence at Jerusalem made that the effective worship location. The Chronicler is navigating a genuine liturgical tension — the legal center (Gibeon) vs. the providentially designated center (Jerusalem) — that will only be resolved when Solomon builds the temple and brings the ark and tabernacle furnishings together.
21.30: wᵉlōʾ-yāḵōl dāwîd lāleḵet lᵉḏārōš ʾĕlōhîm kî nibhal mippᵉnê ḥereḇ malʾak YHWH — "David could not go there to seek God because he was terrified before the sword of the angel of YHWH." nāḇal/bāhal (terrified, alarmed) captures genuine theophanic fear, not cowardice. The verse functions as the transition logic: David's inability to use the Mosaic altar at Gibeon is not disobedience but divine redirection. YHWH has effectively relocated authorized worship to the Jerusalem site through the theophany itself — the temple is built where divine fire fell and divine terror prevented departure.
21.9: waydabber YHWH ʾel-gāḏ ḥōzeh dāwîd — YHWH speaks directly to Gad, whom the Chronicler titles ḥōzeh dāwîd ("David's seer"), using the visionary prophet title (ḥōzeh) rather than the more common nāḇîʾ. The seer tradition (cf. 1 Sam 9:9) emphasizes visual revelation; Gad is the prophet whose role is to mediate YHWH's vision for what David cannot directly see. lēʾmōr ("saying") introduces the divine word that Gad must transmit — YHWH → Gad → David is the complete prophetic chain that governs the chapter's disciplinary structure.
21.20: wayyiphen ʾornān wayyarᵉʾ ʾet-hammalʾāḵ — Ornan turned and saw the angel; the verb pānâ (turn around) implies he was working with his back to the city when the angel appeared over Jerusalem. wᵉʾarḇaʿat bānāyw ʿimmô yitḥabbᵉʾû — "his four sons who were with him hid themselves" — ḥāḇāʾ (hide, take cover) reflects the instinctive theophanic fear response; ordinary humans conceal themselves before divine manifestations (cf. Exod 3:6, Moses hides his face; 1 Kgs 19:13, Elijah wraps his face). Ornan himself did not hide — he remained at his post, the owner of the site where heaven and earth were intersecting. The contrast between the hiding sons and the standing father sets up Ornan's composed response when David arrives in v21.

22.9: YHWH’s announcement of Solomon’s name to David — hinnēh bēn nôlāḏ lāḵ hûʾ yihyeh ʾîš mᵉnûḥāh wᵉhaniḥôtî lô miqqōl ʾōyᵉḇāyw missāḇîḇ kî šᵉlōmōh yihyeh šᵉmô wᵉšālôm wāšeqeṭ ʾettēn ʿal yiśrāʾēl bᵉyāmāyw, ‘Behold, a son shall be born to you who shall be a man of rest. I will give him rest from all his surrounding enemies. For his name shall be Solomon (šᵉlōmōh), and I will give peace (šālôm) and quiet to Israel in his days’ — makes the name-etymology explicit: Solomon is the peace-king. The name šᵉlōmōh derives from the root šālem/šālôm (wholeness, completeness, peace). The Chronicler’s emphasis on šālôm and mᵉnûḥāh (rest, repose) as Solomon’s defining gifts frames the temple-building as the culmination of Israel’s progress toward rest — from the wilderness wandering (no rest, no temple) through the conquest (partial rest, no temple) through David’s wars (not yet rest) to Solomon’s reign (rest, temple). Heb 4:9-10 invokes the same framework for the eschatological rest: ara apoleipetai sabbatismos tō laō tou theou; ho gar eiselthōn eis tēn katapausin autou kai autos katepausen apo tōn ergōn autou — ‘so then, there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God, for whoever has entered God’s rest has also rested from his works.’ Solomon’s šālôm-reign is the historical type of the eschatological rest; Jesus, son of David (Matt 1:1) and ‘greater than Solomon’ (Matt 12:42), is its fulfillment.
22.1: wayyōʾmer dāwîd zeh hûʾ bêt YHWH hāʾĕlōhîm wᵉzeh-mizbēaḥ lᵉʿōlâ lᵉyiśrāʾēl — David's declaratory sentence is the theological hinge of Chronicles. The demonstrative zeh ("this") is repeated: this place / this altar — a performative speech-act designating sacred geography. The threshing floor where fire fell and the sword sheathed is now officially identified as the permanent temple site and national altar. David does not ask for divine confirmation; the theophany of ch21 was itself the designation. He interprets the events with prophetic authority.
