22.2: gērîm (resident aliens, sojourners) were the labor pool conscripted for stone preparation. The Mosaic law protected gērîm from exploitation but did not prohibit their employment in public works; the Chronicler's matter-of-fact note about their assembly reflects the Persian-period reality that the Jerusalem temple community included non-Israelite workers integrated into the economic and social fabric of Yehud. David's procurement policy here parallels Solomon's later corvée labor organization (2 Chr 2:17–18), demonstrating planned consistency across the two reigns.
22.3: barzel lārōḇ lammismerôt daltôt haššᵉʿārîm wᵉlammaḥbᵉrôt — iron in great quantity for door-nails and joints; nᵉḥōšet lᵉʾên mišqāl — bronze beyond weighing. The enumeration of materials (iron/bronze/cedar/stone) follows the ANE royal building-account convention visible in Mesopotamian inscriptions where kings catalog their procurement to demonstrate both divine favor (abundance) and royal diligence (preparation). David's stockpiling occurs during his reign but is designated entirely for Solomon — an act of dynastic stewardship rather than personal glory.
22.4: Cedar from Sidon and Tyre was the prestige building material of the ancient Levant, unavailable in Israel's highlands. The Phoenician trade relationship established here by David (and formalized by Solomon with Hiram of Tyre in 2 Chr 2) reflects the economic reality that Israel's hill-country ecology could not supply the temple's architectural needs. The Chronicler's inclusion of this detail grounds the sacred project in practical geopolitics: the temple required international trade partnerships that transcended Israel's borders, foreshadowing its role as a house for all peoples.
22.5: David's explanation for his preparations is simultaneously humble and visionary: šᵉlōmōh bᵉnî naʿar wārāk ("my son Solomon is young and inexperienced/tender") — rāk means soft/gentle, sometimes with the nuance of vulnerability; naʿar marks life stage, not inability. The temple must be lᵉgādôl lᵉšēm wᵉlᵉtipʾeret lᵉḵol-hāʾărāṣôt — "magnificent in fame and glory to all lands" — a universal visibility claim. David's preparations are a form of protective provision: he removes the logistical burden from Solomon's shoulders so that the young king can focus on the spiritual dimensions of the task.
22.6: David's personal charge to Solomon begins with wayyiqrāʾ lišlōmōh bᵉnô — "he called his son Solomon" — an intimate summons preceding the formal commission. The content of the charge (vv7–16) follows the structure of a testamentary address: historical retrospective (vv7–8) → promise citation (vv9–10) → blessing (vv11) → conditional exhortation (vv12–13) → resource inventory (vv14–16). This is David's spiritual will, not merely a building brief.
22.7: hayyâ ʿim-lᵉḇāḇî liḇnôt bêt lᵉšēm YHWH ʾĕlōhāy — "it was in my heart to build a house for the name of YHWH my God." hayyâ ʿim-lᵉḇāḇî ("was with my heart") is the Chronicler's idiom for sincere personal desire (cf. 1 Kgs 8:17, Solomon's retrospective). Intention matters in Chronicles — David's failed intention is not erased by its non-fulfillment; it is honored by YHWH's alternative arrangement. The house "for the name" (lᵉšēm) reflects the Name Theology of Deuteronomy: YHWH's presence is mediated through his name dwelling in a designated place, not through physical embodiment.
22.8: YHWH's prohibition is stated with full solemnity: dāmîm lārōḇ šāpaḵtā — "bloods in abundance you have shed." dāmîm (plural) denotes blood-guilt — not merely the physical act of killing but the weight of responsibility for multiple deaths. milḥāmôt gᵉdōlôt ʿāśîtā — "you have waged great wars." The prohibition is not moral condemnation of David's wars per se (the Chronicler presents many of them as YHWH-commissioned) but a fitness-for-function judgment: the temple is a house of peace (šālôm), and its builder must embody that peace. War-making and temple-building require incompatible charisms.
22.10: hûʾ yiḇneh bêt lišmî wᵉhûʾ yihyeh-lî lᵉḇēn wᵉʾănî lō lᵉʾāḇ — the direct citation of 2 Sam 7:13–14 / the Davidic covenant. "He will build a house for my name and he will be my son and I will be his father" — the language of adoptive royal sonship from the Ancient Near Eastern treaty tradition where the great king describes his vassal-king as "son." The Chronicler quotes the covenant verbatim within David's speech, making the temple-building mandate a matter of covenant fulfillment rather than merely royal ambition. Solomon builds not because David wants it but because YHWH promised it.
22.11: David's blessing: YHWH ʿimmāḵ wᵉhiṣlaḥtā ûḇānîtā bêt YHWH ʾĕlōhêḵā kaʾăšer dibber ʿālêḵā — "may YHWH be with you so that you succeed and build the house of the LORD your God as he has spoken concerning you." The conditional structure — YHWH's presence enables success — is the Chronicler's fundamental historical principle. Every successful building or battle in Chronicles succeeds because YHWH was with the protagonist; every failure occurs in his absence. David's blessing is both paternal affection and theological instruction.
22.12: raq yittᵉn-lᵉḵā YHWH śēḵel ûḇînâ — "only may YHWH give you wisdom and understanding." śēḵel is practical wisdom/prudence; ḇînâ is discernment/insight. The paired terms echo Proverbs' wisdom vocabulary — the temple's construction requires the full complement of the wisdom tradition's intellectual virtues. The purpose clause — wᵉlišmōr ʾet-tôrat YHWH ʾĕlōhêḵā ("to keep the torah of YHWH your God") — makes wisdom instrumental to covenant faithfulness, not an end in itself. Solomon's later request for precisely this gift (2 Chr 1:10) fulfills David's blessing.
22.13: ʾāz taṣlîaḥ ʾim-tišmōr laʿăśôt ʾet-haḥuqqîm wᵉʾet-hammišpāṭîm — "then you will succeed if you carefully observe the statutes and ordinances." The conditional ʾāz/ʾim structure (then/if) reflects Deuteronomic covenant logic: success is not guaranteed by hereditary right or building quality but by Torah faithfulness. ḥăzaq wᵉʾĕmāṣ ʾal-tîrāʾ wᵉʾal-tēḥāt — "be strong and courageous, do not fear and do not be dismayed" — the Joshua commission (Josh 1:6–9) applied to the temple-builder: the spiritual courage required to maintain Torah is as formidable as the courage required to conquer Canaan.
22.14: bᵉʿonyî hăḵînôtî lᵉḇêt YHWH — "in my distress/affliction I have prepared for the house of the LORD." ʿonî (distress, affliction, poverty) is a surprising self-description from a king who is about to enumerate staggering resources; it acknowledges that David's wars, sins, and limitations are the context of his giving. The numbers are colossal: mēʾâ ʾelep kkārim zāhāb (100,000 talents of gold) / ʾelep ʾălāpîm kkārê-kesef (a million talents of silver) — whether literal or representing an incomprehensibly vast sum, the Chronicler makes clear that the temple received the accumulated wealth of David's campaigns, transformed from the instrument of war into the material of sacred space.
22.15: ʿimmᵉḵā lārōḇ ʿōśê melāʾḵâ — "with you in abundance are those who do the work." David's inventory of craftsmen (stonecutters/masons/woodworkers/ḵol-ḥākām-lēḇ bᵉḵol-melāʾḵâ — "every skilled-heart person in every craft") echoes the Bezalel-and-Oholiab assembly for the tabernacle (Exod 31:6; 36:1–2). The phrase ḥākām-lēḇ (skilled/wise of heart) is the Exodus vocabulary for craftsmen whose skill is divinely given — David frames the temple workforce in tabernacle terms, establishing the spiritual continuity between Moses' wilderness tent and Solomon's permanent house.
22.16: zāhāḇ kesef wᵉnᵉḥōšet ûḇarzel ʾên mispār — "gold, silver, bronze, and iron beyond counting." The fourfold metal formula (gold/silver/bronze/iron) reflects the descending scale of cultic and material value from the innermost sanctuary (gold) to the outer structure (iron). qûm wᵃʿăśēh wîhî YHWH ʿimmāḵ — "rise and do it, and may YHWH be with you." The imperative charge is brief and absolute: all preparation is complete, divine presence is invoked, the command is given. David's last word to Solomon on the temple is not another exhortation but a departure — he entrusts the work.
22.17: David extends the temple charge to all Israel's leaders: wayyṣaw dāwîd lᵉḵol-śārê yiśrāʾēl laʿzōr lišlōmōh bᵉnô — "David commanded all the leaders of Israel to support his son Solomon." The royal succession is presented as a community project rather than a dynastic transfer: the leaders are not merely informed of the succession but commissioned to actively support the new king in the temple project. This democratic dimension of the Chronicles theology — shared communal investment in the sacred project — will climax in 1 Chr 29's freewill-offering assembly.
22.18: The rhetorical questions construct Israel's covenant security as the basis for action: hălōʾ YHWH ʾĕlōhêkem ʿimmāḵem ("Is not YHWH your God with you?") / ûheniḥ lāḵem missāḇîḇ ("Has he not given you rest on every side?") — the mᵉnûḥâ (rest) from enemies is both the political precondition for temple-building and the theological meaning of Solomon's reign (22:9). nātan bᵉyāḏî ʾet-yōšᵉḇê hāʾāreṣ — YHWH has already given the land's inhabitants into David's hand; the military work is done. What remains is the sacred work.
22.19: The climactic charge: ûttᵉnû lᵉḇaḇḵem wᵉnapšᵉḵem liḏrōš lYHWH ʾĕlōhêḵem — "give your heart and soul to seeking YHWH your God." lēḇāḇ (heart, will, intellect) + nepeš (soul, life force) — the total-person dedication formula (Deut 6:5; 30:6) applied to the temple enterprise. qûmû ûḇᵉnû ʾet-miqdaš YHWH — "rise and build the sanctuary of YHWH" — the final imperative uses miqdāš (holy place, sanctuary) rather than bêt (house), shifting from architectural to sacred register. Bringing the ark and holy vessels into the sanctuary is the goal: not architecture for its own sake but the dwelling-place of YHWH's name among his people.

6.1: bᵉnê lēwî — the chapter opens with the primary threefold division of Levi's sons: Gershom (also written Gershon elsewhere; H1647/H1648), Kohath (H6955), and Merari (H4847). The Chronicler consistently uses Gershom rather than Gershon; LXX harmonizes to Gerson throughout. The triad is the structural organizing principle for the entire chapter: the Aaronic high-priestly line runs through Kohath (vv1-15), the broader Levitical families and temple musicians are organized under all three (vv16-48), and the Levitical city allotments in the final section (vv54-81) are distributed among the same three clans.
6.2: The Kohathite sub-clans — Amram, Izhar, Hebron, Uzziel — are listed in the same order as Exod 6:18. The priestly family runs through Amram (H6019 — exalted people); Izhar (H3324 — fresh olive oil, he shines), Hebron (H2275 — alliance, league), and Uzziel (H5816 — El is my strength) are the lateral branches. The distinction between the Amramite priestly line and the other Kohathite sub-clans is the structural basis for the Levitical transport legislation in Num 3:27-32.
6.3: yaldê ʿAmrām — 'the children of Amram' uses yālaḏ-derivative yᵉlādîm (H3206, children) rather than the usual bᵉnê (sons), because the list includes Miriam who is female. The same form appears in Num 26:59. The inclusion of Miriam alongside Aaron and Moses reflects her prophetic role (Exod 15:20-21; Mic 6:4). The four sons of Aaron — Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar — are listed in birth order; Nadab and Abihu died without sons (Lev 10:1-2; Num 3:4), so the priestly line continues through Eleazar.
6.4: wayyôleḏ ʾelʿāzār ʾet pînᵉḥās — the standard genealogical formula switches here to wayyôleḏ (Qal imperfect with waw-consecutive of yālaḏ, H3205 — 'and he fathered'). Phinehas (H6372 — possibly 'serpent's mouth' or an Egyptian loanword pa-nehes meaning 'the Nubian') is the priest who turned back the Baal-Peor plague (Num 25:7-13) and received the covenant of eternal priesthood. The Chronicler traces his line to establish the post-exilic Zadokite priesthood's legitimate pedigree.
6.5: Abishua (H500 — father of salvation) → Bukki (H1231 — proven/tested, or a short form of a longer name) → Uzzi (H5813 — my strength). The chain continues in the same wayyôleḏ formula. These names appear only here and in Ezra 7:4-5 and lack independent historical attestation, but their placement in the chain is essential for the unbroken Aaronic succession.
6.6: Zerahiah (H2228 — YHWH has risen/shone; from zāraḥ H2224 to rise, of the sun) → Meraioth (H4812 — rebellions or bitter things; from mārah H4784 to be contentious). The name Meraioth's negative semantic load is unusual for a priestly name; names with charged meanings appear in genealogies without implied moral commentary on their bearers.
6.7: Meraioth → Amariah (H568 — YHWH has said/promised; from ʾāmar + Yāh) → Ahitub (H285 — my brother is good; from ʾāḥ + ṭôḇ). The repeated name Amariah in vv7 and 11, and the repeated Ahitub in vv7 and 11, reflect either genuine name-repetition within the priestly family (common in sacerdotal families honoring ancestors) or a telescoping of the genealogy that has collapsed two distinct figures.
6.8: Ahitub → Zadok (H6659 — righteous, just; from ṣādaq H6663) — the founding figure of the Jerusalem priestly establishment. Zadok appears first in the narrative as one of David's two high priests (2 Sam 8:17), then as Solomon's sole high priest after Abiathar's removal (1 Kgs 2:27, 35). The Zadokite line becomes the authorized Jerusalem priesthood; Ezek 44:15 restricts the restored temple's service to the 'sons of Zadok.' The name ṣādôq gave rise to the term 'Sadducee' (Greek Saddoukaios, Hebrew ṣᵉdôqîm), the Second Temple priestly party claiming descent from this line. Ahimaaz (H290 — my brother is Ammaz/powerful) was the swift messenger who ran to David with news of Absalom's death (2 Sam 18:19-32).
6.9: Azariah (H5838 — YHWH has helped; from ʿāzar + Yāh) → Johanan (H3110 — YHWH is gracious; from ḥānan + Yᵉhô). The name Azariah recurs three times in the high-priestly chain (vv9, 10, 13), reflecting its frequency in the priestly line; it is also the birth-name of the king called Uzziah. The alternating theophoric elements (ʿAzaryāh / Yôḥānān / ʿAzaryāh again) create a rhythmic pattern in the name-chain.
6.10: hûʾ hakkōhēn ʾăšer kihēn bayyêkāl ʾăšer bānāh Šᵉlōmōh bîrûšālāim — 'he is the one who served as priest in the temple that Solomon built in Jerusalem.' The embedded relative clause is a genealogical anchor, identifying one of the Azariahs in the chain as the serving priest in Solomon's temple (probably the Azariah of 1 Kgs 4:2). The verb kihēn (H3547, Piel perfect of kāhan — to serve as priest) is the denominal verb from kōhēn; the Piel expresses the functional exercise of the priestly office.
6.11: A second Azariah → a second Amariah → a second Ahitub. The recurrence of the same names within three generations is a well-attested pattern in priestly families; naming sons after grandfathers maintained the family's genealogical identity and liturgical prestige within the Levitical system. The repetition may also reflect genuine compression of the genealogy by the Chronicler.
6.12: The second Ahitub → a second Zadok → Shallum (H7967 — recompense; from šālam). This second Zadok is the ancestor of Hilkiah the high priest who found the Torah scroll in Josiah's temple reform (2 Kgs 22:8). The genealogy traces the unbroken Zadokite line across the entire monarchic period, establishing continuity of authorized priesthood from David to the exile (v15).
6.13: Shallum → Hilkiah (H2518 — my portion is YHWH; from ḥēleq + Yāh). This is the Hilkiah who discovered the Torah scroll during Josiah's reform (2 Kgs 22:8, 23:4). The name's meaning resonates with the Levitical tradition: YHWH himself is the Levites' portion and inheritance (Num 18:20; Deut 18:2), so naming a priest 'my portion is YHWH' is theologically apt. Hilkiah → a third Azariah.
6.14: The third Azariah → Seraiah (H8304 — YHWH is ruler/prince; from śārar + Yāh). Seraiah is the chief priest at the fall of Jerusalem (2 Kgs 25:18), executed by Nebuchadnezzar at Riblah (2 Kgs 25:18-21). He fathered Jehozadak (H3087 — YHWH is righteous; Yᵉhô + ṣādaq — a compound of the divine name and the Zadok-root ṣdq). The compound name may reflect the family's identification with its Zadokite priestly heritage.
6.15: wîhôṣādāq hālaḵ bᵉhaglaôt YHWH ʾet Yᵉhûḏāh wîrûšālāim bᵉyaḏ Nᵉḇûḵaḏneʾṣṣar — 'Jehozadak went when YHWH exiled Judah and Jerusalem by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar.' The verb hālaḵ (H1980 — went) with bᵉhaglaôt (the infinitive construct of gālāh H1540 — to go into exile) uses the standard exile-vocabulary. The phrase bᵉyaḏ Nᵉḇûḵaḏneʾṣṣar (by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar) uses yāḏ (hand) as the instrument of YHWH's action: the Babylonian king is YHWH's yāḏ. The genealogical chain reaches its endpoint; Jehozadak's son Jeshua/Joshua was the high priest who returned with Zerubbabel (Ezra 3:2; Hag 1:1; Zech 3:1).
6.16: The repetition of 'the sons of Levi were Gershom, Kohath, and Merari' (verbatim repetition of v1) marks the structural seam between the high-priestly genealogy (vv1-15) and the broader Levitical family lists (vv16-30). Structural repetition as a literary device — re-opening a new section with the same formula used to open the chapter — is a recognized technique in biblical genealogical literature, functioning like a section-divider in a legal document.
6.17: wᵉʾēlleh šᵉmôt bᵉnê gēršôm — 'these are the names of the sons of Gershom.' The name-list (šᵉmôt) formula is distinct from the begetting formula; it introduces a sibling list rather than a generation-chain. Libni (H3845 — white, pale) and Shimei (H8096 — he has heard; from šāmaʿ) are Gershom's two sons, consistent with Exod 6:17 and Num 3:18. Šimʿî is a common name rooted in the concept of hearing/obeying; at least four different people named Shimei appear in Chronicles.
6.18: The sons of Kohath are repeated from v2 (Amram, Izhar, Hebron, Uzziel) now placed structurally within the three-tribe framework of vv16-30. The repetition is not redundancy but organizational structure: v2 gave the Kohathite clans as the source of the priestly line; here they are situated alongside the Gershomite and Merarite clans as three parallel sections of the full Levitical family list.
6.19: The sons of Merari — Mahli (H4249 — sick, diseased; or possibly a gentle/mild one) and Mushi (H4187 — drawn from water, or touch; both appear in Exod 6:19 and Num 3:20). The closing formula ʾēlleh mišpᵉḥôt haLēwî lᵉḇêt ʾăḇōtāyw (these are the Levitical families according to their fathers) uses mišpāḥôt (H4940 — families, clans, extended kinship groups) as the organizational unit, distinct from maṭṭeh/šēḇeṭ (tribe) and bêt ʾāḇ (father's house).
6.20: The Gershomite descent chain uses bᵉnô (his son) as a connector rather than wayyôleḏ, indicating a descent list rather than a strict father-son narrative chain: Libni → Jahath (H3189 — grasping; from ʾāḥaz) → Zimmah (H2155 — lewdness/plot; an unusual name for a Levite, possibly a family name whose negative semantic load had faded in use). The bᵉnô pattern 'X his son, Y his son' (bᵉnô X bᵉnô Y) is a shorthand for representing ancestral succession without the narrative weight of the wayyôleḏ formula.
6.21: The Gershomite chain continues: Joah (H3098 — YHWH is brother; from Yᵉhô + ʾāḥ) → Iddo (H5714 — timely, at the appointed time; from ʿēd) → Zerah (H2226 — rising/shining; from zāraḥ, the same root as Zerahiah in v6) → Jeaterai (H2979 — who causes to remain, or YHWH causes to remain). The Gershomite line ends at seven names (vv20-21); the same line appears in slightly different form in other lists, reflecting the natural variation in genealogical transmission.
6.22: The Kohathite line begins here with Amminadab (H5992 — my kinsman is generous/noble; from ʿam + nāḏaḇ) rather than the expected Amram of v2. This is textually significant: Amminadab may be a variant or replacement name for Amram, or a separate figure inserted into the genealogy. His son Korah (H7141 — bald; from qāraḥ) is the leader of the rebellion in Num 16. The Chronicler's genealogical inclusion of Korah without comment reflects the convention of listing all descendants regardless of historical reputation; his descendants become legitimately integrated into temple worship (vv33-38).
6.23: Korah → Assir (H617 — imprisoned; from ʾāsar) → Elkanah (H511 — God has acquired/created; from ʾēl + qānāh) → Ebiasaph (H43 — my father has gathered; from ʾāḇ + ʾāsap) → Assir (second occurrence). The name Elkanah appears eight times in this chapter, reflecting its frequency in Kohathite families. The double Assir and the chain connecting through Korah to the temple musicians integrates the Korahite guilds (Pss 42-49, 84-88) into the legitimate Levitical structure.
6.24: Assir → Tahath (H8480 — beneath, underneath; appears twice in this genealogy, vv24 and 37) → Uriel (H222 — God is my flame/light; from ʾûr + ʾēl) → Uzziah (H5818 — YHWH is my strength; same name as the Judean king; from ʿōz + Yāh) → Shaul (H7586 — asked for/prayed for; same name as Israel's first king). The name pattern alternates between descriptive names (Tahath = below) and theophoric names (Uriel, Uzziah).
6.25: The sons of Elkanah were Amasai (H6022 — my burden; from māśāʾ) and Ahimoth (H287 — my brother is death; from ʾāḥ + māwet). Ahimoth's name is stark: the 'brother of death' formula is unusual in priestly genealogies. Some scholars suggest it may reflect memorial naming — a child born after a sibling's death — a practice attested across ancient cultures. The MT is supported by LXX's Achimoth.
6.26: From Elkanah: son Zophai (H6689 — honeycomb; from ṣôp; or watchman, from ṣāpāh) → Nahath (H5184 — rest, quiet; or descent). The Elkanah here is a different generation from vv23-25, tracing a lateral branch of the extended Kohathite clan. The repeated name creates apparent confusion but traces multiple collateral lines through the same patriarch.
6.27: Eliab (H446 — my God is father; from ʾēl + ʾāḇ) → Jeroham (H3395 — he will be compassionate; from riḥam) → Elkanah (the third Elkanah in this section). This Elkanah is Samuel's father (1 Sam 1:1-2). The descent chain from Kohath → Amram → Elkanah establishes Samuel's Levitical credentials. In 1 Sam 1:1, Samuel's family is described as from 'the hill country of Ephraim,' indicating their tribal residence, not tribal origin; the Chronicler's genealogy is the authoritative statement of their Levitical descent.
6.28: ûḇᵉnê šᵉmûʾēl habᵉḵôr waššnî wᵉʾăḇiyyāh — 'the sons of Samuel: the firstborn Vashni (reading MT) and Abijah.' The name Vashni (H2059) is textually problematic: 1 Sam 8:2 names Samuel's firstborn as Joel (Yôʾēl — YHWH is God) and his second as Abijah. Scholarly consensus is that the MT of Chronicles has suffered a scribal error: the original text read habᵉḵôr Yôʾēl wᵉhašŠēnî (the firstborn Joel and the second), with wahaššēnî (the second) being misread as a name 'Vashni.' Most modern translations (ESV, NIV, NRSV) emend to Joel; the LXX has Joel, supporting the emendation. The MKT preserves the MT reading to flag the textual divergence.
6.29: The Merarite line: Mahli → son Libni (H3845 — white; the same name as Gershom's son in v17, reflecting cross-clan name repetition) → Shimei → Uzza (H5798 — strength; the same name as the man who touched the ark in 1 Chr 13:9-10). The chain uses bᵉnô connectors throughout: 'Mahli, his son Libni, his son Shimei, his son Uzza.'
6.30: Shimea (H8092 — he has heard; from šāmaʿ; same root as Shimei) → Haggiah (H2293 — festival of YHWH; from ḥag + Yāh; naming a person after a festival day is unusual among theophoric patterns) → Asaiah (H6222 — YHWH has made/done; from ʿāśāh + Yāh). This closes the Merarite genealogy of vv29-30 and completes the three-part family list of vv16-30.
6.31: wᵉʾēlleh ʾăšer heʿĕmîḏ Dāwîḏ ʿal yᵉḏê šîr bêt YHWH — 'these are the ones whom David stationed over the service of song in the house of the LORD.' The verb heʿĕmîḏ (H5975 Hiphil — caused to stand, stationed, appointed) indicates official installation; David as the organizer of temple worship is a defining theme of Chronicles. The phrase ʿal yᵉḏê šîr (over the hands of song) uses yāḏ idiomatically for 'in charge of/responsible for.' Šîr (H7892 — song, singing) names the musical service; the cognate šîrāh appears in Miriam's song (Exod 15:1).
6.32: The musicians 'served with song before the tabernacle of the tent of meeting' — šārat (H8334, Piel — to serve, to minister) is the standard Levitical service verb (distinct from priestly kāhan). The temporal qualifier — 'until Solomon built the house of the LORD in Jerusalem' — frames the tent-of-meeting music as a provisional arrangement superseded by the temple. Kᵉmišpāṭām (according to their prescribed order) uses mišpāṭ (H4941 — judgment, custom, ordinance) for the established liturgical regulation.
6.33: hēmān hamᵉšōrēr — 'Heman the singer/cantor.' The title mᵉšōrēr (H7891 Poel participle of šîr — one who sings, a cantor) is Heman's defining designation. He is identified as son of Joel son of Samuel — establishing Samuel's grandson as a chief musician, connecting the prophetic (Samuel) and musical (Heman) traditions in Kohathite piety. The Korahite Psalms (Pss 42-49, 84-85, 87-88) are the guild's literary output; Ps 88 is attributed to 'Heman the Ezrahite.'
6.34: The genealogy traces Heman back through Samuel's line: son of Elkanah → son of Jeroham → son of Eliel (H447 — my God is God; from ʾēl + ʾēl; a double-theophoric name) → son of Toah (H8430 — depression, humility; appears only here). The chain uses ben (son of) ascending from son to father, the reverse of the descending wayyôleḏ pattern.
6.35: Son of Zuph (H6689 — honeycomb; the same ṣôp root as Zophai in v26; 1 Sam 1:1 describes Elkanah as 'a Zuphite') → son of Elkanah → son of Mahath (H4287 — smiting; appears only in this genealogy) → son of Amasai (H6022 — cf. v25). The chain works its way back toward Kohath through a lateral line distinct from the direct Amram path.
6.36: Son of Elkanah (fourth Elkanah) → son of Joel (H3100 — YHWH is God; the same name as Samuel's firstborn in 1 Sam 8:2, absent from v28 in MT) → son of Azariah → son of Zephaniah (H6846 — YHWH has hidden/protected; from ṣāpan H6845 to hide/treasure + Yāh). The name Zephaniah uses the Qal perfect of ṣāpan (to hide, to store up); the theophoric meaning is 'YHWH has hidden/protected him,' carrying nuances of divine sheltering care.
6.37: Son of Tahath (H8480 — second occurrence; cf. v24) → son of Assir → son of Ebiasaph (H43 — cf. v23) → son of Korah (H7141 — cf. v22). The chain arrives at Korah, establishing Heman's Korahite identity. The Korahite sons who composed the Korahite psalms are not the rebel Korah himself; Num 26:11 records that 'the sons of Korah did not die' when the earth swallowed Korah, and their survival and eventual incorporation into temple worship is precisely what this genealogy charts.
6.38: Son of Izhar → son of Kohath → son of Levi → son of Israel. The ascending chain arrives at the patriarchal ancestors: Izhar (one of Kohath's four sons, v2), Kohath (v1), Levi, Israel/Jacob. The descent-to-patriarch formula serves as the genealogical anchor. Ben Yiśrāʾēl (son of Israel) closes the ascending chain at the point where all twelve-tribe genealogies terminate, placing Heman's line within the fullest national scope.
6.39: ûʾăḥîhû ʾāsāp — 'and his colleague Asaph.' The term ʾāḥ (H251 — brother) here means professional colleague rather than biological brother (Heman and Asaph are from different clans: Kohathite vs. Gershomite). Asaph (H623 — he has gathered; from ʾāsap) stood at Heman's right hand — the position of honor. The Asaphite musical guild produced twelve Psalms (Ps 50, 73-83); 1 Chr 25:1-2 describes Asaph as 'prophesying' with musical instruments.
6.40: Asaph's ascending genealogy: son of Berechiah (H1296 — YHWH blesses; from bāraḵ + Yāh) → son of Shimea (H8092 — he has heard; same name as the Merarite in v30) → son of Michael (H4317 — who is like God?; from the rhetorical question mî ḵəʾēl; one of the earliest attestations of this theophoric formula, later the archangel's name in Dan 10:13) → son of Baaseiah (H1202 — in the work of YHWH; appears only here).
6.41: Son of Malchijah (H4441 — my king is YHWH; from malkî + Yāh) → son of Ethni (H869 — my gift; appears only here) → son of Zerah (H2226 — rising; cf. vv6 and 21) → son of Adaiah (H5718 — YHWH has adorned/refreshed; from ʿāḏāh H5710 to put on ornaments + Yāh). The name Adaiah uses the relatively rare root ʿāḏāh (to adorn oneself, to put on jewelry), giving the theophoric meaning 'YHWH has adorned himself' or 'YHWH has adorned him.'
6.42: Son of Ethan (H387 — enduring, long-lived; the same name as the wise man of 1 Kgs 4:31 and possibly 'Ethan the Ezrahite' of Ps 89) → son of Zimmah (H2155 — cf. v20) → son of Shimei (H8096 — second occurrence; cf. v17). The multiple Shimeais and Ethansin the genealogy reflect the narrow name-pool in priestly families, where theophoric and virtue-names were recycled across generations.
6.43: Son of Jahath (H3189 — cf. v20) → son of Gershom → son of Levi. Asaph's chain terminates at Gershom/Levi, confirming his Gershomite clan identity and distinguishing him from Kohathite Heman. The three parallel genealogies (Heman vv33-38, Asaph vv39-43, Ethan vv44-47) represent all three Levitical clans in balanced liturgical arrangement.
6.44: The third musician: ʾētān (Ethan, H387 — enduring/long-lasting) from the Merarite clan, standing at the left hand. The spatial arrangement — Heman at center, Asaph at the right, Ethan at the left — mirrors liturgical processional formation. Son of Kishi (H6984 — laying YHWH's snare, or bow; from qaš + theophoric element) → son of Abdi (H5660 — my servant; from ʿeḇeḏ) → son of Malluch (H4409 — he has reigned; from mālaḵ).
6.45: Son of Hashabiah (H2811 — YHWH has thought/considered; from ḥāšaḇ + Yāh — YHWH has reckoned, planned, or valued) → son of Amaziah (H558 — YHWH is strong; from ʾāmaz + Yāh; the same name as several Judean kings) → son of Hilkiah (H2518 — cf. v13). The theophoric naming density in these Levitical genealogies is notably high compared to secular Israelite names, reflecting the priestly-musical guilds' strong YHWH-centered naming tradition.
6.46: Son of Amzi (H557 — my strength; short form of Amaziah) → son of Bani (H1137 — built; from bānāh — one built/established by God) → son of Shamer (H8106 — guardian, watchman; from šāmar H8104 to keep, guard). The name šāmer (keeper/watchman) uses the same root as the Psalms' descriptions of YHWH as the guardian of Israel who 'neither slumbers nor sleeps' (Ps 121:4).
6.47: Son of Mahli (H4249 — cf. v19) → son of Mushi (H4187 — cf. v19) → son of Merari → son of Levi. Ethan's chain terminates at Merari, confirming his Merarite clan identity. The three-part genealogical structure (Heman/Kohath, Asaph/Gershom, Ethan/Merari) is now complete, representing the entire threefold Levitical family in the temple's musical ministry.
6.48: wᵉʾăḥêhem haLᵉwiyyim — 'their fellow Levites' — were assigned to all other tabernacle/temple service. The term ʾāḥ (brother/colleague) here means professional associates. Kol ʿăḇōdat hāmišᵉkān bêt hāʾelōhîm (all the service of the tabernacle, the house of God) combines two terms: mišᵉkān (H4908 — tabernacle, the dwelling-place; from šākan to dwell) and bêt ʾelōhîm (house of God), treating them as a unified referent spanning from the wilderness sanctuary to Solomon's temple.
6.49: wᵉʾahᵃrōn ûḇānāyw maqṭîrîm ʿal mizbah hāʿōlāh ûmizbah haqqᵉṭōret — 'But Aaron and his sons were responsible for burning offerings on the altar of burnt offering and the altar of incense.' The adversative wᵉ (but/however) distinguishes Aaronic priestly duties from the Levitical ones of v48. The Hiphil participle maqṭîrîm (causing to smoke; from qāṭar H6999 — to cause sacrificial smoke to rise) is the technical term for altar-burning, distinct from the general verb for burning (śārap). For atonement: lᵉḵappēr ʿal Yiśrāʾēl uses the Piel infinitive of kāpar (H3722 — to cover, to wipe, to make atonement), the root underlying kappōret (the mercy seat/atonement cover, H3727) and Yôm hakkippûrîm (Day of Atonement). The precise liturgical citation — 'exactly as Moses the servant of God had commanded' (kᵉḵōl ʾăšer ṣiwwāh Mōšeh ʿeḇeḏ hāʾelōhîm) — is characteristically Chronistic, anchoring temple practice in Mosaic authority.
6.50: The second Aaronic genealogy (vv50-53) parallels vv4-8 in a compressed name-list format rather than the narrative wayyôleḏ chain. ʾēlleh bᵉnê ʾahᵃrōn introduces the list: Eleazar → Phinehas → Abishua (H500 — cf. v4). The double attestation of the Eleazar-to-Ahimaaz chain — once in narrative-begetting form (vv4-9) and once in name-list form (vv50-53) — provides a second witness to the priestly succession that the Chronicler considered essential for the post-exilic community's authorization of legitimate priesthood.
6.51: Abishua → Bukki (H1231 — cf. v5) → Uzzi (H5813 — cf. v5) → Zerahiah (H2228 — cf. v6). Four generations compressed into a single verse. The same names appear in Ezra 7:4 in the priestly genealogy Ezra provides for his own ancestry, confirming the Chronicler's genealogical data was used in post-exilic legal contexts.
6.52: Zerahiah → Meraioth (H4812 — cf. v6) → Amariah (H568 — cf. v7) → Ahitub (H285 — cf. v7). The name Meraioth (mᵉrāyôt, grammatical plural of mᵉrî — rebelliousness) appears only in Chronicles and Nehemiah, exclusively in priestly genealogical lists. Its plural form may be honorific (an abstract plural of excellence) rather than indicating actual plural referents.
6.53: Ahitub → Zadok (H6659 — cf. v8) → Ahimaaz (H290 — cf. v8). The second Aaronic genealogy ends at Ahimaaz, the same endpoint as the chain-anchor in v9. This creates a deliberate double-witness structure for the most important segment of the priestly genealogy: Zadok → Ahimaaz is attested twice, once in narrative form (v8-9) and once in list form (v53), reinforcing the legitimacy of the founding Zadokite lineage.
6.54: wᵉʾēlleh mᵉšiḇôtām lᵉṭîrôtām bigḇûlām — 'these are their dwelling places according to their allotted territories.' The term mᵉšiḇôtām (H4186 — dwelling places, settlements; from yāšaḇ to dwell) is paired with ṭîrôtām (territories/enclosures) within their gᵉḇûlām (boundaries). The lot system (gôrāl H1486 — lot, casting of lots) was the divinely-sanctioned allocation mechanism for both tribal land and Levitical city assignment; its use signals that the city allotments are YHWH's determination, not human negotiation.
6.55: The first Aaronic allocation: Hebron (Ḥeḇrôn H2275 — alliance, league) in Judah's territory with its migrāšêhā (pasturelands). Migrāš (H4054 — common land, pastureland; from gāraš H1644 to drive away, used for land cleared/reserved for grazing) is the technical term for the open land surrounding Levitical cities, specified in Num 35:4-5 as extending 1,000 cubits from the city wall. This term appears approximately 20 times in vv54-81, becoming the chapter's dominant vocabulary for the city-allotment section.
6.56: The distinction between Aaronic city-territory and Caleb's fields: ûśᵉḏê hāʿîr wᵉḥaṣᵉrêhā nātᵉnû lᵉḵāleḇ ben Yᵉpunneh — 'the open fields and villages of Hebron were given to Caleb son of Jephunneh.' Śāḏeh (H7704 — field, open country) and ḥāṣēr (H2691 — enclosure, courtyard, unwalled village) designate the productive agricultural lands outside the city proper. The dual allocation — city to priests, fields to Caleb — reflects Josh 14:13-14's fulfillment of the Mosaic promise.
6.57: The cities of refuge (ʿārê hammiqlaṭ; miqlaṭ H4733 — place of shelter/asylum; from qālaṭ to shelter) assigned to the Aaronites. Hebron (Josh 20:7), Libnah (H3841 — white; in the Shephelah), Jattir (H3492 — pre-eminent; Negev highlands, 1 Sam 30:27), and Eshtemoa (H851 — I will make myself heard). The six cities of refuge (three east of Jordan, three west, Josh 20:7-8) are a subset of the 48 Levitical cities; the overlap between refuge cities and Aaronic cities is specified in Num 35:6.
6.58: Hilen (H2432 — strength; called Holon in Josh 21:15) and Debir (H1688 — oracle/word; a prominent Judean city captured by Othniel, Josh 15:15-17). The ûmigrāšêhā formula (and its pasturelands) appended to each city name appears in every subsequent verse of this section, creating the standardized legal language derived from Num 35 and Josh 21.
6.59: Ashan (H6228 — smoke; Josh 15:42) and Beth-shemesh (H1053 — house of the sun; a city associated with the ark's return from Philistia, 1 Sam 6:9-20). Beth-shemesh's inclusion in the Aaronic city list is theologically resonant: the ark returned through this city, and the men of Beth-shemesh died for looking into the ark (1 Sam 6:19). A priestly city and an ark-transit city are here co-identified, placing the Aaronic custodial responsibility over this ark-associated site.
6.60: Three cities from Benjamin: Geba (H1387 — hill; north of Jerusalem), Alemeth (H5964 — covering, concealment; Josh 21:18 calls it Almon), and Anathoth (H6068 — answers; the priestly city famous as Jeremiah's birthplace, Jer 1:1). Anathoth's priestly character explains the village's identity: Jeremiah is a priest by birth (Jer 1:1 identifies him as 'of the priests who were in Anathoth'). Total for the Aaronic Kohathite allotment: thirteen cities, consistent with Josh 21:4.
6.61: The remaining (non-Aaronic) Kohathites received ten cities from the half-tribe of Manasseh west of the Jordan. Hannôtārîm (H3498 Niphal participle of yātar — what remains, the remainder) distinguishes these Kohathites from the Aaronic priestly family. The miḥᵃṣî maṭṭēh manašṣeh (half-tribe of Manasseh) designation reflects the bifurcation of Manasseh between Transjordanian (east) and Cisjordanian (west) settlements.
6.62: The Gershomite allotment — thirteen cities from Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and eastern Manasseh (Bashan) — spans the northern tribal territories. Baššān (H1316 — fertile plateau; the region east of the Sea of Galilee famous for its oak forests and cattle, Ps 22:12; Amos 4:1) was the Gershomites' Transjordanian allotment. The number thirteen matches Josh 21:6.
6.63: The Merarite allotment — twelve cities by lot from Reuben, Gad, and Zebulun. Wayyᵉṣᵉʾû bᵉgôrāl (they came out by lot) uses the standard lot-formula. The Merarite cities span the Transjordanian territories (Reuben, Gad) plus the Galilee coastal territory (Zebulun), a geographically dispersed assignment consistent with the Levites' distributed presence across all Israel — precisely the design of Num 35's Levitical city legislation.
6.64: wayyittᵉnû bᵉnê Yiśrāʾēl laLᵉwiyyim ʾet hāʿārîm wᵉʾet migrāšêhem — 'the Israelites gave the Levites the cities with their pasturelands.' The subject is the entire Israelite community acting in corporate faithfulness to Mosaic legislation (Num 35:2-3). The migrāšêhem formula appears in this summary statement, reinforcing that the pastureland is an integral and non-negotiable component of the Levitical city grant.
6.65: Wayyittᵉnû bᵉgôrāl (they assigned by lot) — the lot mechanism again; the cities from Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin are re-enumerated as given by lot from these three southern tribes. The repetition in vv64-65 — a general allotment summary followed by a specific southern-tribe clarification — reflects the two-step structure of Josh 21's account.
6.66: Some Kohathite families also received cities from Ephraim's territory. Ûmimišpᵉḥôt bᵉnê qᵉhāt (and from the families of the sons of Kohath) picks up the non-Aaronic Kohathites whose Manassite cities were mentioned in v61. The Ephraim-territory Kohathite cities placed legitimate priestly-adjacent families throughout the central highland region.
6.67: The cities of refuge in Ephraim: Shechem (H7927 — shoulder/back; the central hill-country city where the Mosaic covenant was renewed in Josh 24; Josh 20:7 designates it as a city of refuge) and Gezer (H1507 — portion cut off; a strategic Shephelah city). Shechem's dual designation — covenant-renewal site and city of refuge — makes it the most theologically layered city in this list.
6.68: Jokmeam (H3362 — let the people rise up; from qûm + ʿam; called Kibzaim in Josh 21:22) and Beth-horon (H1032 — house of the hollow/cave; actually two linked cities — upper and lower Beth-horon — controlling the main ascent route from the coastal plain toward Jerusalem). Beth-horon's military significance made it a repeated site of decisive engagement in Israelite history (Josh 10:10-11; 1 Sam 13:18; 1 Macc 3:16-24).
6.69: Aijalon (H357 — deer-place; from ʾayyāl a stag/deer; the valley where the sun stood still in Josh 10:12-13, one of the most dramatic divine interventions in the conquest narratives) and Gath-rimmon (H1667 — winepress of the pomegranate; from gat + rimmôn). Both are in the Shephelah; these were originally Danite cities that became Kohathite Levitical cities after Dan's migration north (Judg 18).
6.70: From the western half-tribe of Manasseh: Aner (H6063 — waterfall; appears only in Levitical city lists) and Bileam (H1109 — lord of the people; called Ibleam in Josh 21:25, overlooking the Jezreel Valley). The note that these were 'for the remaining Kohathite families' (lîtèr mišpaḥat bᵉnê qᵉhāt) supplements the eastern Manassite allotment of v61.
6.71: The Gershomite allotment from eastern Manasseh/Bashan: Golan (H1474 — exile, carried away; the elevated plateau east of the Sea of Galilee, a city of refuge Josh 20:8; the modern Golan Heights preserves the ancient name) and Ashtaroth (H6252 — Astartes, plural; a Canaanite goddess-name used as a city-name in Bashan, modern Tell Ashtara in Syria). The irony of a Levitical city named for the goddess Astarte is not commented on by the Chronicler — the name was purely geographic by this period.
6.72: From Issachar: Kedesh (H6943 — sanctuary, holy; distinct from Kedesh in Naphtali, v76) and Daberath (H1705 — word, pasture; at the base of Mt. Tabor, Josh 21:28). The ûmigrāšêhā formula continues with mechanical regularity through vv72-81, reflecting the legal formula's standardized character throughout the Levitical city legislation.
6.73: Ramoth (H7216 — heights; in Issachar, distinct from Ramoth-gilead) and Anem (H6046 — two fountains, dual of ʿayin spring/eye; Josh 21:29 calls it En-gannim). The two-city-per-verse format reflects both the oral-transmission pattern of legal lists and the Chronicler's condensed treatment relative to the fuller Josh 21 account.
6.74: From Asher: Mashal (H4913 — proverb/saying; called Mishal in Josh 21:30) and Abdon (H5658 — service, servitude; from ʿāḇad). Asher's territory ran along the Mediterranean coast north of Carmel; the Asherite Levitical cities secured priestly presence along the coastal corridor that was the zone of Phoenician commercial influence.
6.75: Hukok (H2712 — engraved or portion; called Helkath in Josh 21:31) and Rehob (H7340 — broad place, plaza; from rāḥaḇ). The name Rehob (wide open space) was applied to multiple towns; the Asherite Rehob is in the Acco plain. The toponym rᵉḥōḇ (broad/open space) is the same root as the placename Rehoboth and describes a naturally wide area, likely a valley junction or agricultural flatland.
6.76: From Naphtali: Kedesh in Galilee (Qāḏēš baggālîl — Kedesh in the Galil; the full designation distinguishing this northern city of refuge from the Issacharite Kedesh of v72; Josh 20:7), Hammon (H2540 — warm springs; near the Mediterranean coast north of Acco), and Kiriathaim (H7156 — double city; dual form of qiryāh city; called Kartan in Josh 21:32). 'Galilee' (gālîl H1551 — circle, district) here refers to the northern highland region; it is the territory whose first Assyrian deportation (2 Kgs 15:29) is later addressed by Isa 9:1.
6.77: The Merarite allotment in Zebulun: Rimmono (H7417 — pomegranate; called Rimmon in Josh 21:35) and Tabor (H8396 — mountain/height; Mt. Tabor, the distinctive isolated volcanic hill c. 600m high in the Jezreel Valley — a natural landmark and traditional worship site). The designation habbnôtārîm laLᵉwiyyim ûlibᵉnê mᵉrārî (the remaining Levites, the sons of Merari) mirrors the 'remaining Kohathites' formula of v61.
6.78: From Reuben, east of the Jordan opposite Jericho: Bezer (H1221 — inaccessible fortress; a city of refuge in the wilderness plateau, Deut 4:43; Josh 20:8). The descriptor bammidbar (in the wilderness/desert; H4057 — uninhabited land, steppe) designates the Moabite plateau east of the Dead Sea. Jahzah (H3096 — trodden down; where Sihon of Heshbon was defeated in Num 21:23) marks the border territory of the tribal allotment.
6.79: Kedemoth (H6932 — ancient/eastern places; the area from which Moses sent messengers to Sihon requesting passage, Deut 2:26) and Mephaath (H4158 — splendor or heights; in the Reubenite tableland). These four Reubenite cities — Bezer, Jahzah, Kedemoth, Mephaath — correspond to Josh 21:36-37's Reubenite Merarite allotment.
6.80: From Gad: Ramoth in Gilead (H7433 — heights of Gilead; the major Transjordanian city of refuge east of the Jordan; contested between Israel and Aram throughout 1-2 Kings, from Ahab's death at Ramoth-gilead in 1 Kgs 22 to Jehu's anointing there in 2 Kgs 9) and Mahanaim (H4266 — two camps; where Jacob encountered the angels in Gen 32:2, and where David retreated during Absalom's revolt, 2 Sam 17:24).
6.81: Heshbon (H2809 — stronghold; the Amorite capital under Sihon, Num 21:25-26; later in Gadite territory) and Jazer (H3270 — he helps; in the Ammonite borderland, Num 21:32). The Levitical city list ends here — 48 cities total across all twelve tribes (Num 35:7 specifies 48 as the required total). The chapter's spatial closure at Jazer and Heshbon in the Transjordanian territory mirrors the chapter's opening in the Cisjordanian tribal heartland: Levi's service covers the entire expanse of the promised land from coast to eastern plateau.

27.23: The note that David did not number those under twenty years old — lōʾ nāśāʾ dāwîḏ mispārām lᵉmibben ʿeśrîm šānāh wāmaṭṭāh kî ʾāmar YHWH lᵉharbôt ʾet yiśrāʾēl kᵉḵôḵᵉḇê haššāmāyim, ‘David did not number those twenty years and under, for YHWH had said he would make Israel as numerous as the stars of heaven’ — invokes the Abrahamic stellar-promise (Gen 15:5; 22:17) as the explicit warrant for census-restraint. The connection is significant: the census of ch21 (which brought plague) was a failure to reckon with this same promise. By counting the full military population, David treated Israel’s numbers as knowable human data rather than as YHWH’s promise-in-progress. The exemption of those under twenty recalls the wilderness census (Num 1:3: those twenty and above who can go to war), but the Chronicler’s gloss deepens it: the ‘uncounted’ are those still being added in fulfillment of the promise. The Abrahamic kᵉḵôḵᵉḇê haššāmāyim (as numerous as the stars of heaven) is the OT’s formula for the uncountable covenant-gift: Heb 11:12 applies it to Abraham’s descendants ‘as many as the stars of heaven and as innumerable as the grains of sand by the seashore.’ David’s restraint here acknowledges that the covenant-multiplication cannot be audited.
27.1: This verse introduces the entire rotation system with a triple administrative heading: rāʾšê hāʾāḇôṭ (“heads of the fathers”) signals kinship-based leadership; śārê hāʾălāpîm wəhammēʾôṭ (“commanders of thousands and hundreds”) signals military hierarchy; hamməšārəṭîm ʾet hammelek (“those serving the king”) uses the verb šārat, the same term for priestly and Levitical cultic service (see ch24–26). That the military-administrative system is described with the vocabulary of priestly service is characteristic of the Chronicler: covenant service pervades every domain of Israelite life, not only the sanctuary.
27.2: Yāšôḇʾām ben Zabdîʾēl commands the first division for the first month with 24,000 men. The maḥᵃlōqôt (“divisions/courses”) system distributes 288,000 men across twelve months, ensuring that the full military capacity of Israel is maintained without massing armed power permanently in one place. The same principle governs the priestly and Levitical courses (ch24–25): institutional rotation is the Chronicler’s model for sustainable and just administration.
27.3: mibbənê Peréṣ (“from the sons of Perez”) — Jashobeam’s lineage links the first military commander to the royal Judahite line from which David himself descended (2:4; Ruth 4:12, 18–22). The first slot in David’s military rotation is held by someone from the messianic lineage, subtly grounding military organization within the larger narrative of covenant election running through Judah and its descendants.
27.4: mišneh (“second, deputy”) — the second commander has a named officer Mikloth as his mišneh, a term drawn from the broader Hebrew administrative vocabulary for a second-in-command (cf. Esth 10:3 for Mordecai as second to the king). The Chronicler specifies the chain of command within each division, not just the commanding officer: accountability and succession are built into the structure.
27.5: Bənāyāhû ben Yəhôyādāʾ hakkōhēn (“Benaiah son of Jehoiada the priest”) commands the third division. The assignment of a priest’s son to military command — and his explicit identification by his priestly lineage — signals the interpenetration of cultic and martial leadership in the Davidic administration. The same Benaiah appears in the Solomonic succession narrative (1 Kgs 1–2) as the military executor of David’s final instructions, making him a pivot figure between the two reigns.
27.6: hûʾ Bənāyāhû haggibôr bišlōšîm wəʾal hašlōšîm (“this was Benaiah the mighty man among the thirty and above the thirty”) — the catalog formula from 11:25 is embedded here, connecting the administrative record to the heroic roster. ûmaḥlûqtô ʾAmmîzāḇāḇ bənô (“his division under his son Ammizabad”) — son succeeds father; the division-level command has dynastic continuity, just as the priesthood and the monarchy do.
27.7: ʾăśāhēʾl ʾăḥî Yôʾāḇ (“Asahel brother of Joab”) — Asahel died early in David’s career pursuing Abner (2 Sam 2:23), yet his son Zebadiah holds his place in the mature administrative roster. The system preserves family-unit command across generations: a man’s military legacy survives his death through his lineage, giving the entire institution a longer memory than any individual’s life.
27.8: Šamhût hayyizrāḥ (“Shamhuth the Izrahite”) — fifth month. The term yizrāḥî may be a variant spelling of zerāḥî (Zerahite), connecting this commander to Zerah son of Judah (2:4, 6). The alternation of commanders from different Judahite sub-clans distributes military prestige across the families of the covenant tribe, preventing the concentration of military honor in a single house within Judah.
27.9: ʾÎrāʾ ben ʾIqqeš hattəqôʾî (“Ira son of Ikkesh the Tekoite”) — sixth month. Tekoa, a Judahite town in the southern hills, later became the hometown of Amos (Amos 1:1) and the source of the “wise woman” whom Joab sent to David (2 Sam 14:2). Its presence in the military roster shows that southern Judean communities were fully integrated into the Davidic administrative system from its inception.
27.10: Ḥeleṣ happəlônî (“Helez the Pelonite”) — seventh month; mibbənê ʾEprayim (“from the sons of Ephraim”). A northern tribe provides one of the twelve commanders, explicitly identified by tribal affiliation. The Chronicler’s pan-Israelite vision of the united kingdom is enacted here: military leadership is not a monopoly of Judah but is distributed across the covenant community, including the dominant northern tribe.
27.11: Sibbəkay haḥûšātî (“Sibbecai the Hushathite”) — eighth month; lazzarḥî (“of the Zerahite line”). Sibbecai appears in the battle narrative against the Philistine giant at Gezer (20:4), where he killed Sippai. The inclusion of a hero from the Philistine giant wars in the administrative rotation integrates military valor into institutional order: the exceptional becomes the routine through structured service.
27.12: ʾăḇîʾezer hāʾannətôtî (“Abiezer the Anetothite”) — ninth month; labinyāmînî (“from Benjamin”). Anathoth was a Levitical city in Benjamin (Josh 21:18) and later the hometown of Jeremiah (Jer 1:1). The presence of an Anathothite in David’s military roster places this prophetically significant location within the Davidic covenant structure from the beginning.
27.13: Maherāy hannetoṑātî (“Maharai the Netophathite”) — tenth month; lazzarḥî (“Zerahite line”). Netophah was a Judahite town near Bethlehem (2:54; Neh 7:26; Ezra 2:22). Its appearance here and in the post-exilic return lists creates a chronological bridge: the families in David’s administration are the same families whose descendants return from exile, demonstrating institutional continuity across the catastrophe of the exile.
27.14: Bənāyāh happirrᵉʾātônî (“Benaiah the Pirathonite”) — eleventh month; mibbənê ʾEprayim (“from the sons of Ephraim”). A second Ephraimite division commander (cf. v10) confirms that northern tribal representation in the military system is substantive, not merely a single token. The repeated Ephraimite presence makes the inclusion of the dominant northern tribe a structural feature, not an exception.
27.15: Ḥeldāy hannetoṑātî (“Heldai the Netophathite”) — twelfth month; ləʾotnîʾēl (“from Othniel”). Othniel was the first judge of Israel (Judg 3:9–11), a Calebite-Judahite deliverer who set the pattern for the entire era of judges. The final month’s commander derives his lineage from Israel’s inaugural deliverer, giving the twelve-month military roster a narrative arc that sweeps from the judges through the Davidic monarchy — covenant history encoded in administrative structure.
27.16: The tribal officer list opens with Reuben (ʾĕlîʾezer ben Ziḵrî) and Simeon (Šəpaṭyāh ben Maʾăḵāh). That Reuben appears — despite having forfeited the birthright (5:1–2) — reflects the Chronicler’s principle that administrative recognition outlasts internal re-ranking; all tribes retain named representation within the covenant community regardless of their hierarchical standing. The nāgîd or śar title for each tribal officer marks them as the king’s commissioned agents within their tribal domains.
27.17: For the Levites: Ḥăšaḇyāh ben Qəmûʾēl; for Aaron: Ṣādôq. The Levites and Aaronides appear in the tribal list alongside secular tribes rather than in a separate cultic category, showing that the Chronicler treats priestly administration as continuous with civil administration. Zadok, who will oversee the ark’s final installation (1 Kgs 8:4) and whose line will define legitimate priesthood through Ezekiel’s vision (Ezek 44:15–16), already holds the Aaronide slot in David’s administrative system.
27.18: For Judah: ʾĕlîhû mēʾaḥê Dāwîḇ (“Elihu, one of David’s brothers”). The royal tribe’s tribal officer is David’s own brother — the royal family administers itself through its own kinship. This Elihu may be the brother called Eliab or a variant name for one of Jesse’s sons (1 Sam 16:6). For Issachar: ʾOmrî ben Mîḵāʾēl. The assignment of a competent officer to each tribe means that David’s authority extends through personal or trusted intermediaries to every part of the covenant community.
27.19: For Zebulun: Yišmaʿyāh ben ʾOḇadyāh; for Naphtali: Yərîmôt ben ʾAzrîʾēl. These northern tribes — Zebulun and Naphtali — are the ones Isaiah would later name as the first to suffer Assyrian conquest and the first to see the “great light” (Isa 9:1–2; cf. Matt 4:15–16). Their presence in David’s administrative system underscores that the entire covenant community was once integrated before the fracture of the exile.
27.20: For Ephraim: Hôšēaʾ ben ʾAzazyāhû; for the half-tribe of Manasseh: Yôʾēl ben Pədāyāhû. That Ephraim receives a civil tribal officer in addition to its military division commanders (vv10, 14) confirms the tribe’s importance in David’s administrative imagination. The northern tribes were never merely subordinate appendages in the Chronicler’s vision of the united kingdom; they are full participants with their own administrative infrastructure.
27.21: For Gilead-Manasseh (the eastern half): ʾIddô ben Zəḵaryāhû; for Benjamin: Yāʾăsîʾēl ben ʾAbnēr. The inclusion of the Transjordanian Manassite half-tribe means that tribes living east of the Jordan — the most geographically vulnerable to absorption by neighboring powers — have formal administrative representation in the royal system. The covenant community is defined by covenant affiliation, not by geography.
27.22: For Dan: ʾAzaral ben Yərôḥām. ʾēlleh śārê šiḇṭê yiśrāʾēl (“these were the princes of the tribes of Israel”) — the concluding summary frames the entire tribal officer list as a comprehensive covenant register. Dan, despite its later associations with northern apostasy (Jeroboam’s golden calves at Dan; Judg 18), is included without stigma. The Chronicler records the Davidic administration as it was at its finest moment, before the fractures that history would bring.
27.24: lōʾ šillēm (“did not complete it”) — the verb šālem also means “to make whole, to pay, to make peace”; the census was never “paid off” or reconciled. The result was qeṣep (“wrath”) on Israel — the technical term for divine anger at covenant violation. wəlōʾ ʾālāh hammispār bəsēper dibrê hayyāmîm lammelek Dāwîḇ (“and the number did not come up into the king’s book of chronicles”) — the incomplete census was deliberately excluded from the official administrative record. The Chronicler records the non-recording, making the gap in the record itself a theological statement: what is tainted by sin does not enter the royal archives.
27.25: ʾAzmāweṭ ben ʾădîʾēl — over the royal storehouses; Yəhônāṭān ben ʾUzzyāyû — over the country storehouses in cities, villages, and towers. The distinction between central court storage and distributed provincial storage reflects sophisticated administrative awareness of supply logistics. The term ʾōṭār (“storehouse, treasury”) appears throughout the Psalms and wisdom literature as the vehicle of YHWH’s providential abundance (cf. Ps 135:7; Prov 8:21; Deut 28:12).
27.26: ʾEzrî ben Kəlûḇ — over the agricultural workers who worked the ground (ʿāḇāmāh). The verb ʾāḇaḇ (“to work, to serve”) shares its root with ʾēḇēḇ (“servant”) and ʾăḇôdāh (“service, worship”). The organization of agricultural labor under royal stewardship uses the same vocabulary as the organization of priestly service: working the land is a form of covenant service to YHWH, who gave the land and its fertility as a covenant blessing.
27.27: Šimʾî hārāmātî — over the vineyards; Zabdî haššipʹmî — over wine production and the wine cellars. The vineyard (kerem) as a covenant image pervades the prophetic tradition: Israel as YHWH’s vineyard (Isa 5:1–7; Ps 80:8–16). The royal administration of vineyards thus operates within an already theologically charged agricultural vocabulary. That the wine cellar steward has a specific title and name demonstrates the institutional weight given to what might seem like a minor administrative role.
27.28: Baʾal Ḥānān haggədērî — over olive trees and sycamore trees in the Shephelah (šəpēlāh, “the lowland”); Yôʾāš — over the oil stores. The olive tree is a central symbol of covenant blessing in Deuteronomy (8:8; 28:40) and the prophets (Zech 4:3; Hab 3:17). The Shephelah, contested between Judah and the Philistines, was politically sensitive territory; its administrative officer represents both agricultural and strategic concerns. Royal control of olive production means control of Israel’s primary cooking fat, lamp fuel, and ritual anointing oil.
27.29: Šiṭray haššārônî — over herds in the Sharon plain; Šāpāṭ ben ʾAdlay — over valley herds. The Sharon plain, Israel’s most fertile coastal region, was proverbial for its beauty and productivity (Isa 35:2; 65:10; Song 2:1). Royal management of Sharon herds represents oversight of Israel’s prime pastureland. The valley herds would have been managed in the Jezreel and other lowland areas, similarly valuable for livestock production.
27.30: ʾOḇîl hayyišməʾēlî (“Obil the Ishmaelite”) — over the camels. The appointment of an Ishmaelite as chief camel handler is culturally precise: the Ishmaelites were renowned as camel traders in the ancient Near East (Gen 37:25; Judg 8:24). The royal administration employs non-Israelite expertise where expertise exists — the covenant community’s stewardship of creation includes wisdom that crosses ethnic boundaries. Yəḥdəyāhû hammērônôtî — over the donkeys, the primary pack animal for agricultural and commercial transport.
27.31: Yāzîz hahagərî (“Jaziz the Hagerite”) — over the flocks. The Hagrites were a Transjordanian group against whom Reuben fought and prevailed (5:10, 18–22); the incorporation of a Hagrite officer into the royal administration transforms a former opponent into a royal servant — a miniature of the Davidic vision in which surrounding peoples come under Israel’s administrative umbrella. kol ʾēlleh śārê hārəḵûš (“all these were princes of the property”) — the comprehensive stewardship language (rəḵûš, “property, possessions”) frames the entire list as an exercise of delegated dominion over the covenant land’s resources.
27.32: Yəhônāṭān dôd Dāwîḇ (“Jonathan the uncle of David”) — a counselor; sofer mevin (“a discerning scribe”). The combination of scribal skill and discernment (mevin) for a royal advisor anticipates the wisdom tradition that will characterize Solomon’s court. Yəhîʾēl ben Ḥaḵmônî was with the king’s sons — the role of tutor or companion to the royal princes is an important court function; the next generation of Davidic leadership must be formed under wise guidance from within the court circle.
27.33: ʾĎîṭōpēl yôʾēṣ lammelek (“Ahithophel, the king’s counselor”) — listed without any mention of his subsequent betrayal (2 Sam 15–17); the Chronicler records the administrative structure as it stood before the narrative complications. Ḥûšay hāʾarkaʾî rēaʾ hammelek (“Hushai the Archite, the king’s friend”) — rēaʾ (“friend, companion”) is a specific court title in ANE monarchies (cf. the Egyptian “King’s Friend”), not merely an affectionate description. Hushai’s counter-intelligence role against Ahithophel in 2 Sam 15–17 is the most dramatic episode in the history of this court position.
27.34: After Ahithophel, Jehoiada son of Benaiah and Abiathar served as counselors. Yôʾāḇ śar ṣəḇāʾ hammelek (“Joab was the commander of the king’s army”) — the chapter closes with Joab, whose military command was the practical instrument through which everything else in this chapter operated. The final word of ch27 belongs to the man whose relationship with David was always complex yet indispensable: no administrative elegance could function without the force Joab commanded. The Chronicler acknowledges this without apology or comment.

28.9: David’s charge to Solomon — kî YHWH ḥōqēr kol lᵉḇāḇôt wᵉḵol yeṣer maḥăšāḇôt mēḇîn, ‘for YHWH searches all hearts and understands every plan and thought’ — uses two key vocabulary items. The verb ḥāqar (to search, probe, investigate) appears in Ps 139:1 (YHWH ḥᵉqartanî wattēḏāʿ, ‘O YHWH, you have searched me and known me’) and Jer 17:10 (ʾănî YHWH ḥōqēr lēḇ, ‘I YHWH search the heart’). The noun yeṣer (inclination, plan, formation — from yāṣar, to form/shape) appears in Gen 6:5 where ‘every yeṣer of the thoughts of the human heart was only evil continually’ — the diagnosis that precedes the flood. Here David uses the same yeṣer in the context of worship-formation: if Solomon seeks YHWH, the yeṣer maḥăšāḇôt (inclination of his thoughts) will be known and approved; if he forsakes YHWH, he will be cast off. The NT applies the same divine heart-knowledge: Heb 4:13 (panta de gymna kai tetrachēlismena tois ophthalmois autou, ‘all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account’) and John 2:25 (‘he himself knew what was in man’). The ḥōqēr lēḇāḇôt is the consistent OT attribute that grounds both judgment and pastoral care: YHWH’s searching is not surveillance but the omniscience of the covenant God who knows what his covenant partner needs before asking.
28.19: The divine inscription of the temple plan — hakkōl bikᵉtāḇ miyyaḏ YHWH ʿālay hiśkîl kōl malʾᵉḵôt hattabnît, ‘all this in writing from the hand of YHWH; he made me understand all the works of the plan’ — frames the temple blueprint (tabnît) as divinely revealed, parallel to the tabernacle blueprint given to Moses. In Exod 25:9, YHWH instructs Moses: kᵉḵōl ʾăšer ʾănî marʾeh ʾôtᵉḵā ʾēt tabnît hammiškan, ‘according to all that I show you, the tabnît of the tabernacle’; in Exod 25:40, the command is to make everything bᵉtabnîtām (according to their pattern), the verse that Heb 8:5 cites: horate, phēsin, poiēseis panta kata ton typon ton deichthenta soi en tō orei — ‘see that you make everything according to the pattern that was shown you on the mountain.’ The Chronicler’s use of tabnît for both the tabernacle-vocabulary and the temple-plan creates a deliberate parallel: David receives the temple-tabnît as Moses received the tabernacle-tabnît — both from YHWH, both in writing or vision, both then executed by a designated builder (Moses by Bezalel; David by Solomon). Hebrews reads the tabernacle’s tabnît as a shadow of the heavenly sanctuary (Heb 8:5; 9:23-24); the Chronicler’s extension of the same pattern to the temple suggests the entire earthly sanctuary sequence (tabernacle → temple) is a single divine pedagogical movement toward what Heb 9:11 calls ‘the greater and more perfect tent, not made with hands.’
28.1: Dāwîḇ hiqhîl (“David assembled”) — the Hiphil of qāhal (to assemble), the root of qāhāl (“assembly, congregation”). The comprehensive list of those summoned — tribal commanders, military division officers, stewards, royal sons, court officials, and mighty men — encapsulates the entire administrative structure of chs24–27. David convenes everything he has organized in a single final assembly; the bureaucratic investment of the preceding chapters exists for this moment of covenant transfer.
28.2: wayyāqom Dāwîḇ hammelek ʾal raglāyw (“King David stood on his feet”) — the phrase implies effort from one who is old and enfeebled (29:1); David’s standing is an act of royal dignity in the face of physical decline. bêt mānôaḥ (“house of rest”) — the temple envisioned as the permanent resting place (mānôaḥ) for the ark; mānôaḥ shares its root with mənûḥāh (“rest”), the word underlying Solomon’s name in 22:9. The ark needs rest; Israel needs rest; the temple is the covenant community’s rest made architectural.
28.3: ʾattāh lōʾ ṯiḇneh lî habbayiṭ (“you shall not build me the house”) — YHWH’s direct refusal is stated plainly, without softening. kî ʾîš milḥāmôṭ ʾattāh wədāmîm šāpaḵ„ā (“for you are a man of wars and have shed blood”) — the Chronicler makes explicit what 2 Sam 7 left implicit: blood-guilt disqualifies a builder of the sanctuary. The principle is cultic: the holy space requires a builder with unstained hands (cf. Exod 20:25 on uncut stones for the altar; Num 19:11–22 on corpse impurity). David’s wars were YHWH-commanded; yet the holiness logic of the sanctuary demands the distinction.
28.4: wayyiḇḥar YHWH ʾělōhê yiśrāʾēl bî (“yet YHWH the God of Israel chose me”) — David pivots from prohibition to election. The verb bāḥar (“chose”) is the technical term of covenant-election theology (Deut 7:6; 14:2; Isa 41:8). The election sequence David narrates narrows: from all Israel’s house → from all Judah’s house → from Jesse’s sons → David himself. This narrowing election mirrors the Genesis pattern (Abr → Isaac → Jacob → Judah → Perez → David): YHWH’s purpose concentrates through increasingly specific choices.
28.5: kissēʾ malkût YHWH ʾal yiśrāʾēl (“the throne of the kingdom of YHWH over Israel”) — David does not say “my throne” or “the throne of Israel” but YHWH’s throne. The Davidic monarchy is explicitly identified as the earthly instantiation of divine kingship — a theme that appears again in 29:23 (“Solomon sat on the throne of YHWH”). This high claim about royal theology provides the Christological foundation: the one who inherits YHWH’s throne permanently (Rev 3:21; 22:1) is the one in whom the Davidic promise reaches its ultimate referent.
28.6: Šəlōmōh ḇinəḵā hûʾ yiḇneh bêtî (“Solomon your son — he will build my house”) — the emphatic hûʾ (“he, himself”) singles out Solomon as the uniquely designated builder. kî ḇāḥartî ḇô lî ləḇēn (“for I have chosen him to be my son”) — the divine adoption formula (cf. Ps 2:7) is here given its historical application: David reports YHWH’s completed choice (perfect bāḥartî). The son-of-God status is simultaneously a royal designation and a pattern pointing beyond its immediate holder to the one in whom divine sonship is not a granted title but an eternal reality.
28.7: wahᵃḵînôtî ʾet malkûtô ʾad ləʾôlām (“I will establish his kingdom forever”) — ʾim yeḥəzaq laʾăśôt miṣwôtay (“if he remains firm to do my commandments”). The eternal promise (ʾad ləʾôlām) is coupled with a conditional clause (ʾim). The Chronicler holds both: the Davidic promise is unconditional in its ultimate scope (2 Sam 7:16; Ps 89:28–37) but conditional in its specific historical instantiation through any particular Davidic king. The resolution the Chronicler implies: the dynasty’s eternal dimension survives Solomon’s failure through a future Davidic heir who will keep the commandments perfectly.
28.8: šimrû wədiršû kol miṣwôt YHWH ʾělōhêḵem (“keep and seek all the commandments of YHWH your God”) — the pairing of šāmar (“keep, guard”) and dāraš (“seek, inquire”) is the Chronicler’s signature vocabulary for covenant faithfulness: keeping what is known and actively seeking what is not yet explicit. ləmaʾan tîrəšû ʾet hāʾāreṣ (“so that you may possess the land”) and pass it as an inheritance to your sons forever — land-possession remains the sign of covenant blessing, and obedience remains its condition. The Abrahamic promise is still operative within the Davidic order.
28.10: daʾ kî YHWH bāḥar bəḵā (“know that YHWH has chosen you”) — the command to know (dāʾ) one’s election precedes the command to act; identity grounds vocation. liḇnôt bayit lammiqdāš (“to build a house for the sanctuary”) — hammiqdāš (“the sanctuary”) is the abstract noun from qādaš (“to be holy”); the building project is the spatial instantiation of holiness. ḥăzaq waʾăśēh (“be strong and do it”) — the shortest form of the Mosaic commissioning formula, which receives its fullest expression in v20.
28.11: David gives Solomon the tabnît — a word meaning “pattern, plan, model, likeness” (used in Exod 25:9, 40 for the heavenly tabernacle pattern shown to Moses; Deut 4:16–18 for idolatrous images; Ps 144:12 for the form of children). The architectural tabnît includes the ʾûlām (portico), its bāttîm (house-chambers), gənāzāyw (storerooms), ʾalyôtāyw (upper rooms), hădārāyw happinîmîm (inner chambers), and qōdeš haqqoḫāšîm (Holy of Holies). The enumeration moves from outer to inner, mapping the graduated approach from public to sacred that structures the entire sanctuary system.
28.12: hakkōl bārûaḥ ʾimmô (“all of it by the Spirit with him”) — the architectural plan came through the divine Spirit (rûaḥ). The same Spirit that hovered over creation’s waters (Gen 1:2), equipped Bezalel for tabernacle craftsmanship (Exod 31:3), rested on Israel’s leaders and prophets, now gives David the temple’s design. The courts of the LORD’s house, the surrounding chambers, the treasury storerooms, the upper rooms and inner rooms, the place for the ark — all of this was Spirit-given, not architecturally devised. The temple is a Spirit-authored space.
28.13: maḥᵃlōqôt hakkōhanîm wəhalləwiyyîm (“the divisions of priests and Levites”) are included in the tabnît — the architectural plan encompasses the personnel system. The same term maḥᵃlōqôt (“divisions, courses”) used for the military rotation in ch27 now describes the priestly rotation; military and cultic administration are organized by the same structural principle. ḥᵃluqqat hāʾᵃḇôdāh (“the division of service”) means the blueprint David gives is not just for a building but for a complete institutional system — architecture and personnel are a single integrated plan.
28.14: The weights (mišqāl) of gold for the gold vessels and silver for the silver vessels are specified ləḵol kəlê ʾăḇôdāh wāʾăḇôdāh (“for every vessel of each kind of service”). The precision of weights for sacred objects follows the pattern of Moses at Sinai, where every tabernacle vessel was specified in exact weight and material (Exod 25–31). That David can hand Solomon a plan with specific weights means the heavenly prototype has been translated into concrete provisioning — the divine instruction has already been worked out in material terms.
28.15: Gold for the lampstands (mənôrôt) and their lamps, with the specified weight of each lampstand and each lamp; silver for the silver lampstands by weight. The mənôrāh (“lampstand”) was the symbol of YHWH’s light in the tabernacle (Exod 25:31–40; Num 8:1–4). Zechariah’s vision of the golden lampstand (Zech 4:2–10) identifies it with YHWH’s eyes scanning the whole earth. That David’s plan specifies not one but multiple lampstands for the temple represents the expansion of divine light into the new and larger space of covenant dwelling.
28.16: Gold for the showbread tables (šulḥān plural šulḥānôt) and silver for the silver tables. The šulḥān (“table”) carried the leḥem pānîm (“bread of the face/presence”) — twelve loaves renewed weekly, representing the twelve tribes permanently before YHWH (Lev 24:5–9). The temple’s showbread table continues a practice established in the tabernacle (Exod 25:23–30): the covenant community is permanently presented as an offering before the divine Presence, tribe by tribe, loaf by loaf.
28.17: Gold for the forks (mizlāgôt), sprinkling bowls (mizrāqôt), and cups (qɘšāwôt); and the weight of each golden bowl and silver bowl. Every implement of the priestly liturgical system is specified by weight and material. The mizrāq (“sprinkling bowl”) was used for the presentation and sprinkling of sacrificial blood — the central act of covenant atonement. That its weight and material are divinely specified confirms that the atonement mechanism itself is a heavenly institution, not a human invention.
28.18: Refined gold for the incense altar (mizbaḥ haqqəṭôret) by weight; and gold for the model (tabnît) of the chariot-throne of the cherubim (kərûḇîm pôrəśê kənāpayim, “cherubim spreading their wings”) that covered the ark. The cherub-throne is not merely an architectural element but a cosmic image: YHWH enthroned above the cherubim is a royal title throughout the Psalms and prophets (Ps 80:1; 99:1; 2 Kgs 19:15; Isa 37:16). The final element of the tabnît that David transmits to Solomon is not a floor plan but the image of the divine throne — the ultimate purpose of the entire building is to be the earthly footstool of the heavenly King.
28.20: ḥăzaq wəʾēmāṣ waʾăśēh (“be strong and courageous and do it”) — the fullest form of the Mosaic commissioning formula, traceable through Deut 31:6–7 (Moses to Israel and Joshua), Josh 1:6, 9 (YHWH to Joshua), and now David to Solomon. The three-verb sequence (strength + courage + action) is the complete commissioning structure. ʾal tîrāʾ wəʾal tēḥāt (“do not fear and do not be dismayed”) — the prohibition of yārēʾ (fear) and ḥātat (dismay) covers both the emotional and volitional domains. kî YHWH ʾělōhîm ʾělōhay ʾimmāḵā (“for YHWH God, my God, is with you”) — the covenant-presence formula (ʿimmāḵā) transmitted through David to Solomon: the presence that was with Moses (Exod 3:12), promised to Joshua (Josh 1:5), is now spoken by David’s lips as a gift to his son. lōʾ yarpeḵā wəlōʾ yaʾazḇekkā (“he will not let you down and will not forsake you”) — the double negated abandonment verb, the covenant’s ultimate promise.
28.21: The final verse describes total preparedness: the priestly and Levitical courses (maḥᵃlōqôt) are ready for every act of service; all willing and skilled craftsmen (nādîḇ bəḵol melaḵāh) for every aspect of the work. wəḵol hāʾām ləḵol dəḇāreḵā (“and all the people are at your every word”) — Solomon has everything: divinely given plan, organized personnel, accumulated materials (22:14; 29:2–5), and the entire nation’s obedience. The chapter ends not with a command but with a gift: everything necessary for the building is already in place. The only remaining question is whether Solomon will execute it — and the answer to that question will define the entire subsequent history of the covenant people.

16.1: The ark is placed inside the tent (ʾōhel) David pitched — not the Mosaic tabernacle (mišķān), which remains at Gibeon (v39). This creates the two-sanctuary situation unique to David's reign: the ark without the tabernacle. The offerings here — ʿōlōt (burnt offerings) and šᵉlāmîm (peace/fellowship offerings) — are both required for covenant-inaugural ceremonies (cf. Exod 24:5).
16.2: David blesses the people bᵉšēm YHWH — in the name of YHWH. The verb bārak (to bless) in the piel, used of a king blessing the people, recalls Solomon's blessing at the temple dedication (1 Kgs 8:55-56) and Moses blessing Israel (Deut 33:1). Only priests typically blessed the assembly (Num 6:22-27); David's priestly-kingly role here is significant for the Chronicler's portrait.
16.3: The distribution of food: kikkār leḥem (a loaf of bread), ešpār (a cut of meat — the term appears only here and 2 Sam 6:19, likely a specific cut or portion), and ʾăšîšāh (raisin cake, associated with festive celebration, cf. Song 2:5). The communal meal following a covenant ceremony signals the completion of the event and the people's full incorporation into the celebration.
16.4: David appoints Levites to minister (lᵉšārēt) before the ark — three functions: lᵉhazķîr (to invoke, lit. “to cause to remember” — possibly a type of petition-prayer), lᵉhôdôt (to give thanks — hiphil of yādāh), and lᵉhallēl (to praise — piel of hālal). These three liturgical acts frame the psalm that follows and define the theological purpose of the Davidic worship establishment.
16.5: Asaph as chief (hārōʾš) of the Levitical worship ministers — the same Asaph after whom twelve of the psalms are titled (Pss 50, 73-83). The assignment of Obed-edom (cf. ch13) to a musical role here rehabilitates a figure who had hosted the ark; his family became a permanent fixture in the Jerusalem temple establishment.
16.6: The two priests, Benaiah and Jahaziel, are assigned taʿăśe baḥăṣōṣᵉrôt — to sound the trumpets (ḥăṣôṣᵉrôt) continually. The ḥăṣōṣᵉrāh (silver trumpet) was a distinctly priestly instrument (Num 10:1-10), different from the shofar; its continuous sounding marked the ongoing liturgical presence of the ark. The dual assignees mirrors the two silver trumpets of Num 10:2.
16.7: Bᵉyôm hahûʾ bāz dāwîd lᵉrōʾš lᵉhôdôt laYHWH bᵉyad-ʾĀsāp wᵉʾeḥāyw — on that day David first appointed Asaph and his brothers. The word bāz (then, at that time) marks the occasion as the inaugural moment of the Davidic liturgical arrangement. The psalm that follows (vv8-36) is presented as the foundational act of worship — composed or assembled by David, delivered through Asaph.
16.8: hôdû laYHWH qirᵉʾû bišmô — Give thanks to the LORD, call on his name. This opening matches Ps 105:1 almost verbatim. hôdû is the hiphil imperative of yādāh, a verb whose root meaning includes “to throw/cast” and has developed the theological sense of acknowledging what is true about someone — here, YHWH's acts. hôdîʿû bāʿammîm ʿălîlôtāyw — make known his deeds among the peoples: the proclamation is explicitly universal in scope from the first verse.
16.9: šîrû-lô zammᵉrû-lô — sing to him, make music to him.
