25.3: From Jeduthun’s line: his six sons serve under Jeduthun’s direction ‘with the lyre, to prophesy in giving thanks and praising YHWH’ (ʿal-yĕdê yêdûtûn bannĕḇîyim bĕḵinnôr lāmôḏōt ûlāhallêl YHWH). The prepositional phrase lāmôḏōt ûlāhallêl (to give thanks and to praise) defines the prophetic content of the music: thankgiving (yāḏāh, Hiphil infinitive) and praise (hālal, Piel infinitive). Temple prophetic music is functionally oriented toward tôḏāh (thanksgiving offering/declaration) and hll (praise) — the two poles of the Levitical worship vocabulary.
25.4: From Heman’s line: his sons Bukkiah, Mattaniah, Uzziel, Shebuel, Jerimoth, Hananiah, Hanani, Eliathah, Giddalti, Romamtiezer, Joshbekashah, Mallothi, Hothir, Mahazioth. These fourteen names have been noted by scholars as forming, with some emendation, a prayer-poem: ‘I have been favored [by God]; have mercy on me, Lord; have mercy on me; Thou art my God, I praise Thee, I raise up [the oracle]; Thou sittest amid adversity; I see [visions] abundantly; and I spoke, by the grace of God, for the exaltation of the Horn.’ If this reading is correct (it is debated), Heman’s fourteen sons’ names are encoded liturgical language.
25.5: ĥêmān ḥôzeh hammelek — ‘Heman the king’s seer.’ ḥôzeh is the participle of ḥāzāh (to see, behold in vision) — a seer receives visual prophetic revelation. The same term describes Gad (2 Sam 24:11; 1 Chr 21:9), Asaph (2 Chr 29:30 ‘Asaph the seer’), and Jeduthun (2 Chr 35:15 ‘Jeduthun the seer’). All three temple musician-leaders are seers. ‘Given according to God’s word (dibrê hāʾĕlōhîm) to exalt his horn (lĕrûm qeren).’ rûm qeren (to raise the horn) is the idiom for bestowing strength and honor: the horn raised high = exaltation (cf. Ps 89:17; 92:10; 1 Sam 2:10 ‘YHWH will exalt the horn of his anointed’). God gave Heman fourteen sons to exalt YHWH’s glory.
25.6: ‘All these sons served under their father’s direction (ʿal-yêḏê ʾăḇîhem) in music for the LORD’s house, with cymbals (bĕmĕṣiltāyim), lyres (ḇinnĕḇālîm), and harps (ûḇĕkinnôrôt).’ The three instruments enumerated — cymbals, lyres, harps — are the characteristic temple orchestra. The term mĕṣiltayim (cymbals) appears only in Chronicles and Ezra-Nehemiah for the specifically Levitical cymbal-instrument (as distinct from ṣĕlṣĕlîm used in other contexts). Ps 150:4-5 employs the same instruments in a concluding doxology.
25.7: ‘Including their skilled fellow musicians (mĕḇînîm) who were trained in the LORD’s songs (mĕlummĕḏîm šîr YHWH), the total was two hundred eighty-eight.’ mĕḇîn (discerning, understanding, skilled) is the Hiphil participle of bîn (to understand, discern). The 288 comes from 24 courses × 12 members each; the mathematical structure mirrors the 24 priestly courses. mĕlummĕḏîm (trained, taught) is the Pual participle of lāmad (to learn, teach) — the same root as the noun talmîḏ (disciple/student) in the following verse.
25.8: ‘They cast lots for their duty assignments, regardless of rank (kĕqāṭōn kêgāḏôl), whether teacher or student (mêḇîn ʿim-talmîḏ).’ talmîḏ (disciple, student) is one of the rarest words in the OT — appearing only here and in Isa 8:16 (‘bind up the testimony, seal the law among my disciples’). The term that becomes central in NT usage (the Twelve as Jesus’ mathētai, Gk. translation of talmîḏ) has its OT root in temple music education. The lot ensures that the divine appointment falls ‘whether great or small, teacher or student’ — the lot is democratic in the sense that it reflects YHWH’s choice alone.
25.9: The first lot fell to Joseph from Asaph’s family (yôsēp libnê-ʾāsāp) — he and his brothers and sons, twelve. The organizational unit of each course is twelve (the number of the tribes, the complete Israel). The total of 24 × 12 = 288 encodes both the complete liturgical cycle (24) and the complete Israel (12). Each course serves for one week in a two-week rotation pattern (24 courses × 2 = 48 weeks; the remaining weeks fall at the three pilgrimage festivals when all priests/Levites serve together).
25.10: The second lot fell to Gedaliah (gĕdalyāhû) — he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Gedaliah means ‘YHWH is great.’ The name is also borne by the ill-fated governor of Judah after the Babylonian conquest (2 Kgs 25:22-25; Jer 40-41). The musician Gedaliah and the governor Gedaliah share a name that expresses the same theological confession; the name’s meaning endures through both uses.
25.11: Third lot: Zaccur (zakkûr), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Zaccur means ‘remembered’ (Niphal participle of zākar) — the one whom [YHWH] remembers. The same name appears among the gatekeepers (26:2) and in the post-exilic period (Neh 3:2; 10:12; 13:13). The lot-assignment formula (third lot / name / family size twelve) will repeat with minor variation through v31.
25.12: Fourth lot: Izri (yiṣrî), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Izri does not appear in the earlier Jeduthun list (v3, where the parallel slot has ‘Zeri’ ṣerî). The variation (Izri/Zeri) is likely a spelling variant: in Hebrew the letters yod-zayin-resh-yod can be confused with zayin-resh-yod. The LXX and other textual witnesses show variation here; the variant names are the same course.
25.13: Fifth lot: Nethaniah (nĕtanyāhû), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Nethaniah means ‘YHWH has given.’ The same name is borne by Ishmael son of Nethaniah, who assassinated Gedaliah the governor (2 Kgs 25:25; Jer 40:14). The Levitical musician and the violent Ishmael share the same patronym; the name represents a common Second Temple-period theological affirmation.
25.14: Sixth lot: Bukkiah (buqqiyyāhû), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Bukkiah means ‘proven/tried by YHWH’ (from bāqaʿ or bāqaq). The name is unique to this musical family in the OT.
25.15: Seventh lot: Jesharelah (yĕšareʾêlāh), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. The name does not appear elsewhere in this exact form. In the earlier list (v2) Asaph’s son is called ‘Asarelah’ (ʾăśarʾêlāh), which is likely the same name with a different spelling of the theophoric element. The variant spellings within the same chapter reflect the fluidity of pre-standardized Hebrew orthography.
25.16: Eighth lot: Jeshaiah (yĕšaʿyāhû), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Jeshaiah means ‘YHWH is salvation’ — the same semantic content as Isaiah and Joshua/Jesus. The theophoric name group yāšaʿ (to save) + YHWH produces the vocabulary of Israel’s salvation hope.
25.17: Ninth lot: Mattaniah (mattanyāhû), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Mattaniah means ‘gift of YHWH.’ This is the original name of King Zedekiah before Nebuchadnezzar changed it (2 Kgs 24:17). The Levitical musician and the last king of Judah share the same birth name; Mattaniah = ‘YHWH’s gift’ while Zedekiah = ‘YHWH is my righteousness.’
25.18: Tenth lot: Shimei (šimʿî), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Shimei means ‘YHWH has heard.’ The name appears for many OT figures including the Benjaminite who cursed David (2 Sam 16:5-13) and the Levite who led post-exilic revival in Ezra 10:23.
25.19: Eleventh lot: Azareel (ʿăzarʾêl), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Azareel means ‘God has helped’ (same as Azarel or Azriel). In v2 Asaph’s son is listed by a different name for this slot, suggesting that Azareel may be an alternate name or that the course took the name of its original head’s family designation rather than his birth-name.
25.20: Twelfth lot: Hashabiah (ḥăšaḇyāhû), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Hashabiah means ‘YHWH has considered/reckoned’ (from ḥāšaḇ, to think, plan, reckon). The name appears 12 times in Chronicles, Ezra, and Nehemiah for various Levitical officials — it was clearly a common name in Levitical families of the post-exilic period.
25.21: Thirteenth lot: Shubael (šûḇāʾêl), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Shubael means ‘return to God’ or ‘captive of God.’ This is identified in 23:16 as the head of Gershom’s (Moses’s son’s) line — the musician Shubael descends from Moses’s grandson, making this musical course carry the Mosaic lineage into the musical division.
25.22: Fourteenth lot: Mattithiah (mattityāhû), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Mattithiah means ‘gift of YHWH’ (from nātan + Yāh, differently constructed from Mattaniah). Mattithiah appears in Ezra 10:43 as one who married a foreign woman and agreed to put her away, and in Neh 8:4 as one who stood beside Ezra as he read the law — a significant liturgical role.
25.23: Fifteenth lot: Jeremoth (yĕrêmôt), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Jeremoth means ‘heights’ or ‘high places.’ In v3’s parallel list of Jeduthun’s sons, the corresponding name is ‘Jeshaiah’ — either a different son was named to this course in the lot assignment, or the names represent variants.
25.24: Sixteenth lot: Hananiah (ḥănanyāhû), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Hananiah means ‘YHWH is gracious.’ Among the most common OT theophoric names, Hananiah appears for the three friends of Daniel (Hananiah = Shadrach in Dan 1:6-7), showing that the name bridges the musical and the prophetic streams of Israelite tradition.
25.25: Seventeenth lot: Joshbekashah (yošbeqāšāh), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. The name is unusual and its meaning uncertain; it may mean ‘dwelling in hardship’ or ‘sitting in a hard place.’ It appears only in this musical context.
25.26: Eighteenth lot: Hanani (ḥănānî), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Hanani means ‘gracious’ or ‘YHWH is gracious.’ Hanani the seer rebuked King Asa for trusting Syria rather than YHWH (2 Chr 16:7-10) and was imprisoned for it; Nehemiah’s brother Hanani brought news of Jerusalem’s broken walls (Neh 1:2). The name’s frequency reflects its theological content.
25.27: Nineteenth lot: Mallothi (mallôtî), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Mallothi means ‘I have spoken’ or ‘utterance’ — a verb-form name from millêl (to speak). The speaking-name for a musician who serves in prophetic musical proclamation has an internal coherence: the musician speaks YHWH’s word through the medium of sacred song.
25.28: Twentieth lot: Eliathah (ʾĕlyātāh), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Eliathah means ‘my God has come’ or ‘God appears.’ The name encodes the theology of divine presence — God comes, God is present — which is the fundamental premise of the temple itself.
25.29: Twenty-first lot: Hothir (hôtîr), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Hothir means ‘abundance, surplus, excess’ (from yātar, to leave over, be abundant). The name of abundance for one of the temple’s musicians gestures toward the worship theme of overflowing gratitude.
25.30: Twenty-second lot: Giddalti (giddaltî), he and his brothers and sons, twelve. Giddalti means ‘I have magnified/exalted’ (Piel perfect of gāḏal: to make great, magnify). The first-person verb as a name is a testimony-form: ‘I have magnified [the Lord].’ Ps 34:3 ‘magnify YHWH with me’ (gaddêlû YHWH ʾittî) uses the same root in the imperative.
25.31: Twenty-third lot: Mahazioth (maḥăzyôt); twenty-fourth lot: Romamtiezer (rômamtîʿezer). Romamtiezer is the most distinctive name in the list — it means ‘I have exalted his help’ or ‘exaltation of help.’ The root rûm (to exalt) + ʿezer (help) combines the exaltation-vocabulary of v5 (lĕrûm qeren, to exalt the horn) with the help-vocabulary of the divine name ‘Eliezer’ (my God is help) in 23:15. The twenty-fourth course closes the musical cycle on a note of exaltation and divine assistance — both themes central to Levitical temple worship.

19.1: The chapter opens with the death of Nahash king of the Ammonites — the same Nahash whose defeat by Saul had established the latter's kingship (1 Sam 11). David's declaration, ʾeʿĕśeh ḥesed ʿim-Ḥānûn ben-Nāḥāš kî-ʿāśāh ʾāḇîw ḥesed ʿimmādî — “I will show ḥesed to Hanun son of Nahash, because his father showed ḥesed to me” — frames the mission in covenant-loyalty vocabulary. ḥesed denotes the reciprocal obligation of treaty partners; David's initiative honors the alliance established with the late king.
19.2: David sends a delegation lᵉnaḥămô — to comfort/console him (nāḥam piel). The verb is the standard one for mourning consolation (cf. Gen 37:35; 2 Sam 12:24). That David sends officials (ʿăḇādîm, lit. servants) to perform this duty rather than going himself reflects ancient Near Eastern protocol for inter-royal mourning diplomacy. The mission is cast as an act of covenant maintenance (ḥesed) carrying political implications.
19.3: Hanun's advisors interpret the delegation with suspicion: hălᵉḵabbēd ʾet-ʾāḇîḵā bᵉʿênêḵā šālaḥ lᵉḵā dāwid mᵉnaḥᵉmîm — “Do you think David is honoring your father in your eyes that he sent comforters to you?” The verb kābad (piel: to honor/treat as weighty) is used sarcastically. Their accusation: hălōʾ bîᵉʿăḇûr laḥqōr wᵉlahapoḵ wᵉlaraggēl — “Did they not come to spy, to overthrow, and to spy out?” The threefold verb series — ḥāqar (investigate/spy), hāpaḵ (overturn), rāgal (spy on the land) — expresses escalating hostility.
19.4: Hanun's response — wayyiqqaḥ Ḥānûn ʾet-ʿaḇdê dāwid waygallaḥ wayyikrat ʾet-madwêhem baḥᵉṣî ʿad-hašōq — he seized the men, shaved them (implying beard-removal, a severe humiliation in ANE culture), and cut their garments at the buttock. The shaving of the beard was a form of public shaming; the exposure of the lower body (šōq) added sexual humiliation. This act violated diplomatic immunity and constituted a declaration of war. The parallel in 2 Sam 10:4 is identical.
19.5: David sends word to the men: šᵉḇû ḇîrêḥô ʿad ʾăšer yiṣmaḥ zᵉqanḵem — “Remain at Jericho until your beards have grown.” The beard-growth instruction confirms the interpretation of v4's shaving as the insult. Jericho, at the Jordan's edge, is the natural first city entering Canaan from the east — the men can recuperate there without the further humiliation of appearing at the capital. David's response is measured and protective rather than immediately retaliatory.
19.6: The Ammonites recognize they have made themselves odious (nib ʾāšû — they had made themselves a stench, from bāʾaš, to stink/be abhorrent). They hire mercenaries: wayyišlᵉḥû banê ʿAmmôn wayyiśkᵉrû ʾet-ʾArām Naharayim wᵉʾet-ʾArām Maʿăḵāh wᵉʾet-Ṣôḇāh ʾalpayim ûšlōšîm rekeḇ — 32,000 chariots from Aram Naharaim, Aram Maacah, and Zobah. The figure differs from 2 Sam 10:6 (33,000 foot soldiers). The specific regions — Aram Naharaim (“Aram of the two rivers,” = upper Mesopotamia), Maacah (south of Damascus), and Zobah (central Syria) — represent the major Aramean powers of the period.
19.7: wayyiśkᵉrû ʾălêhem šᵉlōšîm ûšᵉnayim ʾelep rekeḇ — 32,000 chariots and the king of Maacah with his troops. They camp at Mêḏᵉḇāʾ (Medeba, east of the Dead Sea in Transjordan). The Ammonites themselves mustered from their cities. The geographical deployment — Aramean chariots in open country at Medeba, Ammonites holding the city entrance — establishes the tactical challenge Joab will face in v10.
19.8: David sends Joab with all the host of mighty warriors (kol-ṣᵉḇāʾ haggibbōrîm). The term gibbōrîm (mighty men, warriors) likely refers to the elite corps developed under David (cf. 2 Sam 23:8-39; 1 Chr 11:10-47). Joab's deployment with the full professional army signals David's recognition of the seriousness of the Aramean coalition.
19.9: The Ammonites position themselves petaḥ hāʿîr — at the city entrance (gateway of Medeba). The Aramean kings are positioned separately in the open field (baśśādeh). This two-front positioning — urban Ammonites + open-field Arameans — forces Joab to divide his forces. The tactical situation closely parallels the 2 Samuel 10 account.
19.10: Joab perceives (wayyarᵉ Yôʾāḇ kî-hāytāh pᵉnê hammilḥāmāh ʾēlāyw mippānîm ûmēʾāḥôr) that the battle fronts face him from front and behind. His response — dividing the force — follows classic Israelite battle doctrine when facing encirclement. He selects elite troops (mibḥar bᵉyiśrāʾēl) for the Aramean engagement and deploys his brother Abishai for the Ammonite front.
19.11: Joab places his chosen troops (mibḥar bᵉyiśrāʾēl — the choicest of Israel) against Aram and assigns the rest to Abishai his brother (ʾāḥîw). The division reflects tactical judgment: Aramean chariots in open field require the most capable unit; the Ammonites at the city gate can be held by the remainder.
19.12: Joab's tactical speech: ʾim-teḥĕzaq mimmenî ʾărām wᵉhāyîtā lî litᵉšûʿāh — “If Aram is too strong for me, you will come to my aid” — and vice versa. The mutual support arrangement is a practical contingency plan. The speech ends with the formula: ḥăzaq wᵉnitḥăzzᵉqāh bᵉʿad-ʿammênû ûbᵉʿad ʿārê ʾĕlōhênû — “Be strong (ḥāzaq, qal) and let us show ourselves strong (ḥāzaq, hithpael) for our people and for the cities of our God.”
19.13: The battle cry formula: ḥăzaq wᵉnitḥăzzᵉqāh — the qal imperative followed by the hithpael cohortative of ḥāzaq (to be/become strong). This pairing of simple command with reflexive/intensive form is used elsewhere in high-stakes leadership moments: Moses to Joshua (Deut 31:6-7), Joshua to Israel (Josh 1:6-9), David to Solomon (1 Chr 22:13). The conclusion — wᵉYHWH yaʿăśeh haṭṭôḇ bᵉʿênāyw — “YHWH will do what seems good in his eyes” — is a radical trust statement: the outcome is submitted to divine discretion after human effort is expended.
19.14: Joab draws near (wayyiqraḇ Yôʾāḇ wᵉhāʿām ʾăšer ʿimmô) to engage Aram, and they flee before him (wayyānuśû mippānāyw). The Aramean flight is emphatic — the coalition that seemed so formidable collapses at the first engagement. The same verb nûs (to flee) appears in v15 for the Ammonites when they see the Arameans fleeing.
19.15: When the Ammonites see (wayyirᵉʾû) that Aram had fled, they themselves flee before Abishai. The sequence — Arameans flee first, then Ammonites flee when they see it — reflects the psychological collapse of a coalition. The Ammonites enter the city (wayyāḇōʾû hāʿîr); Joab returns to Jerusalem. The campaign ends inconclusively for the cities — Rabbah is not taken yet (see ch20).
19.16: The Aramean defeat at Medeba triggers a wider mobilization: wayyirᵉʾû ʾărām kî nig ʾăḵû lipnê yiśrāʾēl wayyišlᵉḥû malʾāḵîm wayyôṣiʾû ʾet-ʾărām ʾăšer mēʿēḇer hannāhār — seeing they were defeated, they send for Arameans from beyond the River (Euphrates). Šôḇaḵ śar-ṣᵉḇāʾ Hadadezer — Shophach, commander of Hadadezer's army (spelled Shobach in 2 Sam 10:16). The variant spelling in Chronicles suggests an alternate tradition or textual transmission.
19.17: David is told (wayyuggad lᵉḏāwid) and he gathers all Israel, crosses the Jordan (wayyaʿăḇor hayYardēn), and comes upon them (wayyāḇōʾ ʾălêhem). The Jordan crossing signals a major campaign — not a border skirmish but an expeditionary force into Transjordan. David positions (wayyaʿărok ʾălêhem) for battle; the Arameans take up formation against Israel.
19.18: The Arameans flee before Israel: wayyānos ʾărām mippᵉnê yiśrāʾēl wayyahrog dāwid mēʾărām šiḇʿat ʾălāpîm rekeḇ — David kills 7,000 of Aram's charioteers (rekeḇ, chariot-riders) and 40,000 foot soldiers. The 2 Sam 10:18 parallel says “700 charioteers” — the Chronicler's tenfold higher figure may reflect different counting conventions or textual transmission. Shophach the commander is also killed.
19.19: The vassal Aramean kings who had served Hadadezer make peace with David: wayyirᵉʾû ʿaḇdê Hadadezer kî nugᵉpû lipnê yiśrāʾēl wayyašlimû ʿim dāwid — “the servants of Hadadezer saw they were struck down before Israel and made peace (šālam hiphil) with David.” The hiphil of šālam (to be whole, complete, at peace) here means to make a treaty of submission. The result: wᵉʾet-ʾAmmon lōʾ ʾāḇāh ʿāzōr — the Arameans would no longer help the Ammonites, leaving them isolated for the final campaign of ch20.

20.1: wayhî lᵉʿêt tᵉšûḇat haššānāh lᵉʿêt ṣēʾt hammᵉlāḵîm — “at the time of the return of the year, the time when kings go out.” This formula (identical to 2 Sam 11:1) marks the spring campaign season when armies mustered after winter. The phrase tᵉšûḇat haššānāh (turn/return of the year) refers to the agricultural new year's onset after the spring rains. The Chronicler reproduces this verse from Samuel but omits everything that follows it in Samuel — the entire Bathsheba/Uriah narrative (2 Sam 11:2–12:25) — moving directly from Joab's siege to the final capture of Rabbah. This omission is the Chronicler's most consequential redaction: David's great moral failure simply does not appear in Chronicles.
20.2: David takes the crown (keter) from the head of Milcom (malkām). The noun malkām is ambiguous: it can mean “their king” (from melek + 3mp suffix) or it can be the name of the Ammonite deity Milcom/Molech. Many translators read “their king,” but the Septuagint and Vulgate read “Milcom” (the god), and the crown's weight — ûmišqālô kikkār zāhāḇ, a talent of gold (about 75 lbs) — is appropriate for a divine statue's crown rather than a human king's. David also takes the valuable items (šālāl) of the city. The crown placed on David's head (wayyᵉhî ʿal rōʾš dāwid) symbolizes the defeat of Ammon's patron deity and David's sovereign inheritance.
20.3: The treatment of the Ammonites: wᵉʾet-hāʿām ʾăšer bāh hôṣîʾ wayyāśar bamagēr ûbamaḥărēšôt habarzel ûbammagrēzôt — he brought out the people that were in it and put them to work with saws and with iron picks and with axes (magrēzôt). The 2 Sam 12:31 parallel has been much debated — whether the verbs mean “he put them to saws” (execution by sawing) or “he made them work with saws” (forced labor in stonecutting). The Chronicler's wording, with the hiphil yaʿăḇor (“passed/set them to”), is most naturally read as labor conscription for building projects, consistent with Solomon's later corvée practices.
20.4: The first of three Philistine giant encounters that form the chapter's second section (vv4-8): wayhî ʾaḥărê-ḵên wattaqom milḥāmāh bᵉGezer — thereafter battle arose at Gezer. Sibbᵉḵay haḥûšātî hikkāh ʾet-Sippay min-yaldê hārāpāʾ — Sibbecai the Hushathite struck down Sippai (spelled Ishbi-benob in 2 Sam 21:16). The formula min-yaldê hārāpāʾ — “from the offspring of Rapha” — marks these as descendants of the Rephaim (pre-Israelite giants, cf. Deut 2:11, 20; 3:11). Rāpāʾ or hārāpāʾ is either a personal name (an ancestor of Philistine giants) or a class noun (the Rapha, the giants).
20.5: The second encounter: wattehî-ʿôd milḥāmāh ʾet-Pᵉlištîm wayyak Elḥānān ben-Yāʿîr ʾet-Laḥmî ʾăḥî Golyāt haGittî — Elhanan son of Jair killed Lahmi the brother of Goliath the Gittite. The significant phrase is ʾăḥî Golyāt — the brother of Goliath — which resolves an apparent conflict with 2 Sam 21:19, where the parallel verse reads that Elhanan “killed Goliath the Gittite.” Chronicles clarifies: it was Goliath's brother, not Goliath himself (already killed by David in 1 Sam 17). The Chronicler's text either represents an independent tradition or is a theologically-motivated clarification preserving David's unique role as Goliath's killer.
20.6: The third encounter: wattehî-ʿôd milḥāmāh bᵉGat wayhî ʾîš middāh wᵉʾeṣbᵉʿōtāyw šēš-wāšēš ʿeśrîm wᵉʾarbaʿ — and there was again war at Gath, where there was a man of great stature, with six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot, twenty-four in all. The formula ûhûʾ yulad lᵉhārāpāʾ — “and he also was born to the Rapha” — connects him to the giant lineage. Jonathan son of Shimea (David's brother) killed him. The six-fingered, six-toed condition (polydactyly) may have been regarded as a mark of the Rephaim.
20.7: wayyᵉḥārēp ʾet-yiśrāʾēl wayyakkēhû Yônātān ben-Šimᵉʾā ʾăḥî dāwid — the giant taunted (ḥārap, piel: to reproach, taunt, defy) Israel, and Jonathan son of Shimea, David's brother, struck him down. The verb ḥārap (to taunt/defy) was used of Goliath defying Israel's ranks (1 Sam 17:10, 25, 26, 36, 45) — a deliberate thematic echo. The name Jonathan (= yᵉhônātān, “YHWH has given”) is the same as David's covenant friend, though a different individual.
20.8: Summary formula: ʾēlleh yullᵉdû lᵉhārāpāʾ bᵉGat wayyippᵉlû bᵉyad dāwid ûbᵉyad ʿăḇādāyw — “these were born to Rapha in Gath and they fell by the hand of David and by the hand of his servants.” The formula nāpal bᵉyad (to fall into/by the hand of) denotes defeat in battle. The fourfold giant-killing sequence — Goliath (1 Sam 17), then three more in this chapter — establishes that the elimination of the giant threat to Israel was accomplished through David's reign and legacy. For the Chronicler, this is another necessary condition for the temple: the land secured, the giants eliminated.

26.1: The chapter addresses the šōʿᵉrîm — gatekeepers. The institutional organization of gatekeeping is central to the Chronicler's portrait of David's preparations. lᵉmaḥlᵉqôt haššōʿᵉrîm — “for the divisions of the gatekeepers.” The root šāʿar (gate) forms the denominative participle: those assigned to the gates. Meshelemiah (= Shelemiah) son of Kore belongs to the sons of Asaph (though textually Asaph here may mean a Korahite clan; the LXX differs). The Korahites (mibᵉnê-Qōraḥ) trace to Korah, whose rebellion in Num 16 cost him his life but whose lineage continued (Num 26:11) and produced Psalms 42-49.
26.2: Meshelemiah's sons: Zechariah the firstborn (bᵉkôr), followed by six others. The listing by birth order signals legitimacy claims and inheritance rights for gatekeeper positions. bᵉkôr (firstborn) carries both status and responsibility in Israelite social organization — the firstborn gatekeeper presumably holds authority over the post.
26.3: The seven sons of Meshelemiah named (vv2-3). The number seven for the firstborn gatekeeper's sons parallels the fullness of provision: sufficient personnel to staff the gate rotation. The names are formulaic: Elam, Jehohanan, Elioenai, Jaaziah, Elihoenai, Zechariah, Hashbaddanah appear in variant forms in Ezra-Nehemiah contexts, indicating the gatekeeper families persisted into the post-exilic period.
26.4: Obed-edom's sons: the Obed-edom who served as host to the ark (1 Chr 13:14, 15:18-24) is now listed as the father of eight sons who become gatekeepers. ûḇānîm ṭôḇāh ʾĕlōhîm lô — the narrative aside, “God blessed him,” from 13:14, explains the numerous progeny. The eight sons named in vv4-7 (Shemaiah, Jehozabad, Joah, Sachar, Nethanel, Ammiel, Issachar, Peullethai) follow by name, establishing the legitimacy of the Obed-edom gatekeeper clan.
26.5: The second son Jehozabad is followed by Joah. kî bēraḵô ʾĕlōhîm — the divine blessing on Obed-edom referenced here echoes the explicit statement in 13:14 that YHWH blessed Obed-edom and all his household during the ark's stay. The gatekeeper appointment thus carries the narrative memory of the ark's transit: the family that hosted the ark now guards the temple's gates.
26.6: Shemaiah the son of Obed-edom (ûlišᵉmayāh bᵉnô) himself has sons listed: rulers (môšᵉlîm) in their father's house, for they were mighty men (gibbōrê ḥayil). The phrase gibbōrê ḥayil (warriors of valor/wealth) applied to gatekeepers is notable — gatekeeper duty in the ancient world required martial capability; the gates controlled access to the sacred space and required defense. The sons listed are described as having rule over their family clan.
26.7: The sons of Shemaiah: Othni, Rephael, Obed, Elzabad — and then specifically wᵉ ʾeḥāyw Elihu and Semachiah, who are called bᵉnê ḥayil (sons of valor). The distinction between those simply named and those additionally called bᵉnê ḥayil may indicate the latter held special military-gatekeeping functions. The total gatekeeper force from Obed-edom's line is given as 62 in v8.
26.8: Summary: kol-ʾēlleh mibᵉnê ʾôḇēd ʾĕdôm hēmmāh ûḇᵉnêhem wᵉʾaḥêhem ʾîš-ḥayil baḵōaḥ lāʿăḇōdāh šᵉnayim wᵉšišîm lᵉʾôḇēd ʾĕdôm — 62 men from Obed-edom's line, all men of ability (ʾîš-ḥayil) for the service. The 62 represents one of the larger clan-service numbers in this section. ḥayil in the context of temple service carries both the military connotation (for gate defense) and general capability.
26.9: Meshelemiah's sons and brothers — 18 strong men (šᵉmōnāh ʿāśār) capable men (bᵉnê ḥayil). The relative sizes of the three gatekeeper families — Meshelemiah 18, Obed-edom 62, Hosah 13 (v11) — reflect actual family sizes rather than an idealized system. The Chronicler preserves authentic personnel records, now organized liturgically.
26.10: Hosah of the Merarites has sons: his chief (hārōʾš) is Shimri. The note is unusual: kî lōʾ hāyāh bᵉkôr wᵉyaśîmēhû ʾāḇîw lᵉrōʾš — Shimri was not the firstborn, yet his father appointed him as chief. The firstborn convention for succession is here overridden by paternal appointment — a legitimating explanation for why the non-firstborn holds the chief position. This administrative note shows the Chronicler's concern for proper institutional authority.
26.11: Hosah's other sons: Hilkiah, Tebaliah, Zechariah — total 13 sons and brothers of Hosah (kol-bᵉnê-ḥôsāh ûʾaḥêhem šᵉlōšāh ʿāśār). The combined gatekeeper force from the three families totals 18 + 62 + 13 = 93 men, with additional clan members implied. This was a substantial institutional presence in the temple complex.
26.12: The gate assignments are made by lots (gôrālôt): lēʾēlleh maḥlᵉqôt haššōʿᵉrîm — these are the divisions of the gatekeepers. lāraʾš laggᵉḇer kaʾăḥîw — for the leaders, the man like his brothers (implying equality of status regardless of family size). The lot system prevents nepotism and attributes the gate assignment to divine providence rather than human preference. Each gate assignment is thereby understood as YHWH's appointment.
26.13: The lots are cast for each family unit (lᵉḇêt ʾăḇôtām, for their father's house) — both small and great family alike: laqqāṭōn kaggādôl. This democratizing principle — the small family has equal standing in the lot as the large — reflects the theological understanding that temple service is not distributed by human power but by divine ordering.
26.14: The lot fell (wayyippol haggôrāl) for the east gate to Shelemiah. His son Zechariah gets the north gate — described as a man of śeḵel (understanding/wisdom), who advises wisely (yôʿēṣ bᵉśeḵel). The east gate was the primary processional entrance facing the sunrise; its assignment to the Shelemiah/Meshelemiah family is prestigious. The north gate's assignment to Zechariah, characterized specifically for wisdom, suggests a deliberate match of personnel to station.
26.15: Obed-edom's lot fell to the south gate (haššāʾûl laNegeḇ). His sons are assigned to the storehouse (lāʾasuppîm). The term ʾăsuppîm (store chambers, collecting points) appears in Neh 12:25 and refers to storage facilities adjacent to the gate. The south gate and storehouse assignment gives the Obed-edom clan responsibility for both entry control and material administration.
26.16: Shuppim and Hosah have the west gate and the Shallecheth gate (šaʿar haššalleket) — a gate on the ascending causeway (bamsillāh hāʿôlāh). This may be the gate through which waste and refuse were removed from the temple courts. Gatekeepers stand watch (šōmᵉrîm) at corresponding stations: guard beside guard (šōmēr ʾeṣel šōmēr).
26.17: The daily rotation: eastward, six Levites; northward, four per day; southward, four per day; the storehouse, two and two (šᵉnayim šᵉnayim). The numbers reflect the size and traffic of each gate. The east gate's six-person assignment (double others) befits its status as the primary processional entrance. The paired assignment at the storehouses indicates coordinated oversight of material goods.
26.18: For the Parbar (western court precinct, lapparbār) — four on the roadway and two at the Parbar itself. The term parbār is a hapax legomenon in 1 Chr 26:18 (and 2 Kgs 23:11), likely an architectural term for a western colonnaded court or suburb. Its exact meaning is uncertain; ancient versions (LXX pharbara, Vulgate pharbar) transliterate rather than translate.
26.19: Summary: ʾēlleh maḥlᵉqôt haššōʿᵉrîm libᵉnê haQōrāḥî ûlibᵉnê Merārî — these are the divisions of the gatekeepers belonging to the sons of Korah and the sons of Merari. The two levitical lineages — Kohathite (Korahites within Kohath) and Merarite — together staff the entire gate system. The Gershonites, the third major Levite division, are not assigned gate duty here but appear elsewhere in liturgical service.
26.20: The Levites who oversee the treasuries: wᵉʾăḥêhem halwîm ʿal ʾôṣᵉrôt bêt hāʾĕlōhîm wᵉʿal ʾôṣᵉrôt haqqᵉdāšîm — “and their Levite brothers over the treasuries of the house of God and over the treasuries of the dedicated things.” Two distinct treasuries: (1) ʾôṣᵉrôt bêt hāʾĕlōhîm — the operating treasury of the temple itself; (2) ʾôṣᵉrôt haqqᵉdāšîm — treasury of holy gifts, i.e., dedicated/consecrated donations. The distinction parallels later rabbinic categories of temple funding.
26.21: The sons of Laadan, who was a Gershonite: the family heads of Laadan the Gershonite are the Jehielites (yᵉḥîʾēlî). Jehiel the son of Laadan (or the Jehielite clan descended from him) leads the Gershonite treasury group. The Gershonites' assignment to treasury oversight rather than gate service reflects the functional differentiation of Levite clans in Chronicles' idealized organization.
26.22: The sons of Jehiel: Zetham and Joel his brother — ʿal ʾôṣᵉrôt bêt YHWH — over the treasuries of the house of YHWH. The naming of specific individuals responsible for specific treasuries reflects the Chronicler's interest in institutional accountability. Named stewards are identifiable and answerable for their charge.
26.23: The Amramites, Izharites, Hebronites, and Uzzielites (the four Kohathite families, from Kohath's four sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, Uzziel, cf. 1 Chr 23:12). These four families together provide the treasury oversight personnel for the inner sanctum functions. The Amramites are especially significant — Moses, Aaron, and Miriam were all Amramites, making this clan the priestly and prophetic lineage.
26.24: wišᵉḇûʾēl ben-Gēršôm ben-Mōšeh nāgîd ʿal-hāʾôṣārot — Shebuel son of Gershom son of Moses, ruler over the treasuries. The identification of this treasury overseer as the son of Gershom son of Moses is remarkable: a direct grandson of Moses holds institutional authority in David's temple-preparation administration. Nāgîd (ruler, designated leader) is the title used for the king-designate (1 Sam 9:16) and here for the chief treasury officer — a high-status administrative title.
26.25: The Eliezer line — Rehabiah, Jeshaiah, Joram, Zichri, Shelomoth. Shelomoth (Šᵉlōmōt) and his brothers are assigned over all the dedicated treasuries that David the king, the family heads, the commanders of thousands and hundreds, and the commanders of the army had dedicated. The list traces the genealogy from Eliezer (Moses' second son, Exod 18:4) through five generations to Shelomoth, establishing the legitimacy of this Mosaic lineage for treasury oversight.
26.26: ʾăšer hiqdîš dāwid haméleḵ ûrāʾšê hāʾāḇôt — what David the king and the family heads had dedicated. The dedicated gifts include booty from wars (mimmilḥāmôt ûmin-haššālāl) — spoils taken from Edom, Moab, Ammon, the Philistines, and Amalek. War spoils dedicated to the temple rather than kept personally demonstrate the sacred purpose of David's campaigns: they were fought not for personal enrichment but for the provision of the temple that David could not himself build.
26.27: kî mēhaqqᵉrāḇôt ûmin-haššālāl hiqdîšû lᵉḥazzēq lᵉḇêt YHWH — “for from the battles and from the spoil they had dedicated [things] to strengthen (ḥāzaq piel) the house of YHWH.” The verb ḥāzaq (piel: to strengthen, repair, maintain) is the standard term for temple maintenance provision (cf. 2 Kgs 12:5-15; 2 Chr 24:5-13). The dedicated spoils were specifically earmarked for temple upkeep, not general revenue.
26.28: And all that Samuel the seer (hārōʾeh), Saul son of Kish, Abner son of Ner, and Joab son of Zeruiah had dedicated — everything dedicated was under the care of Shelomoth and his brothers. The inventory of dedicators spans Israel's entire pre-Solomonic history: Samuel (the founding prophet-judge), Saul (the first king), Abner (Saul's general), and Joab (David's general). The convergence of dedicated gifts from across political eras indicates the temple treasury as a national repository transcending individual reigns.
26.29: The Izharites (haYiṣhārî): Chenaniah and his sons are assigned to external duties (lamlāʾḵāh haḥîṣônāh) — judicial-administrative work beyond the temple. lᵉšōṭᵉrîm ûlᵉšōpᵉṭîm ʿal yiśrāʾēl — as officials (šōṭᵉrîm, enforcement officers) and judges (šōpᵉṭîm) over Israel. The extension of Levitical function into civil judiciary echoes Moses' arrangement in Deut 16:18 and the Jehoshaphat reforms in 2 Chr 19:8-11.
26.30: The Hebronites: Hashabiah and his brothers — 1,700 capable men (gibbôrê ḥayil) — are assigned over Israel west of the Jordan (maʿăḇar haYardēn maʿᵉrābāh) for all YHWH's work and the king's service. The dual mandate — kol-mᵉlaʾḵet YHWH wᵉʿăḇōdat hamméleḵ — reflects the Chronicler's understanding that civil and sacred administration are intertwined under the Davidic covenant.
26.31: The Hebronites' chief was Jerijah (Yᵉriyyāhû). In the fortieth year of David's reign (bišnat hāʾarbāʿîm lᵉmalkût dāwid), a search was made among them and mighty men were found at Jazer of Gilead. The datum “fortieth year” anchors this administrative reorganization to the end of David's reign — the final preparation before Solomon's accession. Jazer is a Transjordanian city assigned to the Gadites (Num 32:35).
26.32: Jerijah had 2,700 capable men (gibbôrê ḥayil) — family heads — whom David the king assigned over the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh for all matters of God and matters of the king. The number 2,700 for Transjordan (vs. 1,700 for Cisjordan in v30) may reflect the more dispersed and exposed nature of the Transjordanian territories requiring larger administrative presence. This final verse closes the gatekeeper-treasury-official section by establishing Levitical oversight over all twelve-tribe territory — a pan-Israelite administrative completeness that anticipates the fully-assembled Israel of Solomon's reign.
